blocks:
Ayahs

أَتَىٰٓ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ فَلَا تَسْتَعْجِلُوهُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (١)
Allah’s edict is coming! So do not seek to hasten it. Immaculate is He and exalted above [having] any partners that they ascribe [to Him]. (1)
يُنَزِّلُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ بِٱلرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِۦ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦٓ أَنْ أَنذِرُوٓا۟ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنَا۠ فَٱتَّقُونِ (٢)
He sends down the angels with the spirit of His command to whomever He wishes of His servants: ‘Warn [the people] that there is no god except Me; so be wary of Me.’ (2)
خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ تَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٣)
He created the heavens and the earth with consummate wisdom. He is above having any partners that they ascribe [to Him]. (3)
خَلَقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ فَإِذَا هُوَ خَصِيمٌ مُّبِينٌ (٤)
He created man from a drop of [seminal] fluid, and, behold, he is an open contender! (4)
وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمَ خَلَقَهَا ۗ لَكُمْ فِيهَا دِفْءٌ وَمَنَـٰفِعُ وَمِنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ (٥)
He created the cattle, in which there is warmth for you and [other] uses, and some of them you eat. (5)
وَلَكُمْ فِيهَا جَمَالٌ حِينَ تُرِيحُونَ وَحِينَ تَسْرَحُونَ (٦)
There is in them a beauty for you when you bring them home for rest and when you drive them forth to pasture. (6)
وَتَحْمِلُ أَثْقَالَكُمْ إِلَىٰ بَلَدٍ لَّمْ تَكُونُوا۟ بَـٰلِغِيهِ إِلَّا بِشِقِّ ٱلْأَنفُسِ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكُمْ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٧)
And they bear your burdens to towns, which you could not reach except by straining yourselves. Indeed your Lord is most kind and merciful. (7)
وَٱلْخَيْلَ وَٱلْبِغَالَ وَٱلْحَمِيرَ لِتَرْكَبُوهَا وَزِينَةً ۚ وَيَخْلُقُ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٨)
And horses, mules and asses, for you to ride them, and for pomp, and He creates what you do not know. (8)
وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ قَصْدُ ٱلسَّبِيلِ وَمِنْهَا جَآئِرٌ ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ لَهَدَىٰكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٩)
With Allah rests guidance to the straight path, and some of the paths are devious, and had He wished He would have guided you all. (9)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً ۖ لَّكُم مِّنْهُ شَرَابٌ وَمِنْهُ شَجَرٌ فِيهِ تُسِيمُونَ (١٠)
It is He who sends down water from the sky: from it you get your drink and with it are [sustained] the plants wherein you pasture your herds. (10)
يُنۢبِتُ لَكُم بِهِ ٱلزَّرْعَ وَٱلزَّيْتُونَ وَٱلنَّخِيلَ وَٱلْأَعْنَـٰبَ وَمِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (١١)
For you He makes the crops grow with it and olives, date palms, vines, and fruits of all kinds. There is indeed a sign in that for a people who reflect. (11)
وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ ۖ وَٱلنُّجُومُ مُسَخَّرَٰتٌۢ بِأَمْرِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (١٢)
He disposed the night and the day for you, and the sun, the moon and the stars are disposed by His command. There are indeed signs in that for people who exercise their reason. (12)
وَمَا ذَرَأَ لَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُخْتَلِفًا أَلْوَٰنُهُۥٓ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَذَّكَّرُونَ (١٣)
And [He disposed for your benefit] whatever He has created for you in the earth of diverse hues—there is indeed a sign in that for a people who take admonition. (13)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى سَخَّرَ ٱلْبَحْرَ لِتَأْكُلُوا۟ مِنْهُ لَحْمًا طَرِيًّا وَتَسْتَخْرِجُوا۟ مِنْهُ حِلْيَةً تَلْبَسُونَهَا وَتَرَى ٱلْفُلْكَ مَوَاخِرَ فِيهِ وَلِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (١٤)
It is He who disposed the sea [for your benefit] that you may eat from it fresh meat, and obtain from it ornaments which you wear, and you see the ships plowing through it, that you may seek His bounty and that you may give thanks. (14)
وَأَلْقَىٰ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ رَوَٰسِىَ أَن تَمِيدَ بِكُمْ وَأَنْهَـٰرًا وَسُبُلًا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ (١٥)
He cast firm mountains in the earth lest it should shake with you, and [made] streams and ways, so that you may be guided (15)
وَعَلَـٰمَـٰتٍ ۚ وَبِٱلنَّجْمِ هُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ (١٦)
—and the landmarks [as well]—and by the stars they are guided. (16)
أَفَمَن يَخْلُقُ كَمَن لَّا يَخْلُقُ ۗ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ (١٧)
Is He who creates like one who does not create? Will you not then take admonition? (17)
وَإِن تَعُدُّوا۟ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ لَا تُحْصُوهَآ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٨)
If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful, (18)
وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا تُسِرُّونَ وَمَا تُعْلِنُونَ (١٩)
and Allah knows whatever you hide and whatever you disclose. (19)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ لَا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ (٢٠)
Those whom they invoke besides Allah do not create anything and are themselves created. (20)
أَمْوَٰتٌ غَيْرُ أَحْيَآءٍ ۖ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ أَيَّانَ يُبْعَثُونَ (٢١)
They are dead and lifeless, and are not aware when they will be resurrected. (21)

(Commentary)

It is most probable—upon contemplating the surah—that the opening section was revealed during the latter part of the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) period in Mecca, shortly before the Migration (الهجرة). It consists of forty verses in which Allah, the Glorified, mentions in one part the various types of His celestial and terrestrial blessings (النعم) that sustain human life and benefit man in his livelihood—a perfected system and continuous governance (تدبير) that points to His, the Exalted, oneness in His Lordship (الربوبية).

In another part, He argues against the invalidity of the polytheists' claims and the futility of their efforts, stating that He will requite them just as He requited similar nations of the past, and that He will issue the final judgment between them on the Day of Resurrection.

The Glorified opened these verses with His saying: أَتى أَمْرُ اللهِ فَلا تَسْتَعْجِلُوهُ سُبْحانَهُ وَتَعالى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ — "The edict of Allah has come! So do not seek to hasten it. Immaculate is He and exalted above [having] any partners that they ascribe [to Him]." (16:1). He branched the verses of argumentation upon the glorification (التنزيه) and hallowing (التسبيح) contained therein. From this, it is known that the primary purpose of the opening of the surah is to announce the imminence of the Divine command, its closeness to them, and the proximity of its descent upon them. In this, there is a rebuff to the polytheists, for they used to hasten the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—out of mockery—whenever they heard the speech of Allah, the Glorified, frequently mentioning the descent of His command and warning them of it. This includes His saying to the believers: فَاعْفُوا وَاصْفَحُوا حَتَّى يَأْتِيَ اللهُ بِأَمْرِهِ — "Yet excuse them and forbear until Allah issues His edict." (2:109). This is nothing other than His command for the triumph of truth over falsehood, monotheism (التوحيد) over polytheism (الشرك), and faith (الإيمان) over disbelief (الكفر). This is what contemplation of the opening of the surah yields.

As for its conclusion—which consists of eighty-eight verses from His saying: وَالَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا فِي اللهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما ظُلِمُوا — "Those who migrate for the sake of Allah after they have been wronged..." (16:41) until the end of the surah—despite the connection and correlation between them, the context (سياق) of the verses suggests they were revealed during the early period of the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) time in Medina, shortly after the Migration. Thus, the beginning and the end of the surah are close in their time of revelation. This is because they contain verses whose contents only apply to certain events that occurred shortly after the Migration, such as His saying: وَالَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا فِي اللهِ (the verse), and His saying: وَلَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّما يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ — "We certainly know that they say, ‘It is only a human that teaches him.’" (16:103), which was revealed, according to one opinion, regarding Salman al-Farsi who believed in Medina; and His saying: مَنْ كَفَرَ بِاللهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ إِيمانِهِ إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ — "Whoever renounces faith in Allah after [affirming] his faith—barring someone who is compelled..." (16:106), which was revealed regarding Ammar—as will follow—as well as the verses revealed concerning the Jews and the verses regarding legal rulings (الأحكام). All of this supports the probability of these verses being Medinan (مدنية).

Nevertheless, the variation in revelation is apparent in some of them, such as His saying: وَالَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا etc. (verse 41), and His saying: وَإِذا بَدَّلْنا آيَةً مَكانَ آيَةٍ — "When We replace a sign with another sign..." (16:101) through the end of two or five verses, and His saying: مَنْ كَفَرَ بِاللهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ إِيمانِهِ (verse 106) and several verses following it.

Fairness—after all this—dictates that His saying: وَالَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا (verse 41) through the end of two verses, and His saying: مَنْ كَفَرَ بِاللهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ إِيمانِهِ (verse 106) and a few verses after it, and His saying: وَإِنْ عاقَبْتُمْ فَعاقِبُوا — "If you decide to punish, then punish..." (16:126) and two verses after it, are Medinan, as the context testifies to that. The rest are more similar to Meccan (مكية) verses than Medinan ones. Although this does not align with all transmitted reports, the context bears witness to it, and it is more worthy of being followed. It was previously mentioned in the commentary on verse 118 of Surat al-An'am the possibility that it was revealed after Surat al-Nahl, which is Meccan. The purpose that serves as a unifying theme for the verses at the end of the surah is that they contain a command for patience (الصبر) and a beautiful promise for patience for the sake of Allah.

The overall purpose of the surah is to announce the imminence of Allah's command, which is the manifestation of the true religion (الدين الحق) over them. The Exalted clarifies this by explaining that Allah is the only God (الإله) worthy of worship (المعبود) and none other, because the governance (تدبير) of the world is sustained by Him, just as creation (الخلقة) is sustained by Him, and because all blessings (النعم) terminate in Him, while this is negated for any other. Therefore, it is obligatory that Allah alone be worshipped and none other. It also clarifies that the true religion belongs to Allah, so it must be adhered to, and no religion should be legislated besides it. It refutes the doubts raised by the polytheists regarding prophethood (النبوة) and legislation (التشريع), and explains matters of the Divine religion.

This is what the majority of the verses in the surah aim for, returning to its explanation time and again, and within them are verses addressing the matter of the Migration and related subjects.

His saying, the Exalted: أَتى أَمْرُ اللهِ فَلا تَسْتَعْجِلُوهُ سُبْحانَهُ وَتَعالى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ — The apparent context is that the address is to the polytheists, because the subsequent verses are directed as an argument against them, up to His saying in the twenty-second verse: إِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ — "Your God is the One God." (16:22). The address therein is directed to the polytheists, and they are all, as it were, branched out from His saying at the end of this verse: سُبْحانَهُ وَتَعالى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ. This necessitates that the "command" (الأمر) whose arrival is announced is a command that purifies the realm of Lordship from their polytheism by uprooting its source. There is no mention in His speech of the believers seeking to hasten any matter; rather, what is mentioned is the polytheists seeking to hasten what was mentioned in His, the Exalted, speech regarding the Hour (الساعة), the victory (الفتح), and the descent of punishment. This is indicated by His saying: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَتاكُمْ عَذابُهُ بَياتاً أَوْ نَهاراً ما ذا يَسْتَعْجِلُ مِنْهُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ — "Say, ‘Tell me, should His punishment overtake you by night or day, what part of it will the guilty seek to hasten?’" (10:50) until His saying: وَيَسْتَنْبِئُونَكَ أَحَقٌّ هُوَ قُلْ إِي وَرَبِّي إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ وَما أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ — "They ask you, ‘Is it true?’ Say, ‘Yes! By my Lord, it is indeed true, and you cannot frustrate [Him].’" (10:53), among other verses.

Accordingly, what is meant by the "command" (الأمر) is what Allah promised the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and those who believed, and what He threatened the polytheists with time and again in His speech: that He will surely aid the believers, disgrace the disbelievers, punish them, and make His religion manifest by a command from Him, as He said: فَاعْفُوا وَاصْفَحُوا حَتَّى يَأْتِيَ اللهُ بِأَمْرِهِ (2:109). To this also refers the pronoun in فَلا تَسْتَعْجِلُوهُ (so do not seek to hasten it) based on what the context provides. Alternatively, the "arrival of the command" (إتيان الأمر) could mean its imminence of realization and its proximity to manifestation. This is common in speech; it is said to one waiting for the arrival of the prince: "Here is the prince, he has come," meaning his arrival is near, though he has not yet arrived.

Based on this as well, His saying: سُبْحانَهُ وَتَعالى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ is a form of transition (التفات) from direct address to the third person, indicating that they ought to be turned away from being addressed directly due to the degradation of their intellects because of their polytheism; they only sought to hasten the descent of the command out of polytheism, mockery, and derision.

By what has been mentioned, the view of some that the address in the verse is to the believers, or to both the believers and polytheists together, is refuted, for the context does not suit it. Furthermore, the Exalted, in His speech, restricts "hastening" (الاستعجال) to those other than the believers and negates it from them, saying: يَسْتَعْجِلُ بِهَا الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِها وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مُشْفِقُونَ مِنْها وَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهَا الْحَقُّ — "Those who do not believe in it seek to hasten it, but those who have faith are apprehensive of it and know that it is the truth." (42:18).

Similarly, their claim that the "command" refers to the Day of Resurrection is refuted. Although the polytheists did seek to hasten it, as indicated by their saying as recounted by Allah: مَتى هذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ — "They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’" (36:48), the context of the verses does not support this, as you have seen.

Strange is the argument used by a group of them that the "command" refers to the Day of Resurrection because the Exalted said at the end of Surat al-Hijr: فَوَ رَبِّكَ لَنَسْئَلَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — "By your Lord, We will question them all," (15:92), which was a reminder of their gathering and questioning; thus He said at the opening of this surah: أَتى أَمْرُ اللهِ, announcing the proximity of the Day of Resurrection. Likewise, His saying at the end of al-Hijr: وَاعْبُدْ رَبَّكَ حَتَّى يَأْتِيَكَ الْيَقِينُ — "And worship your Lord until the Certainty comes to you," (15:99), which is interpreted as death, is highly appropriate for the "command" in this surah to be the Day of Resurrection. What reinforces this appropriateness is His saying there: يَأْتِيَكَ (comes to you) and here: أَتى (has come). Such fabricated sayings are among those that should not be given attention.

Similar to this is the saying of some that the "command" (الأمر) is the singular of "commands" (الأوامر), meaning the ruling (الحكم), as if He is referring to the rulings in the surah regarding covenants, oaths, prohibited foods, and others, and the address on this basis would be to the believers specifically—and this is as you see [untenable].

His saying, the Exalted: يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلى مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ — "He sends down the angels with the Spirit [at His] behest upon whomever He wishes of His servants..." (16:2) until the end of the verse. People, despite their intense disagreement, ancient and modern, regarding the reality of the Spirit (الروح), do not differ in that they understand from it a single meaning: that by which there is life (الحياة), which is the criterion for consciousness (الشعور) and will (الإرادة). This meaning is what is intended in the noble verse.

As for its reality in general, what is provided by verses such as His saying: يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الرُّوحُ وَالْمَلائِكَةُ صَفًّا — "On the day when the Spirit and the angels stand in ranks..." (78:38) and His saying: تَعْرُجُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَالرُّوحُ إِلَيْهِ — "The angels and the Spirit ascend to Him..." (70:4) and others, is that it is an independent existent (موجود مستقل) possessing life, knowledge, and power, and is not of the nature of attributes (الصفات) or states (الأحوال) subsisting in things, as might be imagined. He has informed us by His saying: قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي — "Say, ‘The Spirit is of the behest of my Lord.’" (17:85) that it is of the genus of His command (أمره). He also defined His command in verses like: إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ فَسُبْحانَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it, ‘Be,’ and it is. So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things..." (36:82-83). This indicates that it is the word of origination (كلمة الإيجاد) by which the Glorified brings things into being—that is, the existence (الوجود) that He bestows upon them, but not from every aspect, rather from the aspect of its attribution to Him, the Exalted, without matter (مادة), time (زمان), or place (مكان), as provided by His saying: وَما أَمْرُنا إِلَّا واحِدَةٌ كَلَمْحٍ بِالْبَصَرِ — "Our command is but a single [word], like the twinkling of an eye." (54:50). This expression is only used for that which has no gradualness (تدريج)—that is, it has no matter or motion. Let this summary remain with you until its detailed explanation comes to you, Allah willing, in the commentary on Surat al-Isra.

In conclusion, the Spirit (الروح) is the word of life (كلمة الحياة) that Allah, the Glorified, casts into things, thereby giving them life by His will. For this reason, He called it revelation (وحيا) and considered its casting and sending down upon His Prophet as "revealing" (إيحاء) in His saying: وَكَذلِكَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ رُوحاً مِنْ أَمْرِنا — "Thus have We revealed to you a Spirit of Our behest." (42:52). Since revelation (الوحي) is hidden speech and making one understand by way of sign and gesture, the casting of His word—the word of life—into the heart of the Prophet (عليه‌السلام) is a revelation of the Spirit to him. Understand this.

Thus, His saying: يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ, the [preposition] ba (بـ) is for accompaniment (المصاحبة) or causality (السببية), and there is no great difference between them in the final outcome, as is apparent to the contemplative. For the sending down of the angels accompanied by the Spirit is only to cast it into the spirit of the Prophet (عليه‌السلام) to bestow upon him Divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية). Likewise, their sending down because of the Spirit is because His word—meaning the word of life—governs the angels and gives them life just as it governs man and gives him life. The pronoun in يُنَزِّلُ (He sends down) refers to Him, the Exalted, and the sentence is an inception (استئناف) providing the reason for His saying in the previous verse: سُبْحانَهُ وَتَعالى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ.

The meaning is: Allah is immaculate and exalted above their polytheism or above the partner they claim for Him; and because of His being immaculate and exalted above a partner, the Glorified sends down the angels accompanied by the Spirit—which is of the genus of His command and His word in origination—or because of it, upon whomever He wishes of His servants, [saying]: "Warn that there is no god except Me; so be God-conscious of Me."

Some have mentioned that what is meant by the Spirit is the revelation (الوحي) or the Quran, and it is called "spirit" because the life of hearts is through it, just as the actual spirit is that by which the life of bodies exists. He said: and His saying: مِنْ أَمْرِهِ means "by His command" (بأمره), similar to His saying: يَحْفَظُونَهُ مِنْ أَمْرِ اللهِ — "guarding him by Allah’s command" (13:11), because no one guards him from His command. [End of quote].

As for his saying that min (مِنْ) in His saying مِنْ أَمْرِهِ is in the sense of the ba (بـ) based on His saying: يَحْفَظُونَهُ مِنْ أَمْرِ اللهِ, it was already mentioned in the commentary on Surat al-Ra'd that min remains in its apparent meaning, and that some of His, the Exalted, command guards things from other parts of His command. Thus, there is no reason to take مِنْ أَمْرِهِ to mean "by His command"; rather, His saying بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ means "with the Spirit that is from His command"—the prepositional phrase being stable (مستقر), not redundant (لغو)—as in His saying: قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي, and its meaning is as previously stated.

As for his saying that the Spirit means the revelation or the Quran, and likewise the saying of some that it means prophethood (النبوة), it is not without merit in terms of the result—meaning that the result of the angels descending with the Spirit of His command is revelation or prophethood. However, in itself—that is, naming revelation or prophethood "spirit" by way of homonymy (الاشتراك اللفظي) or metaphor (المجاز) because it gives life to hearts and flourishes them, just as the spirit gives life to bodies—this is invalid. This is because we have explained repeatedly that the way to identify the referents (مصاديق) of words in His, the Exalted, speech is to refer to other parts of His speech that are suitable for its interpretation, rather than referring to common usage (العرف) and what it perceives as the referents of words.

What is gathered from His, the Glorified, speech is that the Spirit is a creation among the creations of Allah, and it is a single reality (حقيقة واحدة) possessing different levels and degrees (مراتب ودرجات). Among them is that which is in animals and non-believing humans; among them is that which is in the believers, as the Exalted said: وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِرُوحٍ مِنْهُ — "...and He has strengthened them with a spirit from Him." (58:22); and among them is that by which prophets and messengers are strengthened, as He said: وَأَيَّدْناهُ بِرُوحِ الْقُدُسِ — "...and We strengthened him with the Holy Spirit." (2:87), and He said: وَكَذلِكَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ رُوحاً مِنْ أَمْرِنا (42:52), as will be detailed later, Allah willing.

This is what the noble verses provide. As for whether the application of the word to this meaning is literal (حقيقة) or metaphorical (مجاز), and what they have delved into regarding whether it is an explicit metaphor (استعارة مصرحة) or a metaphor by metonymy (استعارة بالكناية), or whether His saying: بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ is of the nature of a simile (التشبيه) due to the explicit mention of the thing likened to—based on min in His saying مِنْ أَمْرِهِ being explanatory (بيانية), as they stated regarding His saying: حَتَّى يَتَبَيَّنَ لَكُمُ الْخَيْطُ الْأَبْيَضُ مِنَ الْخَيْطِ الْأَسْوَدِ مِنَ الْفَجْرِ — "...until the white thread becomes manifest to you from the black thread at dawn" (2:187) that it is a simile due to the explicit mention of the thing likened to in the text—all of these are literary and technical discussions that do not have much impact on attaining the realities.

Some mentioned that مِنْ أَمْرِهِ is an explanation (بيان) for the Spirit, and min is for explanation (التبيين), and what is meant by the Spirit is the revelation, as mentioned before.

This is refuted by His, the Exalted, saying: قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي, for it is clear that the two verses follow the same path. The apparent meaning of the verse in al-Isra is that min therein is for origin (الابتداء) or emergence (النشوء), and the intent is to clarify that the Spirit is of the genus of the command and a state among its states. Close to it is His, the Exalted, saying: تَنَزَّلُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَالرُّوحُ فِيها بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ مِنْ كُلِّ أَمْرٍ — "In it the angels and the Spirit descend, by the leave of their Lord, with every command." (97:4).

Some mentioned that what is meant by the Spirit is Gabriel, supporting this with His saying: نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ — "The Trustworthy Spirit has brought it down to your heart," (26:193-194), for it is accepted that what is meant by it in the verse is Gabriel. The ba is for accompaniment, and what is meant by the angels are the angels of revelation who are the assistants of Gabriel, and what is meant by "command" (الأمر) is the singular of "commands" (الأوامر). The meaning would be: the Exalted sends down the angels of revelation accompanied by Gabriel by His command and will.

This is refuted by the fact that this verse is similar to His, the Exalted, saying: يُلْقِي الرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِرهِ عَلى مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ لِيُنْذِرَ يَوْمَ التَّلاقِ — "He casts the Spirit of His behest upon whomever He wishes of His servants, that he may warn [people] of the Day of Encounter." (40:15), and its apparent meaning does not suit the Spirit being Gabriel.

The worst of the views is what some mentioned: that what is meant by the Spirit are the spirits of people—that no angel descends except that one of the spirits is with him. This is narrated from Mujahid, and its invalidity is manifest.

And His saying: عَلى مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ — meaning that the sending of messengers and the descent of the angels with the Spirit of His command upon them is dependent solely upon the Divine will (المشية الإلهية), without any other compeller over Him in that to force Him to the act or prevent Him from it, as is the case with all His, the Exalted, acts; for He, the Exalted, does what He wishes and decrees what He wills.

This does not contradict His act being inseparable from wisdom (حكم) and interests (مصالح), and varying according to the variation of predispositions (الاستعدادات); it does not occur except upon a predisposition in the locus and a capacity for reception. For the predisposition of the predisposed is nothing but like the request of the petitioner; just as the request of the petitioner only brings him closer to the generosity and gift of the one petitioned without forcing him to give, likewise, predisposition in its bringing the predisposed closer to His, the Exalted, bestowal and the deprivation of the non-predisposed from that. Thus, He, the Exalted, does what He wishes without anything making it obligatory upon Him or preventing Him from it, but He does not do anything nor bestow mercy except upon a predisposition in that which He bestows upon and a capacity from it.

He has provided this specifically regarding the messengership (الرسالة) where He said: وَإِذا جاءَتْهُمْ آيَةٌ قالُوا لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ حَتَّى نُؤْتى مِثْلَ ما أُوتِيَ رُسُلُ اللهِ اللهُ أَعْلَمُ حَيْثُ يَجْعَلُ رِسالَتَهُ سَيُصِيبُ الَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا صَغارٌ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَعَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِما كانُوا يَمْكُرُونَ — "When a sign comes to them, they say, ‘We will not believe until we are given the like of what was given to Allah’s apostles.’ Allah knows best where to place His apostleship! Soon the guilty will be overtaken by debasement from Allah and a severe punishment because of the plots they used to devise." (6:124). The verse is clear in that the instances differ in their acceptance of the honor of messengership, and that Allah, the Glorified, knows best the instance that is suitable for it and worthy of that honor, and it is other than these plotting criminals. As for them, they have nothing with Allah except debasement and punishment for their criminality and plotting.

From here, the invalidity of the argument used by some to negate a "preponderant factor" (المرجح) in the case of messengership becomes clear. The summary of what he mentioned is that the verse links messengership to the mere Divine will without restricting it by anything; thus, the messenger only attains messengership by a will from Allah, not because of his being characterized by attributes that qualify him for it and preponderate him over others. The aspect of invalidity is clear from what preceded.

Similar in invalidity is the argument using the verse to claim that messengership is purely a gift (عطائية) and not earned (كسبية). This is because He, the Exalted, is not governed in what is attributed to Him of action; He only does what He wishes. Matters of gift and earning are equal in this regard, and nothing occurs in existence except by His leave.

And His saying: أَنْ أَنْذِرُوا أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا فَاتَّقُونِ — "‘Warn [mankind] that there is no god except Me; so be God-conscious of Me.’" (16:2) is an explanation (بيان) for His saying: يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ because it is in the sense of revelation, or an explanation for the Spirit based on it being in the sense of revelation. Warning (الإنذار) is an announcement containing frightening, just as glad tidings (التبشير) is an announcement containing joy, as mentioned by al-Raghib, or informing of a danger, as mentioned by others. The estimation (التقدير) according to the first is: "Inform them, frightening them, of My oneness in divinity (الألوهية) and the obligation of being God-conscious of Me"; and according to the second: "Make them aware of that," such that أَنَّهُ is a second object, not an accusative by the removal of the preposition.

It has become known from this that His saying: فَاتَّقُونِ is branched out from His saying: لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا. Both sentences together are the second object, or in its place, for His saying: أَنْذِرُوا. This is clarified by the fact that "no god" (لا إله)—and He is the One from Whom everything begins and to Whom everything returns, or the One worshipped in truth (المعبود بالحق)—it is among the necessities of His attribute of divinity that man should be God-conscious of Him, because every good and happiness is dependent upon Him. If it is assumed that He is One with no partner in His divinity, its necessity is that He alone should be the object of godwariness (تقوى), because godwariness—which is the rectification of the station of action (مقام العمل)—is a branch of what is in the station of belief and theory (مقام الاعتقاد والنظر). Thus, the worship of many gods and submission to them is incompatible with the belief in one God with no partner, who is the Sustainer (القيوم) over everything and in whose hand is the rein of every matter. Therefore, no prophet was commanded to call to monotheism without action, or to action without monotheism. The Exalted said: وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا نُوحِي إِلَيْهِ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا فَاعْبُدُونِ — "We did not send any apostle before you but We revealed to him that ‘There is no god except Me; so worship Me.’" (21:25).

So, what the messengers were commanded to warn with in the verse is the totality of His saying: أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا فَاتَّقُونِ, and it is the entirety of the religion, for true beliefs are included in monotheism (التوحيد) and all practical rulings are included in godwariness (التقوى). No weight is given to what some mentioned: that His saying: فَاتَّقُونِ is for the "hasteners" among the disbelievers mentioned in the first verse, or specifically for the disbelievers of Quraysh, without it being included in what the messengers were commanded to warn with.

His saying, the Exalted: خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِّ تَعالى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ — "He created the heavens and the earth with the truth. Exalted is He above [having] any partners that they ascribe [to Him]." (16:3). The meaning of creating the heavens and the earth "with the truth" (بالحق) has been previously mentioned. The necessity of creating them with the truth is that falsehood (الباطل) should have no effect in them. Therefore, He followed it by hallowing Himself from the partners they call upon to intercede for them with Allah, guide them to good, and protect them from evil, for they are of the falsehood that has no effect.

In this verse and the verses following it, there is an argument for His, the Exalted, oneness in divinity (الألوهية) and Lordship (الربوبية) from the aspects of both creation (الخلق) and governance (التدبير). For creation and origination are signs of divinity, and the fact that some of the creation is a blessing (نعمة) in relation to others is a sign of Lordship. This is because a thing does not become a blessing in relation to another except due to a connection between them and a link from one to the other that leads to a comprehensive system (نظام جامع) between them and a single governance that unites them. The unity of governance is a sign of the unity of the Governor (المدبر). Thus, the fact that what is in the heavens and the earth of creation are blessings for man indicates that Allah, the Glorified, alone is his Lord and the Lord of everything.

His saying, the Exalted: خَلَقَ الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ فَإِذا هُوَ خَصِيمٌ مُبِينٌ — "He created man from a drop of [seminal] fluid, and behold, he is an open adversary!" (16:4). What is meant by it is the creation occurring in the human species, which is making its progeny from a drop of fluid (النطفة); thus, it does not include Adam and Jesus (عليه‌السلام).

The "adversary" (الخصيم) is a permanent attribute (صفة مشبهة) from "adversity" (الخصومة), which is disputation (الجدال). Although the verse could be taken as an expression of favor (الامتنان)—in that it is a great favor that Allah, the Glorified, by His perfect power, changes a drop of base water into a human of complete form, articulate and speaking, announcing everything great and small with his eloquent speech—the abundance of verses that rebuke man and upbraid him for his insolence in disputing with his Lord, such as His, the Exalted, saying: أَوَلَمْ يَرَ الْإِنْسانُ أَنَّا خَلَقْناهُ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ فَإِذا هُوَ خَصِيمٌ مُبِينٌ وَضَرَبَ لَنا مَثَلاً وَنَسِيَ خَلْقَهُ قالَ مَنْ يُحْيِ الْعِظامَ وَهِيَ رَمِيمٌ — "Does not man see that We created him from a drop of fluid? Then behold, he is an open adversary! He draws a comparison for Us, and forgets his own creation. He says, ‘Who shall revive the bones when they have decayed?’" (36:77-78), makes it more likely that the end of the verse is intended to describe man's insolence.

This is also somewhat supported by what is at the end of the previous verse regarding His, the Exalted, hallowing from their polytheism.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْأَنْعامَ خَلَقَها لَكُمْ فِيها دِفْءٌ وَمَنافِعُ وَمِنْها تَأْكُلُونَ — "He created the cattle for you; in them there is warmth and [other] uses and you eat of them." (16:5). Cattle (الأنعام) is the plural of na'am (نعم), which are camels, cows, and sheep; they are so named because of the softness (ni'mah) of their touch, unlike the solid-hoofed animals which are hard, as in al-Majma'. In al-Mufradat: warmth (الدفء) is the opposite of cold. [End of quote]. It seems what is meant by warmth is what is obtained from their skins, wool, and hair of heat to protect against the cold, or what is meant by dif' is that which provides warmth.

What is meant by "uses" (المنافع) are all other things benefited from them besides warmth, such as their wool, hair, skins, milk, fat, and others. His saying: لَكُمْ (for you) could be connected to His saying: خَلَقَها (He created them), and His saying: فِيها دِفْءٌ وَمَنافِعُ would be a circumstantial clause (حال) from the pronoun in خَلَقَها. Alternatively, لَكُمْ could be a stable adverbial phrase (ظرف مستقر) connected to the second sentence—meaning: in the cattle is warmth existing for you.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَكُمْ فِيها جَمالٌ حِينَ تُرِيحُونَ وَحِينَ تَسْرَحُونَ — "And there is a beauty in them for you when you bring them home [from the pasture] and when you take them out to graze." (16:6). Beauty (الجمال) is adornment and a pleasant sight. It is said in al-Majma': al-iraha (الإراحة) is returning the livestock in the evening from their pastures to their dwellings, and the place where they are rested is the murah. al-suruh (السروح) is the departure of the livestock to the pasture in the morning. [End of quote].

He, the Exalted, says: And there is for you in the cattle a beautiful sight when you return them in the evening to their dwellings and when you take them out in the morning to their pastures.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَحْمِلُ أَثْقالَكُمْ إِلى بَلَدٍ لَمْ تَكُونُوا بالِغِيهِ إِلَّا بِشِقِّ الْأَنْفُسِ إِنَّ رَبَّكُمْ لَرَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ — "They carry your loads to a town that you could not reach except with great hardship to yourselves. Indeed your Lord is most kind and merciful." (16:7). Loads (الأثقال) is the plural of thiql (ثقل), which is the baggage that is heavy to carry. What is meant by His saying: بِشِقِّ الْأَنْفُسِ is the hardship (مشقة) that souls endure in traversing long distances and difficult paths.

The meaning is that cattle, such as camels and some cows, carry your heavy baggage to a town that it would not be easy for you to reach except with hardship endured by your souls; thus, He removed the hardships from you by creating them and subjecting them to you. Indeed, your Lord is Most Kind, Most Merciful.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْخَيْلَ وَالْبِغالَ وَالْحَمِيرَ لِتَرْكَبُوها وَزِينَةً وَيَخْلُقُ ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ — "And horses, mules and asses, for you to ride them, and for adornment, and He creates what you do not know." (16:8). This is conjoined to "the cattle" (الأنعام) mentioned previously—meaning: and horses, mules, and asses, He created them for you to ride them, and as an adornment (زينة)—meaning that in their creation there is a connection to your benefits, which is that you ride them and take them as adornment and beauty. His saying: وَيَخْلُقُ ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ means He creates what you have no knowledge of among animals and others, and He subjected them to you so that you may benefit from them. The evidence for what we have estimated is the context.

His saying, the Exalted: وَعَلَى اللهِ قَصْدُ السَّبِيلِ وَمِنْها جائِرٌ وَلَوْ شاءَ لَهَداكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — "With Allah rests the guidance to the straight path, and some of them are devious, and had He wished He would have guided you all." (16:9). al-qasd (القصد)—as mentioned by al-Raghib and others—is the straightness of the path, which is its being upright for its wayfarers, leading them to the goal. It is apparent that the verbal noun (المصدر) is in the sense of the active participle (الفاعل), and the genitive construction is the addition of the attribute to its described object. What is meant is the "straight path" (السبيل القاصد) due to the evidence of its contrast with His saying: وَمِنْها جائِرٌ — meaning: and among the paths is that which is devious (جائِرٌ)—that is, deviating from the goal, leading its wayfarers elsewhere and misguiding them from it.

The meaning of the straight path being "upon Allah" (على الله) is the obligation of making a straight path upon Him, the Exalted, which His servants traverse so that it leads them to the place of happiness and success. Since there is no ruler other than Him to rule over Him, He is the One who made it obligatory upon Himself to make for them a path with this description, then to guide them to it. As for the "making" (الجعل), it is what Allah equipped every existent with—including man—of powers and tools such that if he used them as they were organized, they would lead him to his happiness and desired perfection. The Exalted said: الَّذِي أَعْطى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى — "...He who gave everything its creation and then guided it." (20:50). He said regarding man specifically: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ — "So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — of Allah according to which He originated mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation." (30:30).

As for the guidance (الهداية), it is that which He performed from the side of the primordial nature (الفطرة) and culminated in what is by way of sending messengers, sending down books, and legislating laws. The Exalted said: وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها — "By the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [the discernment of] its wickedness and its godwariness," (91:7-8). He said: إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شاكِراً وَإِمَّا كَفُوراً — "Indeed We have guided him to the way, be he grateful or ungrateful." (76:3).

The Glorified only inserted this verse among these verses—whose context is the enumeration of celestial and terrestrial blessings from the sky, the earth, cattle, horses, mules, asses, the water descending from the sky, crops, and their likes—because the speech in the two verses regarding cattle and horses led to the meaning of traversing paths and riding mounts. Thus, it was appropriate to mention what He bestowed of the spiritual path that leads man to his true goal which he seeks in the journey of life, just as He bestowed its like in the world of matter and the realm of form.

So, the Glorified mentioned that among His blessings which He favored His servants with is that He made obligatory upon Himself for them a straight path that leads them to the happiness of their lives; so He made it for them and guided them to it.

The Glorified attributed the "straight path" (قصد السبيل) to Himself but not the "devious path" (السبيل الجائر), because the path of misguidance is not a path made by Him in parallel to the path of guidance; rather, it is the departure from the path and the failure to be characterized by traversing it. So it is not a path in reality, but rather the absence of the path.

In any case, the verse is clear in attributing the straight path to Him, the Exalted, and omitting the attribution of the devious path, which leads—by way of contrast—to the negation of its attribution to Him, the Exalted.

Since it was possible to imagine that the necessity of His making the straight path is that He would be ungrateful for His blessing and overcome in His governance and Lordship—since He made the path but the majority did not traverse it, and He guided to it but those called were not guided by it—He refuted this with His, the Exalted, saying: وَلَوْ شاءَ لَهَداكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — meaning: the lack of guidance of everyone is not due to an inability on His part, the Glorified, for that, or a victory of these laggards and their triumph over Him; rather, it is because He, the Exalted, did not will that, and had He willed, they would have had no choice but to be guided all together. So He is the Subduer (القاهر), the Victorious in every state.

In other words: the straight path that Allah, the Exalted, made is the path based on human choice (اختيار), which he traverses by performing righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة) and avoiding sins out of his own choice. That which is of this nature is not something that is forced, nor is it universal for everyone, for natures are diverse and compositions are different, and inevitably their effects vary, and individuals differ in faith and disbelief, godwariness and wickedness, obedience and disobedience.

The verse is one over which the Ash'arites and Mu'tazilites among the Muslim sects have disputed. The Mu'tazilites argued that the change in style—by making the straight path "upon Allah" but not the devious path—is to indicate what is permissible to attribute to Him, the Exalted, and what is not, as mentioned in al-Kashshaf.

The Ash'arites burdened themselves in responding to it. Some replied that both paths are from Him, the Exalted, and the devious path was only not attributed to Him out of etiquette (تأدبا). Others replied that what is meant by His saying: وَعَلَى اللهِ قَصْدُ السَّبِيلِ is that it is upon Him, the Exalted, to clarify the true path out of favor and generosity from Him, rather than clarifying the devious path; as for the origin of the "making" (الجعل), both are made by Him, the Exalted. Others denied that the change in style in the verse was for any intended purpose.

The truth is that the indication of the verse that the straight path is attributed to Him, the Exalted, but not the devious path, is something in which there is no doubt. However, that does not necessitate that the devious path is created by other than Him, the Exalted, because—as previously mentioned—the path of misguidance is not a path in reality; rather, its reality is the failure to traverse the path of guidance, just as misguidance (الضلال) is the absence of guidance (الهدى). So it is not an existing thing such that its creation and origination should be attributed to Him, the Exalted. Misguidance is only attributed to Him, the Exalted, where it is attributed, in the sense of His not guiding the misguided—that is, His not creating guidance in his soul.

Furthermore, that which is attributed to Him of misguidance—as in His saying: يُضِلُّ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَيَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ — "...He leads astray whomever He wishes and guides whomever He wishes." (35:8) and His saying: يُضِلُّ بِهِ كَثِيراً وَيَهْدِي بِهِ كَثِيراً — "...He misleads many by it and guides many by it..." (2:26)—is misguidance by way of requital (المجازاة), not initial misguidance. This is interpreted by His saying: وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ — "...and He misleads no one by it except the transgressors." (2:26). Thus, if man transgresses and departs, by his own evil choice, from the garb of servitude by disobeying and not returning—which is his initial misguidance from himself—Allah requites him with misguidance by fixing him in his state and not decreeing guidance for him.

As for the initial misguidance from man, it is merely a holding back and a falling short of obedience, while Allah has guided him through the primordial nature (الفطرة) and the call of prophethood.

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً لَكُمْ مِنْهُ شَرابٌ وَمِنْهُ شَجَرٌ فِيهِ تُسِيمُونَ — "It is He who sends down water from the sky: from it you get your drink and from it are the plants on which you let [your cattle] graze." (16:10). This is the beginning of another type of blessing, which are the botanical blessings that sustain man and others, and what He subjected for the governance of their affairs, such as the night and day, the sun and moon, and their likes. Therefore, He changed the context and said: هُوَ الَّذِي etc., and did not say: "And He sent down from the sky."

His saying: تُسِيمُونَ is from al-isama (الإسامة), which is the grazing of livestock, and from it comes al-sa'imah for grazing livestock. The first min (مِنَ) is partitive (تبعيضية) and the second is for origin (نشوئية). "Trees/Plants" (الشجر) refers to vegetation that has a trunk and leaves, and sometimes it is used broadly to include both that which has a trunk and that which does not. Among this is the shajar mentioned in the verse because of His saying: فِيهِ تُسِيمُونَ. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: يُنْبِتُ لَكُمْ بِهِ الزَّرْعَ وَالزَّيْتُونَ وَالنَّخِيلَ وَالْأَعْنابَ وَمِنْ كُلِّ الثَّمَراتِ etc. — "With it He grows for you crops, the olive, the date palm, the vines, and every kind of fruit..." (16:11). The olive (الزيتون) is a well-known tree and is also applied to its fruit; it is said to be a collective generic noun whose singular is zaytunah. Likewise, the date palm (النخيل), which is applied to both singular and plural. Vines (الأعناب) is the plural of inabah, which is the fruit of the grapevine, and it is applied to the tree itself as in the verse. The context implies that His saying: وَمِنْ كُلِّ الثَّمَراتِ has the estimation: "and from all fruits He grows their trees." Perhaps the explicit mention of these three fruits specifically and conjoining the rest to them is because they are what are mostly used for sustenance.

Since in this vast, general governance—which unites man and animal in their sustenance—there is an argument for His, the Exalted, oneness in Lordship, He concluded the verse with His saying: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ — "There is indeed a sign in that for a people who reflect."

His saying, the Exalted: وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ وَالشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ to the end of the verse — "He has disposed the night and the day for you, and the sun and the moon..." (16:12). The discussion on the meaning of the subjection (تسخير) of the night, day, sun, moon, and stars has been repeated. Because each of the mentioned things—as well as the totality of the night and day, and the totality of the sun, moon, and stars—possesses properties and effects in itself that are capable of independently proving His oneness in Lordship, He concluded the verse with His saying: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ — "There are indeed signs in that for a people who exercise their reason." Thus, He used the plural "signs" (آيات) in this verse, unlike the previous and subsequent verses.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما ذَرَأَ لَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُخْتَلِفاً أَلْوانُهُ إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِقَوْمٍ يَذَّكَّرُونَ — "And whatever He has created for you in the earth of diverse hues. There is indeed a sign in that for a people who take admonition." (16:13). al-dhar' (الذرء) is creation. The diversity of colors of what He created in the earth is other than what has passed, such as the diversity of colors of minerals and other elemental compounds that man benefits from in his livelihood. It is not unlikely that the diversity of colors is a metaphor for the specific (نوعي) difference between them; thus, the verse is close in content to His, the Exalted, saying: وَفِي الْأَرْضِ قِطَعٌ مُتَجاوِراتٌ وَجَنَّاتٌ مِنْ أَعْنابٍ وَزَرْعٌ وَنَخِيلٌ صِنْوانٌ وَغَيْرُ صِنْوانٍ يُسْقى بِماءٍ واحِدٍ وَنُفَضِّلُ بَعْضَها عَلى بَعْضٍ فِي الْأُكُلِ — "In the earth are neighbouring terrains [of diverse kinds] and gardens of vines, and farms, and date palms growing from a single root and from different roots, [all] watered by the same water, and We give some of them an advantage over others in eating..." (13:4). The approach to the argument using this has already been mentioned.

The diversity of colors in what He created in the earth—like the growing of trees and fruits—is a single matter used to argue for His oneness in Lordship; therefore, He said: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً (There is indeed a sign) and did not say "signs."

These are three arguments: He attributed the first to those who reflect (يتفكرون), the second to those who exercise reason (يعقلون), and the third to those who take admonition (يذكرون). This is because the first argument is composed of simple premises for which absolute reflection is sufficient to produce the result. The second is composed of scientific premises whose understanding is not possible except for one who has delved into the states of the celestial and terrestrial bodies and reasoned the effects of their motions and transitions. The third is composed of universal philosophical premises that man only attains by remembering (تذكر) the general universal laws of existence, such as the need of this changing realm for matter, the fact that the general matter is one and uniform in nature, and the necessity of these real differences terminating in another matter beyond the single, uniform matter.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي سَخَّرَ الْبَحْرَ لِتَأْكُلُوا مِنْهُ لَحْماً طَرِيًّا وَتَسْتَخْرِجُوا مِنْهُ حِلْيَةً تَلْبَسُونَها etc. — "It is He who disposed the sea [for your benefit] that you may eat fresh meat from it, and extract from it ornaments which you wear..." (16:14). This is another section of Divine blessings, which are the blessings of the sea, mountains, rivers, paths, and landmarks. What preceded it was the section containing the blessings of the land and plains, such as trees, fruits, and the like. Therefore, He said: وَهُوَ الَّذِي سَخَّرَ and did not say: "And He subjected" etc.

"Fresh" (الطري) is a form of fa'il from al-tarawah, which is the tender and new of a thing, as mentioned in al-Mufradat. al-makhr (المخر) is the cleaving of the water to the right and left; it is said: "the ship cleaves (tamkhuru) the water," so it is a "cleaver" (makhirah). makhr al-ard is also cleaving it for agriculture, as in al-Majma'. What is meant by eating fresh meat from the sea is eating the meat of fish caught from it, and by extracting ornaments you wear is what is extracted from it by diving, such as pearls and coral, with which women adorn and beautify themselves.

His saying: وَتَرَى الْفُلْكَ مَواخِرَ فِيهِ — meaning: you witness the ships cleaving its water to the right and left. Perhaps His saying: وَتَرَى (and you see) is of the general addresses that do not intend a specific addressee; it is frequently used as such, meaning: every seer sees it and everyone who can witness it witnesses it. So it is not of the nature of a transition from the previous plural address to a singular address.

His saying: وَلِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ — meaning: and that you may seek some of His provision in riding the sea and sending ships in it. The sentence is conjoined to a deleted part, and the estimation is: "and you see the ships cleaving it so that you may attain such and such, and that you may seek of His favor." This has many parallels in His, the Exalted, speech.

His saying: وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ — meaning: and among the goals in the subjection of the sea and the running of ships in it is your gratitude to Him, which is hoped from you. For it is of His, the Exalted, increase in blessing; He has enriched you with what He bestowed upon you on land such that you would not need to operate in the sea by diving, running ships, and other things, but He, the Exalted, increased you in blessing by subjecting the sea for you, so that perhaps you would thank Him for this addition. For man rarely notices in necessities that they are a blessing bestowed from His, the Glorified, presence, and that had He willed, He could have cut them off; whereas useful additions are closer to this noticing and transition.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَلْقى فِي الْأَرْضِ رَواسِيَ أَنْ تَمِيدَ بِكُمْ وَأَنْهاراً وَسُبُلاً لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ — "He has cast firm mountains in the earth lest it should shake with you, and rivers and ways, so that you may be guided." (16:15). It is said in al-Majma': al-mayd (الميد) is tilting to the right and left, which is agitation; mada yamidu maydan. [End of quote].

His saying: أَنْ تَمِيدَ بِكُمْ — meaning: for fear that it should shake with you, or so that it should not shake with you. The meaning is that He cast upon the earth firm mountains so that it would not agitate and tilt to the right and left, thereby disrupting the system of your livelihood.

His saying: وَأَنْهاراً — meaning: and He made in it rivers that run with their water and lead it to your farms and gardens, and provide drink for you and the domestic animals you have.

His saying: وَسُبُلاً لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ is conjoined to His saying: وَأَنْهاراً — meaning: and He made paths for the goal of guidance hoped from you. Paths (السبل) include those that are natural, which are the distances located between two spots of the earth connecting one to the other without being cut off by a barrier or obstacle, like the plain between two mountains. And they include those that are artificial, which are formed by the passage of wayfarers and footprints, or which man makes.

It is apparent from the context that "paths" (السبل) is general to both types. There is no harm in attributing what man has made to His, the Exalted, making, just as He attributed the rivers and landmarks to His making, though most of them are of man's making; and just as He attributed what man has made of idols and others to His creation in His saying: وَاللهُ خَلَقَكُمْ وَما تَعْمَلُونَ — "While Allah has created you and whatever you make?" (37:96).

This is because they—whatever they may be—are among the effects of His, the Exalted, made things. Making a thing that possesses an effect is a making of its effect in a way, even if it is not a direct making without an intermediary.

His saying, the Exalted: وَعَلاماتٍ وَبِالنَّجْمِ هُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ — "And landmarks [as well], and by the stars they are guided." (16:16). Landmarks (العلامات) is the plural of alamah, which is that by which a thing is known. it is conjoined to His saying: وَأَنْهاراً — meaning: and He made landmarks by which you seek guidance to things hidden from the senses; these are every natural or conventional sign and indicator that points to its signified, including markers, monuments, languages, gestures, scripts, and others.

Then the Glorified mentioned the guidance by the stars, saying: وَبِالنَّجْمِ هُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ. Perhaps the transition therein from direct address to the third person is to avoid the repetition of تَهْتَدُونَ (you are guided) in the second person at the end of the two verses.

The previous verse: وَعَلَى اللهِ قَصْدُ السَّبِيلِ وَمِنْها جائِرٌ وَلَوْ شاءَ لَهَداكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ, which contains the matter of spiritual guidance (الهداية المعنوية) and which is, as it were, parenthetical among the verses enumerating formal blessings—although it would apparently be more appropriate to place it after this verse, meaning His saying: وَبِالنَّجْمِ هُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ, which addresses formal guidance—however, that would not have been free from confusion and the illusion of contradiction, unlike its current position where it is situated, even if it is like a parenthesis, as is apparent.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَمَنْ يَخْلُقُ كَمَنْ لا يَخْلُقُ to His saying: إِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ (verses 17-22). These verses are a summary restatement of the argument mentioned in detail within the past sixteen verses, and a conclusion for monotheism (التوحيد). It is a single argument established for the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية). Its summary is that Allah, the Glorified, is the Creator of everything; so He is the One who bestowed these blessings—which cannot be encompassed by enumeration—by which the system of the universe is organized. And He, the Exalted, is the Knower of their secret and their manifest; so He is the One who owns all and governs the matter; thus He is their Lord. And nothing of what they call upon possesses any of these attributes; so they are not lords. Therefore, the God is One and none other, and He is Allah, exalted is His Name.

From here, the invalidity of what some mentioned—that the verses establish monotheism through two paths: the path of creation and the path of blessing—becomes clear. The explanation of the invalidity is that the path of creation alone only proves the Maker and His oneness in creation and origination. The idolaters—and the speech in the verses is directed to them—do not deny the existence of the Maker, nor that Allah, the Glorified, is the Creator of all, even their idols, and that their idols are not creators of anything. They only claim for their idols the governance of the world's affairs by a delegation from Allah to them for that, and intercession with Allah. Thus, proving the Maker against these people does not benefit anything.

Rather, the verses of creation were brought to establish the matter of blessing. For it is clear that if Allah, the Glorified, is the Creator of everything, bringing it into existence, then the effects of the existences of things—which are the blessings that are enjoyed—belong to Him, the Glorified, just as their existences belong to Him as an absolute ownership that accepts neither nullification, nor transfer, nor change. So He, the Glorified, is the One who bestows them in reality, and none other, not even the thing whose existence the blessing is an effect of, for it and its effects belong to Allah alone.

Therefore, He joined to the discourse on creation and bestowal His, the Exalted, saying: وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ ما تُسِرُّونَ وَما تُعْلِنُونَ — "And Allah knows what you hide and what you proclaim." (16:19). This is because the mere attribution of creation and bestowal to a thing does not necessitate its Lordship, nor does it warrant its worship, unless knowledge (العلم) is joined to them. This completes the fact that He is a Governor who guides everything to its desired perfection and its happiness written in its record of deeds. It is well known that worship only stands as worship if the Worshipped is characterized by the mark of knowledge, knowing the worship of the one who worships Him, witnessing his submission.

So, the totality of what the verses contain of the discourse on creation, blessing, and knowledge are premises for a single argument established for the monotheism of Lordship, which the idolaters deny, as you have known.

Thus, His saying: أَفَمَنْ يَخْلُقُ كَمَنْ لا يَخْلُقُ أَفَلا تَذَكَّرُونَ — "Is He who creates like one who does not create? Will you not then take admonition?" (16:17) is a comparison of what belongs to Him, the Glorified, of attributes to what belongs to others, and a negation of equality. The interrogation is for denial (الإنكار). The meaning of "one who does not create" refers to their gods whom they call upon besides Allah.

Its explanation—as has appeared from what preceded—is that Allah, the Glorified, creates things and continues in their creation; so He is not equal to one who creates nothing. For He, the Exalted, by His creating things, owns their existences and the effects of their existences, which are the systems specific to them and the general system running over them.

And His saying: وَإِنْ تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَةَ اللهِ لا تُحْصُوها etc. — "If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them..." (16:18) is an indication of the abundance of Divine blessings—an abundance that is beyond the scope of enumeration. In reality, there is nothing but that it is a blessing when measured against the universal system, even if there might be found among them that which is not a blessing when measured against some other part.

The Exalted has justified this by His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ "Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful." (16:18). This is among the most subtle and precise of justifications. The Exalted has conveyed that the fact that blessings exceed the limit of enumeration is only due to the blessings of His being characterized by the two attributes of forgiveness (المغفرة) and mercy (الرحمة). For through His forgiveness—and forgiveness is concealment (الستر)—He conceals the bane of deficiency and the ugliness of inadequacy inherent in things. And through His mercy—and mercy is the completion of deficiency and the fulfillment of need—goodness and perfection appear within them, and He adorns them with beauty. Thus, by the spreading of forgiveness and mercy over all things, every thing becomes beneficial to others and a desired good for them, thereby becoming a blessing (نعمة) in relation to them. Consequently, things are blessings for one another. Thus, the Divine blessing possesses a breadth and expanse commensurate with His forgiveness and mercy: "And if you enumerate Allah’s blessing, you will not be able to count it"; so understand that.

This verse is among the instances where forgiveness (المغفرة) is used for something other than sin and disobedience regarding a masterly command, which is the sense well-known among the practitioners of the Sharia (المتشرعة).

His saying: وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ ما تُسِرُّونَ وَما تُعْلِنُونَ "And Allah knows what you hide and what you proclaim." (16:19) is an allusion to the third pillar among the pillars of Lordship (الربوبية), which is knowledge (العلم). For if the Deity were not characterized by knowledge, worship and non-worship would be equal in relation to Him, and His worship would be a futility (لغو) with no effect.

It is therefore necessary for the worshipped Lord to possess knowledge—and not just any knowledge, in whatever form—but rather knowledge of the manifest (ظاهر) and the hidden (باطن) of the one who worships Him. This is because worship is constituted by intention (النية); it only occurs as true worship if it is performed with a righteous intention, which is something that pertains to the inner conscience (الضمير). Thus, knowledge that the outward form of worship possesses its true essential meaning cannot be complete unless the Worshipped encompasses the manifest and the hidden of the worshipper. However, Allah, the Glorified, is All-Knowing of what man conceals and what he proclaims, just as He is the Creator and Bestower of blessings, and by that, He is worthy of being worshipped.

From here, the reason for choosing the specific expression in the verse to explain His knowledge becomes apparent. He did not use expressions such as His saying: عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهادَةِ "Knower of the Unseen and the Manifest" or His saying: وَاللهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ "And Allah has knowledge of all things." Rather, He said: وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ ما تُسِرُّونَ وَما تُعْلِنُونَ "And Allah knows what you hide and what you proclaim," mentioning knowledge of the secret and the public, and attributing it to man. This is because the discourse concerns man's worship of his Lord. What is necessary in the knowledge of worship—which is linked to the actions of both the limbs and the heart—is that He be knowledgeable of what man conceals and proclaims of heart-felt intention, as well as bodily states and movements.

His saying: وَالَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ لا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْئاً وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ "Those whom they invoke besides Allah do not create anything and are themselves created." (16:20) is an allusion to the absence of the first pillar of the pillars of Lordship in their deities whom they invoke besides Allah. From this follows the absence of the second pillar, which is the bestowing of blessings (إيتاء النعمة). Thus, those they invoke are not gods or lords, while Allah is the Lord.

His saying: أَمْواتٌ غَيْرُ أَحْياءٍ وَما يَشْعُرُونَ أَيَّانَ يُبْعَثُونَ "[They are] dead, not alive, and they do not know when they will be resurrected." (16:21) is an allusion to the absence of the third pillar of the pillars of Lordship in their idols, which is knowledge of what they conceal and proclaim. He has emphasized the negation of this by negating the very basis of life (الحياة), which necessitates the negation of absolute knowledge, let alone its perfect type, which is the knowledge of what they conceal and proclaim. Thus He said: أَمْواتٌ غَيْرُ أَحْياءٍ "[They are] dead, not alive." He first established death, which is incompatible with consciousness (الشعور), then reinforced it by negating life a second time.

He specifically mentioned among the aspects of their ignorance their lack of consciousness regarding when their worshippers among mankind will be resurrected, saying: وَما يَشْعُرُونَ أَيَّانَ يُبْعَثُونَ "and they do not know when they will be resurrected." That is, the idols do not know when their worshippers will be raised. For worship is that for which man is recompensed on the Day of Resurrection; thus, it is necessary for the worshipped Deity to know when the Day of Resurrection is, so that He may recompense His servants therein for their worship. These [idols] know nothing of that.

From here it becomes clear that the first of the two plural pronouns—يَشْعُرُونَ (they know)—refers to the idols, and the second—يُبْعَثُونَ (they will be resurrected)—refers to the polytheists. As for referring both of them to the idols, it is unsatisfactory, because knowledge of the Resurrection is exclusive to Him, the Glorified, and hidden from others; ignorance of it is not exclusive to idols. Even worse than that is the view of some who say that both plural pronouns in the verse refer to the polytheists.

These verses, although outwardly directed toward negating the lordship of idols, the same reasoning—with the slightest scrutiny—applies to the masters of the idols, such as the near-stationed angels, the jinn, the perfected among humans, and the stars—all that the idolaters worship. This is because the attributes of creation, bestowing blessings, and knowledge do not subsist by essence (بالأصالة) and independence (الاستقلال) except in Allah, the Glorified. There is no true Lordship except by essence and independence; so understand.

In these two verses—meaning from His saying: وَالَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ to His saying: يُبْعَثُونَ—there is a transition (التفات) from address (الخطاب) to the third person (الغيبة). Perhaps the subtle point (النكتة) in this is the mention of the Day of Resurrection in them, which the polytheists do not believe in. Thus, the address was turned from them to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to convey the matter to him without facing their opposition.

His saying: إِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ "Your God is the One God." (16:22) is an explanation of the conclusion of the argument established in the preceding verses. That is, since Allah, the Glorified, is the one who possesses that upon which Divinity (الألوهية)—which is being the Worshipped in Truth—depends, and others besides Him whom they invoke lack everything upon which it depends—namely creation, bestowing blessings, and knowledge—then your God who is worthy of being worshipped is One, and the necessary meaning of this is that He is Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه).

(Commentary on Narrations)

In al-Majma': "Forty verses from its beginning are Meccan, and the rest, from His saying: وَالَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا فِي اللهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما ظُلِمُوا لَنُبَوِّئَنَّهُمْ (16:41) until the end of the Surah, are Medinan," narrated from al-Hasan and Qatadah. It is also said: "It is all Meccan except for three verses revealed upon the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله return from Uhud: وَإِنْ عاقَبْتُمْ فَعاقِبُوا (16:126) until the end of the Surah, which were revealed between Mecca and Medina," narrated from Ibn Abbas, Ata, and al-Sha'bi. In one of the narrations from Ibn Abbas: "Part of it is Meccan and part is Medinan; the Meccan part is from its beginning until His saying: وَلَكُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ (16:94), and the Medinan part is His saying: وَلا تَشْتَرُوا بِعَهْدِ اللهِ ثَمَناً قَلِيلاً (16:95) until His saying: بِأَحْسَنِ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ (16:97)."

I say: We have already stated that what the context (السياق) provides is contrary to all of that, so refer back to it.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Hisham ibn Salim, from one of our companions, from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah: أَتى أَمْرُ اللهِ فَلا تَسْتَعْجِلُوهُ (16:1). He said: 'When Allah informs the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله of something [to occur] at a [certain] time, that is His saying: أَتى أَمْرُ اللهِ فَلا تَسْتَعْجِلُوهُ until that time arrives.' And he said: 'Indeed, when Allah informs that something is to be, it is as if it has already been.'"

I say: It is as if this is an allusion to the fact that the expression in the verse uses the past tense (لفظ الماضي) to indicate the certainty of its occurrence (تحقق الوقوع).

In al-Durr al-Manthur: "Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn Abbas who said: 'When أَتى أَمْرُ اللهِ was revealed, the companions of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله were terrified until فَلا تَسْتَعْجِلُوهُ was revealed, then they became calm.'"

In the same source: "Ibn Jarir and Ibn al-Mundhir extracted from Ibn Jurayj who said: 'When this verse was revealed: أَتى أَمْرُ اللهِ فَلا تَسْتَعْجِلُوهُ, some men from the hypocrites said to one another: "This man claims that the command of Allah has come—so refrain from some of what you were doing until you see what happens." When they saw that nothing was descending, they said: "We do not see that anything has descended."

Then there was revealed: اقْتَرَبَ لِلنَّاسِ حِسابُهُمْ "The people’s reckoning has drawn near" (21:1). They said: "This man claims the same again." When they saw that nothing was descending, they said: "We do not see that anything has descended." Then there was revealed: وَلَئِنْ أَخَّرْنا عَنْهُمُ الْعَذابَ إِلى أُمَّةٍ مَعْدُودَةٍ "And if We defer their punishment until a specific time" (11:8).'"

I say: The narration indicates that there were hypocrites among the Muslims before the Hijrah, as is attested by some other narrations.

In the same source: "Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Hakim—who authenticated it—extracted from Uqbah ibn Amir who said: 'The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "Before the Hour, a black cloud will rise upon you from the west like a shield; it will continue to rise in the sky until it fills the sky. Then a caller will cry out: 'O people!' and the people will turn to one another [asking]: 'Did you hear?' Some will say: 'Yes,' and some will doubt. Then he will cry out a second time: 'O people!' and the people will say: 'Did you hear?' They will say: 'Yes.' Then he will cry out: 'O people! The command of Allah has come, so do not seek to hasten it.'"

The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "By Him in whose hand is my soul, two men will be spreading a garment and will not [have time to] fold it; a man will be filling his trough and will not [have time to] water anything from it; and a man will be milking his she-camel and will not [have time to] drink it, and the people will be occupied."'"

I say: Some have attempted to infer from these three narrations—and others of similar meaning—that what is meant by "the command" (الأمر) is the Day of Resurrection, but there is no proof in them for that.

As for the first narration, their terror does not prove that they understood it as such, for the command of Allah, whatever it may be, is something that inspires awe in His servants. Furthermore, there is no proof in their understanding; the ambiguity is not conceptual (مفهومية) such that one would refer to them as the people of the language.

Moreover, the narration is not without its problems, for Allah, the Glorified, considers seeking to hasten the Resurrection to be a characteristic of the disbelievers and censures them for it, while exonerating the believers from it. He said: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مُشْفِقُونَ مِنْها "and those who have faith are apprehensive of it" (42:18). This was already alluded to in the preceding commentary. This is if the address in His saying: فَلا تَسْتَعْجِلُوهُ "so do not seek to hasten it" is to the believers. But if the ones addressed are the polytheists—and they were the ones seeking to hasten it—then the meaning of the prohibition against their hastening it is the arrival of the term and the proximity of the occurrence, not deferment and granting respite. In that case, there would be no meaning to their becoming calm when they heard His saying: فَلا تَسْتَعْجِلُوهُ.

As for the second narration, its outward sense is that they understood it to mean worldly punishment rather than the Hour; thus, it supports what we previously stated in the commentary, not what they mentioned.

As for the third narration, the most it indicates is that the onset of the Hour is among the instances of the coming of Allah's command, and there is no doubt in that. This is different from the "command" in the verse being the Hour itself.

In the book al-Ghaybah by al-Nu'mani, with its chain of transmission from Abd al-Rahman ibn Kathir, from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام: "Regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: أَتى أَمْرُ اللهِ فَلا تَسْتَعْجِلُوهُ, he said: 'It is our command—the command of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic—so do not seek to hasten it. He will support it with three armies: the angels, the believers, and the cavalry. His emergence will be like the emergence of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and that is His saying: كَما أَخْرَجَكَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَيْتِكَ بِالْحَقِ "As your Lord brought you out of your home with the truth" (8:5).'"

I say: It was also narrated by al-Mufid in the book al-Ghaybah from Abd al-Rahman from him (عليه السلام). His meaning is the appearance of the Mahdi عليه‌السلام, as explicitly stated in other narrations, and this is part of the "flow" (جري) of the Quran or its inner meaning (بطن).

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Sa'd al-Iskaf, who said: "A man came to the Commander of the Faithful عليه‌السلام asking him about the Spirit (الروح)—is it not Gabriel? The Commander of the Faithful عليه‌السلام said to him: 'Gabriel is from the angels, and the Spirit is other than Gabriel.' This became difficult for the man, and he said: 'You have said a momentous thing—no one claims that the Spirit is other than Gabriel.' The Commander of the Faithful عليه‌السلام said to him: 'You are astray, narrating from the people of misguidance. Allah says to His Prophet: أَتى أَمْرُ اللهِ فَلا تَسْتَعْجِلُوهُ ـ سُبْحانَهُ وَتَعالى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ "The edict of Allah has come; so do not seek to hasten it. Immaculate is He and exalted above [the partners] they ascribe [to Him]. He sends down the angels with the Spirit" (16:1-2), and the Spirit is other than the angels.'"

I say: This supports what we previously stated. In other narrations, it is stated that it is a creation greater than Gabriel.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding the saying of the Exalted: فَإِذا هُوَ خَصِيمٌ مُبِينٌ "behold, he is an open adversary" (16:4), he (عليه السلام) said: "He created him from a drop of base water, yet he becomes an adversary, speaking and eloquent."

In the same source, regarding the saying of the Exalted: حِينَ تُرِيحُونَ وَحِينَ تَسْرَحُونَ "when you bring them home [in the evening] and when you drive them forth [to pasture in the morning]" (16:6), he (عليه السلام) said: "When they return from the pasture and when they go out to the pasture."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Zurarah, from one of the two [Imams] عليهما‌السلام, who said: "I asked him about the urine of horses, mules, and donkeys. He said: 'We dislike it.' I said: 'Is their meat not lawful?' He said: 'Has Allah not explained to you: وَالْأَنْعامَ خَلَقَها لَكُمْ فِيها دِفْءٌ وَمَنافِعُ وَمِنْها تَأْكُلُونَ "He created the cattle for you; in them there is warmth and [other] uses and you eat of them" (16:5), and He said regarding horses, mules, and donkeys: لِتَرْكَبُوها وَزِينَةً "for you to ride them, and for adornment" (16:8)? Thus He made eating from the cattle which Allah recounted in the Book, and He made horses, mules, and donkeys for riding—and their meat is not forbidden, but people have an aversion to it.'"

I say: The narrations regarding horses, mules, and donkeys vary, and the school of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام is the lawfulness of eating their meat despite it being disliked (كراهية).

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding the saying of the Exalted: وَيَخْلُقُ ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ "and He creates what you do not know" (16:8), he (عليه السلام) said: "The wonders that Allah has created in the land and the sea."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding the saying of the Exalted: وَعَلَى اللهِ قَصْدُ السَّبِيلِ وَمِنْها جائِرٌ "With Allah rests the guidance to the straight path, and some of them are devious" (16:9), Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn al-Anbari in al-Masahif extracted from Ali that he used to recite this verse as: "And among you is the devious one" (فمنكم جائر).

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Ismail ibn Abi Ziyad, from Ja'far ibn Muhammad, from his father, from his ancestors, from Ali عليه‌السلام, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: وَبِالنَّجْمِ هُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ "and by the stars they are guided" (16:16). He said: 'It is the Pole Star (الجدي) because it is a star that does not move, upon which the direction of the Qibla is built, and by it the people of the land and sea are guided.'"

I say: This is also narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام.
