His saying: وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ — "but most people do not know," (16:38) meaning they do not know that it is of the promise that is never broken and the decree (القضاء) that does not change, due to their turning away from the signs (الآيات) indicating it and revealing His promise — which are the creation of the heavens and the earth, the variation of people in injustice and tyranny versus justice and benevolence, and the obligation (التكليف) sent down in the Divine laws.

His saying, the Exalted: لِيُبَيِّنَ لَهُمُ الَّذِي يَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ وَلِيَعْلَمَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنَّهُمْ كانُوا كاذِبِينَ — "So that He may clarify for them that which they differ about, and that the faithless may know that they were liars." (16:39). The lam is for the end (الغاية) and purpose; meaning Allah resurrects those who die so that He may clarify for them, etc. The two ends are in reality one, for the second is a branch and a necessary consequence of the first. For the disbelievers will only know that they were liars in denying the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and return to Allah — by way of the clarification of the difference that appeared between them and the messengers regarding the affirmation and denial of the Return, and the manifestation of the Return to them visually (عياناً).

The clarification of what people differed about regarding the affairs of the Day of Resurrection has been repeated in His speech, along with similar expressions, with such frequency that it is correct to make the "clarification of difference" a defining characteristic of this Day, which "weighs heavy in the heavens and the earth." Upon this are branched the details Allah recounts in His speech regarding what occurs therein: the crossing of the Path (الصراط), the flying of the records (تطاير الكتب), the weighing of deeds (وزن الأعمال), the questioning (السؤال), the reckoning (الحساب), and the decisive judgment (فصل القضاء).

It is well known — especially from the context of the verses on the Resurrection — that the intended "difference" (الاختلاف) is not what exists among them by virtue of creation, such as male and female, tall and short, white and black; rather, it is what exists in the religion of truth regarding differences in belief or action. Allah has already clarified that for them in this worldly life in His revealed books and by the tongues of His prophets through every possible means, as He says a few verses later: وَما أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ إِلَّا لِتُبَيِّنَ لَهُمُ الَّذِي اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ — "We did not send down the Book to you except [for the purpose] that you may clarify for them what they differ about." (16:64).

From here, it becomes clear to the reflective person that the "clarification" (البيان) which the Exalted informs about and reserves for the Day of Resurrection is another type of manifestation and clarity, different from the clarification through wisdom, exhortation, and "disputing in the best manner" that proceeds from the Book and Prophethood in this world. It is nothing but the direct vision (العيان) into which no doubt or suspicion can creep, and with which no psychological passing thought of disagreement can occur, as indicated by the saying of the Exalted: لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ — "You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today." (50:22), and His saying: يَوْمَئِذٍ يُوَفِّيهِمُ اللهُ دِينَهُمُ الْحَقَّ وَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ الْمُبِينُ — "On that day Allah will pay them in full their due reward, and they will know that Allah is the Manifest Truth." (24:25).

On that day, they shall witness the realities of what they differed about regarding the true religious sciences and righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة), and the falsehood they clung to, and judgment shall be passed between them by the manifestation and unveiling of the truth.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما قَوْلُنا لِشَيْءٍ إِذا أَرَدْناهُ أَنْ نَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ — "All that We say to a thing, when We will it, is to say to it ‘Be!’ and it is." (16:40). This is similar to His saying in another place: إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ — "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is." (36:82). From this, it is known that the Exalted calls His command (أمره) a "saying" (قولاً), just as He calls His command and saying — in terms of its strength, firmness, emergence from ambiguity, and its being a willed decree and judgment — a "judgment" (حكماً) and a "decree" (قضاءً). The Exalted said: وَما أُغْنِي عَنْكُمْ مِنَ اللهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ — "I cannot avail you anything against Allah. Sovereignty belongs only to Allah." (12:67), and He said: وَقَضَيْنا إِلَيْهِ ذلِكَ الْأَمْرَ أَنَّ دابِرَ هؤُلاءِ مَقْطُوعٌ مُصْبِحِينَ — "And We revealed to him this decree: that the last remnant of these [people] would be cut off by dawn." (15:66), and He said: وَإِذا قَضى أَمْراً فَإِنَّما يَقُولُ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ — "When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is." (2:117). Just as He calls His specific saying a "word" (كلمة); the Exalted said: وَلَقَدْ سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنا لِعِبادِنَا الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنْصُورُونَ — "Certainly Our decree has gone forth for Our servants, the apostles, that they will indeed be the ones to be helped." (37:171-172), and He said: إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسى عِنْدَ اللهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ خَلَقَهُ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ قالَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ — "Indeed the case of Jesus with Allah is like the case of Adam: He created him from dust, then said to him, ‘Be,’ and he was." (3:59), then He said regarding Jesus (peace be upon him): وَكَلِمَتُهُ أَلْقاها إِلى مَرْيَمَ وَرُوحٌ مِنْهُ — "and His Word that He cast toward Mary, and a spirit from Him." (4:171).

The conclusion from all this is that His act of bringing into existence (إيجاده) — meaning what He emanates of existence upon things from Himself, which is in one sense the very existence of the existing thing — is His "command" (أمره) and His "saying" (قوله) according to what the Quran names it, and His "word" (كلمته). However, it appears that the "word" is the "saying" considered in its specificity and determination.

It becomes clear through this that His will (إرادته) and His decree (قضاؤه) are one, and that conceptually, it precedes the saying and the command. Thus, the Glorified wills a thing and decrees it, then commands it and says to it "Be," and it is. He has explained the reason why things do not fail to follow His command with the most subtle reasoning, saying: وَهُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِّ وَيَوْمَ يَقُولُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ قَوْلُهُ الْحَقُّ — "It is He who created the heavens and the earth with the truth. The day He says, ‘Be,’ it is. His word is the truth." (6:73). Thus, He informed that His saying is the established truth (الحق الثابت) by the reality of the meaning of "establishment" — that is, the very external entity (العين الخارجية) which is His act. Therefore, there is no meaning to assuming a failure to occur, or the occurrence of falsehood or futility within it. It is necessary that reality does not change from what it is; thus, He does not err or make a mistake in His act, His command is not rejected, His saying does not lie, and His promise is not broken.

It has also become clear from this verse and from His saying: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ — "The polytheists say, ‘Had Allah wished, we would not have worshipped anything besides Him,’" etc., that for Allah, the Glorified, there are two wills: a creative will (إرادة تكوين) — the ontological will that cannot be frustrated — from which the willed object never fails to emerge, and a legislative will (إرادة تشريع) — the will expressed through religious laws and commands — which can be disobeyed or obeyed. We shall complete this discussion to some extent, Allah willing.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, with its chain of narration to Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: قَدْ مَكَرَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ فَأَتَى اللهُ بُنْيانَهُمْ مِنَ الْقَواعِدِ ـ فَخَرَّ عَلَيْهِمُ السَّقْفُ مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ ـ وَأَتاهُمُ الْعَذابُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَشْعُرُونَ — "Those who were before them [also] plotted. Then Allah struck their edifice at its foundations, and the roof fell on them from above, and the punishment came at them from where they were not aware." (16:26). He said: "The house of their plotting; meaning they died and Allah kept them in the Fire — and it is a metaphor for the enemies of the Progeny of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)."

I say: Its apparent meaning is that His saying: فَأَتَى اللهُ بُنْيانَهُمْ — "Then Allah struck their edifice," etc., is an allusion to the nullification of their plotting.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: فَأَتَى اللهُ بُنْيانَهُمْ مِنَ الْقَواعِدِ — "Then Allah struck their edifice at its foundations." He said: "It was a house of treachery in which they would gather when they intended evil."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding the saying of the Exalted: قالَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ — "Those who were given knowledge will say..." (16:27). He (peace be upon him) said: "Those who were given knowledge are the Imams; they say to their enemies: 'Where are your partners and those whom you obeyed in the world?'" Then he said: "They are also those whom the angels take away while they are wronging themselves (ظالمي أنفسهم); فَأَلْقَوُا السَّلَمَ — they submit to the affliction that has befallen them, then they say: ما كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ مِنْ سُوءٍ — 'We were not doing any evil.' So Allah refuted them, saying: بَلى — 'Yes!'" etc.

In the Amali of the Shaykh, with its chain of narration to Abu Ishaq al-Hamdani, from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), in what he wrote to the people of Egypt, he said: "O servants of Allah, the closest a servant is to forgiveness and mercy is when he acts in His obedience and is sincere in his repentance. You must maintain godwariness (تقوى الله), for it gathers all good — and there is no good other than it — and through it, the good of this world and the good of the Hereafter are attained. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: وَقِيلَ لِلَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا ما ذا أَنْزَلَ رَبُّكُمْ ـ قالُوا خَيْراً لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا فِي هذِهِ الدُّنْيا حَسَنَةٌ ـ وَلَدارُ الْآخِرَةِ خَيْرٌ وَلَنِعْمَ دارُ الْمُتَّقِينَ — 'And the God-conscious are asked, ‘‘What has your Lord sent down?’’ They say, ‘‘Good!’’ For those who do good in this world there is a good reward, and the abode of the Hereafter is better, and the abode of the God-conscious is surely excellent.'" (16:30).

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Ibn Muskan, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: وَلَنِعْمَ دارُ الْمُتَّقِينَ — "and the abode of the God-conscious is surely excellent." He said: "The world."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying: الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ طَيِّبِينَ — "Those whom the angels take away while they are pure..." (16:32). He (peace be upon him) said: "They are the believers whose births were pure in the world."

I say: In view of its opposite, His saying: الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ ظالِمِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ — "Those whom the angels take away while they are wronging themselves," (16:28) this is not without some obscurity, and the narration is weak.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim recorded from al-Suddi, who said: "The Quraysh gathered and said: 'Muhammad is a man of sweet tongue; if a man speaks to him, he takes away his reason. So look for some of your counted nobles of known lineage, and send them on every road of Mecca at the head of every night or two. Whoever comes seeking him, turn him away from him.'"

So some of them went out on every road. When a man arrived as an envoy for his people to see what Muhammad says, he would stop with them. They would say to him: "I am so-and-so, son of so-and-so," and he would recognize him by his lineage. He would say: "I will inform you about Muhammad, so do not bother going to him; he is a liar, and none follow him in his affair except the foolish, the slaves, and those in whom there is no good. As for the elders of his people and their best, they have parted from him." So one of them would return; and that is His saying: وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ ما ذا أَنْزَلَ رَبُّكُمْ قالُوا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ — "When they are asked, ‘What has your Lord sent down?’ they say, ‘Myths of the ancients.’" (16:24).

But if the envoy was one for whom Allah had decreed guidance, and they said the same to him about Muhammad, he would say: "What a wretched envoy I am for my people if I have come all this way, only to return when I am but a day's journey away, before meeting this man and seeing what he says, so that I may bring my people a clear account of his affair." So he would enter Mecca and meet the believers, asking them: "What does Muhammad say?" They would say: "خَيْراً لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا فِي هذِهِ الدُّنْيا حَسَنَةٌ — 'Good! For those who do good in this world there is a good reward' — he says: wealth — 'وَلَدارُ الْآخِرَةِ خَيْرٌ — and the abode of the Hereafter is better,' which is Paradise."

I say: Consideration supports the story, but the interpretation of "good reward" (حسنة) at the end as "wealth" is not acceptable.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to Safwan ibn Yahya, who said: "I said to Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him): 'Inform me about the will (الإرادة) from Allah and from the creation.' He said: 'The will from the creation is the inner thought (الضمير) and what appears to them after that of action. As for Allah, the Exalted, His will is His act of bringing into existence (إحداثه) and nothing else; for He does not deliberate, nor does He intend (يهم), nor does He reflect. These attributes are negated from Him, as they are attributes of the creation. So the will of Allah is the act and nothing else; He says to it: "Be," and it is, without a word, without speech by a tongue, without intent, without reflection, and without a "how" (كيف) for that, just as there is no "how" for Him.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it hasan), Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in Shu'ab al-Iman (and the wording is his) recorded from Abu Dharr, from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, who said: "Allah says: 'O son of Adam, all of you are sinners except those whom I have pardoned, so seek My forgiveness and I shall forgive you. All of you are poor except those whom I have enriched, so ask Me and I shall give to you. All of you are astray except those whom I have guided, so ask Me for guidance and I shall guide you. Whoever seeks My forgiveness while knowing that I possess the power to forgive him, I forgive him and I do not mind.

Even if the first of you and the last of you, the living of you and the dead of you, the fresh of you and the dry of you, were to gather upon the heart of the most wretched single one among you, that would not decrease from My sovereignty even the weight of a gnat's wing. And even if the first of you and the last of you, the living of you and the dead of you, the fresh of you and the dry of you, were to gather upon the heart of the most god-conscious single one among you, they would not increase in My sovereignty even the weight of a gnat's wing. And even if the first of you and the last of you, the living of you and the dead of you, the fresh of you and the dry of you, were to ask Me until the request of every single one of them was exhausted, and I gave them what they asked for, that would not decrease what I possess even as much as the dip of a needle if one of you were to dip it into the sea.

That is because I am the Generous (جواد), the Majestic (ماجد), the One (واحد); My giving is speech and My punishment is speech. My command for a thing when I will it is only to say to it "Be," and it is.'"

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا۟ فِى ٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا ظُلِمُوا۟ لَنُبَوِّئَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً ۖ وَلَأَجْرُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ ۚ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ (٤١)
Those who migrate for the sake of Allah after they have been wronged, We will surely settle them in a good place in the world, and the reward of the Hereafter is surely greater, had they known (41)
ٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا۟ وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ (٤٢)
—those who are patient and who put their trust in their Lord. (42)
وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ إِلَّا رِجَالًا نُّوحِىٓ إِلَيْهِمْ ۚ فَسْـَٔلُوٓا۟ أَهْلَ ٱلذِّكْرِ إِن كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٤٣)
We did not send [any apostles] before you except as men to whom We revealed. Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know. (43)
بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَٱلزُّبُرِ ۗ وَأَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ٱلذِّكْرَ لِتُبَيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ مَا نُزِّلَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (٤٤)
[We sent them] with manifest proofs and scriptures. We have sent down the reminder to you so that you may clarify for the people that which has been sent down to them, so that they may reflect. (44)
أَفَأَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ مَكَرُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ أَن يَخْسِفَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ أَوْ يَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٤٥)
Do those who devise evil schemes feel secure that Allah will not make the earth swallow them, or the punishment will not overtake them whence they are not aware? (45)
أَوْ يَأْخُذَهُمْ فِى تَقَلُّبِهِمْ فَمَا هُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ (٤٦)
Or that He will not seize them in the midst of their bustle, whereupon they will not be able to frustrate [Him]? (46)
أَوْ يَأْخُذَهُمْ عَلَىٰ تَخَوُّفٍ فَإِنَّ رَبَّكُمْ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٤٧)
Or that He will not visit them with attrition? Indeed your Lord is most kind and merciful. (47)
أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ مَا خَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ مِن شَىْءٍ يَتَفَيَّؤُا۟ ظِلَـٰلُهُۥ عَنِ ٱلْيَمِينِ وَٱلشَّمَآئِلِ سُجَّدًا لِّلَّهِ وَهُمْ دَٰخِرُونَ (٤٨)
Have they not regarded that whatever thing Allah has created casts its shadow to the right and to the left, prostrating to Allah in utter humility? (48)
وَلِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مِن دَآبَّةٍ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ وَهُمْ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ (٤٩)
To Allah prostrates whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, including animals and angels, and they are not arrogant. (49)
يَخَافُونَ رَبَّهُم مِّن فَوْقِهِمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ مَا يُؤْمَرُونَ ۩ (٥٠)
They fear their Lord above them, and do what they are commanded. (50)
۞ وَقَالَ ٱللَّهُ لَا تَتَّخِذُوٓا۟ إِلَـٰهَيْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ ۖ إِنَّمَا هُوَ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۖ فَإِيَّـٰىَ فَٱرْهَبُونِ (٥١)
Allah has said, ‘Do not worship two gods. Indeed He is the One God, so be in awe of Me [alone].’ (51)
وَلَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَهُ ٱلدِّينُ وَاصِبًا ۚ أَفَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ تَتَّقُونَ (٥٢)
To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth, and to Him belongs the enduring religion. Will you, then, be wary of other than Allah? (52)
وَمَا بِكُم مِّن نِّعْمَةٍ فَمِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ ثُمَّ إِذَا مَسَّكُمُ ٱلضُّرُّ فَإِلَيْهِ تَجْـَٔرُونَ (٥٣)
Whatever blessing you have is from Allah, and when a distress befalls you, you make entreaties to Him. (53)
ثُمَّ إِذَا كَشَفَ ٱلضُّرَّ عَنكُمْ إِذَا فَرِيقٌ مِّنكُم بِرَبِّهِمْ يُشْرِكُونَ (٥٤)
Then, when He removes the distress from you—behold, a part of them ascribe partners to their Lord, (54)
لِيَكْفُرُوا۟ بِمَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمْ ۚ فَتَمَتَّعُوا۟ ۖ فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (٥٥)
being unthankful for what We have given them. So let them enjoy. Soon they shall know! (55)
وَيَجْعَلُونَ لِمَا لَا يَعْلَمُونَ نَصِيبًا مِّمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ ۗ تَٱللَّهِ لَتُسْـَٔلُنَّ عَمَّا كُنتُمْ تَفْتَرُونَ (٥٦)
They assign a share in what We have provided them to what they do not know. By Allah, you will surely be questioned concerning what you used to fabricate. (56)
وَيَجْعَلُونَ لِلَّهِ ٱلْبَنَـٰتِ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ ۙ وَلَهُم مَّا يَشْتَهُونَ (٥٧)
And they attribute daughters to Allah—immaculate is He—while they will have what they desire! (57)
وَإِذَا بُشِّرَ أَحَدُهُم بِٱلْأُنثَىٰ ظَلَّ وَجْهُهُۥ مُسْوَدًّا وَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ (٥٨)
When one of them is brought the news of a female [newborn], his face becomes darkened, and he chokes with suppressed agony. (58)
يَتَوَٰرَىٰ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ مِن سُوٓءِ مَا بُشِّرَ بِهِۦٓ ۚ أَيُمْسِكُهُۥ عَلَىٰ هُونٍ أَمْ يَدُسُّهُۥ فِى ٱلتُّرَابِ ۗ أَلَا سَآءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ (٥٩)
He hides from the people out of distress at the news he has been brought: shall he retain it in humiliation, or bury it in the ground! Behold! Evil is the judgement that they make. (59)
لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مَثَلُ ٱلسَّوْءِ ۖ وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْمَثَلُ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٦٠)
There is an evil description for those who do not believe in the Hereafter, and the loftiest description belongs to Allah, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (60)
وَلَوْ يُؤَاخِذُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلنَّاسَ بِظُلْمِهِم مَّا تَرَكَ عَلَيْهَا مِن دَآبَّةٍ وَلَـٰكِن يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۖ فَإِذَا جَآءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لَا يَسْتَـْٔخِرُونَ سَاعَةً ۖ وَلَا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ (٦١)
Were Allah to take mankind to task for their wrongdoing, He would not leave any living being upon it. But He respites them until a specified time; so when their time comes they shall not defer it by a single hour nor shall they advance it. (61)
وَيَجْعَلُونَ لِلَّهِ مَا يَكْرَهُونَ وَتَصِفُ أَلْسِنَتُهُمُ ٱلْكَذِبَ أَنَّ لَهُمُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۖ لَا جَرَمَ أَنَّ لَهُمُ ٱلنَّارَ وَأَنَّهُم مُّفْرَطُونَ (٦٢)
They attribute to Allah what they dislike [for themselves], and their tongues assert the lie that the best of rewards [in the Hereafter] will be theirs. Undoubtedly, the Fire shall be their lot and they will be foremost [in entering it]. (62)
تَٱللَّهِ لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أُمَمٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ فَهُوَ وَلِيُّهُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٦٣)
By Allah, We have certainly sent [apostles] to nations before you. But Satan made their deeds seem decorous to them. So he is their master today and there is a painful punishment for them. (63)
وَمَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ إِلَّا لِتُبَيِّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ ۙ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٦٤)
We did not send down the Book to you except [for the purpose] that you may clarify for them what they differ about, and as a guidance and mercy for a people who have faith. (64)

(Commentary)

The first two verses mention the migration (الهجرة) and give the migrants for the sake of Allah a fair promise in this world and the Hereafter. The remaining verses follow the discourse on their polytheism (شركهم) regarding Allah and their legislating without Allah’s permission. In terms of meaning, they provide a detailed response to the polytheists' claim that the prophetic call to abandon the worship of idols and the prohibition of what Allah has not forbidden is an impossibility, as alluded to in His saying: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا (And the polytheists said...), etc.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا فِي اللهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما ظُلِمُوا لَنُبَوِّئَنَّهُمْ فِي الدُّنْيا حَسَنَةً وَلَأَجْرُ الْآخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ — Those who migrate for the sake of Allah after they have been wronged, We will surely settle them in a good place in the world, and the reward of the Hereafter is surely greater, had they known. (16:41). This is a beautiful promise to the migrants (المهاجرين). There were two migrations of the believers from Mecca: one to Abyssinia, undertaken by a group of believers in the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with permission from Allah and His Messenger, where they stayed for a time in security and comfort from the harm, torture, and persecution (الفتنة) of the Meccan polytheists.

The second was their migration from Mecca to Medina after the migration of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). It appears that the migration intended in the verse is the second one, as the context of the two verses is more consistent with it than the first, which is evident.

His saying: فِي اللهِ (for the sake of Allah) is connected to "migrated" (هاجروا). The meaning of the migration being "in Allah" is that the seeking of His pleasure encompasses them in their migration, such that they do not depart from it for any other purpose. This is similar to saying: "He traveled in search of knowledge" or "He went out in search of a livelihood," meaning he has no goal but the search for knowledge and no desire but the search for a livelihood. The context suggests that His saying: مِنْ بَعْدِ ما ظُلِمُوا (after they have been wronged) is also semantically restricted by this; the implied meaning is: "those who migrated for the sake of Allah after they were wronged for His sake." It was omitted for brevity, and it sufficed as a qualification for the migration because it was the point of trial (محل الابتلاء), so specifying it by clarifying the state is more appropriate.

His saying: لَنُبَوِّئَنَّهُمْ فِي الدُّنْيا حَسَنَةً (We will surely settle them in a good place in the world): It is said this means a "good land" in exchange for the homeland they left, such as Mecca and its surroundings, evidenced by His saying: لَنُبَوِّئَنَّهُمْ, which comes from "settling someone in a place" (بوأت له مكانا), meaning I leveled it and established him therein.

It is also said it means a "good state" of victory, triumph, and the like; thus, His saying: لَنُبَوِّئَنَّهُمْ etc., would be a metaphorical expression (استعارة بالكناية).

The two views ultimately converge, for they only migrated to establish a wholesome Islamic society (مجتمع إسلامي) in which none but Allah is worshipped and nothing but justice and benevolence (الإحسان) governs, or to enter a society of this nature. Thus, if they returned to a good end in their migration or were promised a good end, it would be this righteous society. Even if they praised the land to which they migrated, their praise would be for the Islamic society established therein, not for its water or air. Therefore, the "good thing" (الحسنة) Allah promises them in this world is this society, whether "good" refers to the land or the ultimate goal.

His saying: وَلَأَجْرُ الْآخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ (and the reward of the Hereafter is surely greater, had they known) completes the promise and indicates that the reward of the Hereafter is superior to this worldly reward, had they known what Allah has prepared for them therein of blessings. For therein is felicity without wretchedness, eternity (الخلود) without annihilation (الفناء), pleasure unmixed with pain, and proximity to the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين).

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا وَعَلى رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ — Those who are patient and put their trust in their Lord. (16:42). It is not unlikely to infer from the context of the two verses that the primary focus is on promising the migrants for the sake of Allah a goodly promise in this world and the Hereafter, without regard to reporting the actual occurrence of the migration before the time of the discourse. Thus, the speech would be in the sense of a condition: "Whoever migrates for the sake of Allah, for him is such and such." The focus in His saying: الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا (those who are patient) etc., would be on describing the migrants with patience (الصبر) and trust (التوكل) — reliance upon Allah — without regard to what they had already manifested of these traits during their stay in their homelands among the polytheists in the face of their harm and persecution.

The focus on these descriptions is because both attributes are instrumental in reaching the "good end" promised to them. For if they had not been patient (صبروا) through the bitterness of struggle (الجهاد) and had shown anxiety when calamities struck, and if they had not been supported by trust in Allah (التوكل على الله) but instead relied on their own weak selves, they would have been overwhelmed. They would not have secured a settlement, and the enemy, persistent in his hostility, would have scattered them, causing the righteous society they established in their place of migration to vanish in this world. As for the matter of the Hereafter, its ruin through the ruin or dissolution of the society is even clearer.

However, if the intent was to promise the migrants who had already migrated before the revelation of the verse, to comfort their souls and solace them for being driven from their homes and wealth and for enduring trials and tribulations, then His saying: الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا وَعَلى رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ would be a praise for them for what they manifested during their stay in Mecca and elsewhere of patience for the sake of Allah against the harm of the polytheists and trust in Allah regarding their resolve to submit (الإسلام) to Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ إِلَّا رِجالاً نُوحِي إِلَيْهِمْ فَسْئَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ — We did not send [any apostles] before you except as men to whom We revealed. Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know. (16:43). This is a second return to explaining the nature of sending messengers (الرسل) and the revelation of books (الكتب), so that it becomes clear to the polytheists that the religious call was nothing but an ordinary call by men from among humanity to whom revelation (يوحى إليهم) is given, inviting people to what contains their welfare in this world and the next.

It clarifies that no messenger ever claimed, nor was it claimed in any book of divine laws (شرائع), that the religious call is a manifestation of an overwhelming unseen power (القدرة الغيبية) that conquers everything, or a creative will (الإرادة التكوينية) intended to demolish the existing order, violate the sunnah of free will (الاختيار), and nullify it, such that one of them might say: لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ (Had Allah wished, we would not have worshipped anything besides Him), etc.

Accordingly, His saying, the Glorified: وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ إِلَّا رِجالاً نُوحِي إِلَيْهِمْ is intended to restrict the messengership (الرسالة) to ordinary human beings—men to whom revelation is given—in opposition to the polytheists' claim that if it were true, it would necessitate a violation of the natural order and the nullification of free will and capacity (الاستطاعة).

From this, it becomes clear that the view mentioned by many—that the verse is intended to refute the polytheists of the Quraysh who claimed that a human is not fit for messengership and that it should be the domain of angels—is not entirely sound. They argue the verse informs that the divine sunnah (السنة الإلهية) proceeded according to what wisdom (الحكمة) necessitated: that none be sent for the religious call except men from among humanity to whom He reveals knowledge, commands, and prohibitions.

This is because the context of the verses does not support that; there was no prior mention of their saying that, nor of their proposal that angels be sent as messengers, for the speech to be directed toward that.

Rather, what preceded was the polytheists' saying: لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ etc., which was aimed at proving the impossibility of prophethood (النبوة), not that it was the domain of angels.

Some have used this verse as evidence that Allah, the Glorified, never sent a child or a woman. An objection was raised regarding the prophethood of Jesus (عليه‌السلام) in the cradle, to which it was replied that prophethood (النبوة) is more general than messengership (الرسالة), and what Jesus affirmed for himself in his saying: إِنِّي عَبْدُ اللهِ آتانِيَ الْكِتابَ وَجَعَلَنِي نَبِيًّا — "Indeed I am a servant of Allah! He has given me the Book and made me a prophet" (19:30) was prophethood, not messengership.

The flaw in this is that the aforementioned deduction from the verse relies on His saying: وَما أَرْسَلْنا (We did not send). This verb, as it relates to the "messenger" (الرسول) in the Quran, also relates to the "prophet" (النبي) who is not a messenger. He, the Exalted, said: وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْ رَسُولٍ وَلا نَبِيٍ — "We did not send any apostle or prophet before you..." (22:52). If the aforementioned deduction were valid, it would indicate the exclusion of children and women from both messengership and prophethood altogether. Yet Allah has narrated from Jesus (عليه‌السلام) his saying: إِنِّي عَبْدُ اللهِ آتانِيَ الْكِتابَ وَجَعَلَنِي نَبِيًّا (19:30), and said regarding John (عليه‌السلام): وَآتَيْناهُ الْحُكْمَ صَبِيًّا — "and We gave him judgement while he was still a child" (19:12).

The truth is that the verse: وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ إِلَّا رِجالاً is in the position of explaining that the messengers were men from among ordinary humanity, without specific focus on whether they were mature adults at the very moment they were first sent. The purpose is that Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and John (عليه‌السلام)—who were messengers—were men to whom revelation was given; they were not individuals equipped with an overwhelming unseen power or a divine creative will.

Similar to this verse is His saying, the Exalted, in another place: وَما أَرْسَلْنا قَبْلِكَ إِلَّا رِجالاً نُوحِي إِلَيْهِمْ فَسْئَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ وَما جَعَلْناهُمْ جَسَداً لا يَأْكُلُونَ الطَّعامَ وَما كانُوا خالِدِينَ — "We did not send [any apostles] before you except as men to whom We revealed. Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know. We did not appoint them as bodies that did not eat food, nor were they immortal." (21:7-8).

His saying: فَسْئَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ (Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know): It appears to be an address to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and his people. While the address in the preceding speech was specifically to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), the meaning is directed to everyone. It is a generalization of the address so that each of the addressees may take his path: he who does not know this, like some of the polytheists, should refer to the People of the Reminder (أهل الذكر) and ask them; and he who knows it, like the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers in him, has no need for such reference or questioning.

It is said that the address in the verse is to the polytheists, for they are the deniers, so let them refer and ask. The flaw in this is that it necessitates the sentence being a shift (التفات) from addressing an individual to addressing a group, and there is no apparent subtle point (نكتة) to justify that, and Allah knows best.

"Reminder" (الذكر) is the preservation of the meaning of a thing or its summoning to mind, and it is also said of that by which it is preserved or summoned. Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "Reminder (الذكر) is sometimes said to mean a state of the soul by which a person can preserve the knowledge he acquires, and it is like 'preservation' (الحفظ), except that preservation is said in consideration of its being secured, while reminder is said in consideration of its being summoned. At other times, it is said for the presence of a thing in the heart or speech. Therefore, it is said: 'Reminder is of two types: reminder by the heart and reminder by the tongue.' Each of these is of two kinds: reminder after forgetting, and reminder not after forgetting but from continuous preservation." This is the end of the relevant portion.

It appears that the primary meaning is that which pertains to the heart, and the word is only called "reminder" (ذكراً) in consideration of its conveying the meaning and casting it into the mind. Upon this meaning, its usage in the Quran proceeded, except that its subject therein is the remembrance of Allah (تعالى). Thus, when "reminder" (الذكر) is used absolutely therein without being restricted by anything, it is His remembrance.

By this same consideration, the Quran, the prophetic revelation, and the books sent down to the prophets are called "Reminder" (ذكراً). The verses on this are many; there is no need to cite them here. Indeed, Allah, the Glorified, has called the Quran "Reminder" in the following verse.

Thus, the Noble Quran is a "Reminder," just as the Book of Noah, the Scrolls of Abraham, the Torah of Moses, the Psalms of David, and the Gospel of Jesus (عليه‌السلام)—which are the heavenly books mentioned in the Quran—are all "Reminder." Their people, who engage with them and believe in them, are the "People of the Reminder" (أهل الذكر).

Since the people of a thing and those specialized in it are most knowledgeable of its state and most insightful regarding its reports, it is incumbent upon whoever wishes to gain insight into its matter to refer to its people. The people of the heavenly books, who are dedicated to studying them, learning them, and acting upon their laws, are the experts in them and those knowledgeable of the reports of the prophets who brought them. Therefore, whoever wishes to become informed about any of their matters must refer to them and ask them.

However, since the polytheists addressed by such sayings as فَسْئَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ did not submit to the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prophethood, did not believe him in his claim, and mocked the Quran, the possessor of the Reminder—as He mentions in His saying: وَقالُوا يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِي نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ إِنَّكَ لَمَجْنُونٌ — "They say, ‘O you to whom the Reminder has been sent down, you are indeed crazy!’" (15:6)—His saying فَسْئَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ, according to the specific context, could only apply to the People of the Torah. This is especially so because they were enemies of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), rejecting his prophethood, and the souls of the polytheists were comfortable with them for that reason. Indeed, they said regarding the polytheists: هؤُلاءِ أَهْدى مِنَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا سَبِيلاً — "These are better guided on the way than the faithful." (4:51).

Some said: What is meant by the "People of the Reminder" are those with knowledge of the reports of past nations, whether they be believers or disbelievers. Knowledge was called "reminder" because knowledge of the signified (المدلول) is usually obtained from remembering the evidence (الدليل); thus, it is of the category of naming the effect (المسبب) by the name of the cause (السبب).

The flaw in this is that it is a metaphorical usage (مجاز) without a necessitating context to adopt it, especially since the established usage of "Reminder" (الذكر) in the Noble Quran in various places is other than this meaning.

Others said: What is meant by the "People of the Reminder" are the People of the Quran, because Allah called it a "Reminder," and its people are the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), his companions, and the elite of the believers. The flaw in this is that while the Quran is undoubtedly a "Reminder" and its people are its people, intending that specifically from the verse does not suit the completion of the argument. For those [polytheists] were not submissive to the prophethood of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), so how could they accept it from his followers among the believers?

In any case, the verse is a guidance toward a general rational principle (أصل عام عقلائي), which is the obligation of the ignorant to refer to the expert (أهل الخبرة). The ruling it contains is not a devotional ruling (حكم تعبدي), nor is the command to the ignorant to ask the scholar, or to ask specifically the "People of the Reminder," a legislative masterly command (أمر مولوي تشريعي); this is evident.

His saying, the Exalted: بِالْبَيِّناتِ وَالزُّبُرِ — with manifest proofs and scriptures (16:44). This is connected to an implied word indicated by what was in the previous verse: وَما أَرْسَلْنا (We did not send), meaning: "We sent them with manifest proofs (البينات) and scriptures (الزبر)." These are the clear signs (الآيات) indicating their messengership and the books sent down to them.

This is because the focus in the previous verse was only on explaining that the messengers were humans according to the norm. Thus, it is as if when that was mentioned, it occurred to the listener's mind: "With what were they sent?" So it was answered: "With manifest proofs and scriptures." As for the manifest proofs, they were to prove their messengership; as for the scriptures, they were to preserve their teachings.

It is said that it is connected to His saying: وَما أَرْسَلْنا, meaning: "We did not send with manifest proofs and scriptures except men to whom We revealed." There is no harm in this in itself, but it misses the aforementioned subtle point.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الذِّكْرَ لِتُبَيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ ما نُزِّلَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ — We have sent down the Reminder to you so that you may clarify for the people what has been sent down to them, and so that they may reflect. (16:44). There is no doubt that the sending down (تنزيل) of the Book to the people and the sending down (إنزال) of the Reminder to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) are one, in the sense that its being sent down to the people is its being sent down to him so that they may take it and apply it, as He, the Exalted, said: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ قَدْ جاءَكُمْ بُرْهانٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكُمْ نُوراً مُبِيناً — "O mankind! Certainly a proof has come to you from your Lord, and We have sent down to you a manifest light." (4:174), and He said: لَقَدْ أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكُمْ كِتاباً فِيهِ ذِكْرُكُمْ أَفَلا تَعْقِلُونَ — "We have certainly sent down to you a Book in which is your Reminder. Do you not exercise your reason?" (21:10).

Thus, the sum of the meaning is that the intent of the descent of this Reminder is for all of humanity, and that you and the people are equal in that. We only chose you to direct the discourse and deliver the word, not to invest you with an unseen power or a divine creative will (إرادة تكوينية إلهية) making you a dominator over them and over everything. Rather, it is for two purposes:

First: That you clarify (تبين) for the people what was sent down gradually to them, because divine knowledge (المعارف الإلهية) is not attained by people without a medium. Thus, one of them must be sent for clarification and teaching. This is the purpose of messengership: revelation (الوحي) descends to him, he carries it, and then he is commanded to convey it, teach it, and clarify it.

Second: In the hope that they reflect (يتفكرون) upon you, so they may see that what you have brought is the truth from Allah. For the circumstances surrounding you and the events and states that occurred to you throughout your life—from being an orphan, the obscurity of your name, the deprivation of learning and writing, the lack of a righteous mentor, poverty, and being confined among an ignorant, lowly people devoid of the advantages of civilization and human virtues—were all definitive reasons that you should never taste a drop from the spring of perfection, nor grasp a handle of the cords of felicity. Yet Allah, the Glorified, sent down to you a Reminder with which you challenge the jinn and humans, a guardian (مهيمنا) over all other heavenly books, a clarification (تبيانا) for all things, a guidance, a mercy, a proof, and a manifest light.

Thus, reflecting upon you is an excellent guide leading to the fact that you had no hand in what you brought, nor do you have any part in the matter; rather, Allah sent it down with His knowledge and supported you for that with His power, without any of the ordinary causes interfering.

This is what the noble verse provides, considering its context and the context of what preceded it. Its summary is that His saying: لِتُبَيِّنَ (so that you may clarify) etc., is the goal of the sending down (الإنزال), not for its own sake, but in terms of its relation to the person of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and that the omitted object of "they reflect" (يتفكرون) is something like "upon you," not "upon the Reminder."

However, the commentators mentioned that His saying: لِتُبَيِّنَ is the goal of the sending down, and that what is meant by reflection is reflecting upon the Reminder so that it may be known thereby that it is the truth. The meaning of the verse according to this is: "We sent down to you the Reminder," i.e., the Quran, so that you may clarify to all people what was sent down to them in that Reminder of the principles of knowledge, rulings, laws, the states of past nations, and the sunnah of Allah that occurred among them; and in the hope that they reflect upon the Reminder and thus be guided to the fact that it is the truth from Allah, or reflect upon what you clarify to them.

You are well aware that this necessitates, firstly, something akin to "attaining what is already attained" (تحصيل الحاصل) in sending it down to him to clarify to them what was sent down to them, while the sending down is one. There is no defense for this except to change the structure to something like: "We sent down to you the Reminder so that you may clarify it to them."

Secondly: His saying: إِلَيْكَ (to you) becomes redundant and unnecessary, especially in view of His saying: وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ. This is because the goal of the sending down is clarification, and there is no effect in that being sent down to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) specifically rather than someone else. Likewise, reflecting upon the Reminder is a hoped-for goal for knowing it is the truth from Allah, without regard to whom it was sent down. This necessitates His saying إِلَيْكَ being an addition in the speech for which there is no need.

Thirdly: The disconnection of the verse in its context from the context of the verse preceding it: وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ إِلَّا رِجالاً نُوحِي إِلَيْهِمْ and the verses before it.

There is another possibility by which some of the previous problems might be averted: that what is meant by the "Reminder" sent down is the wording of the Noble Quran, and by "what was sent down to them" the meanings of the rulings, laws, and others. Thus, His saying: لِتُبَيِّنَ would be the goal of the sending down, and His saying: وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ would be conjoined to an implied word and be the goal of the clarification, not the sending down. This is contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse, so you must excel in reflecting upon it.

Among the subtle expressions in the verse is His saying: وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ (We sent down to you) and ما نُزِّلَ إِلَيْهِمْ (what was sent down to them), differentiating the two verbs by using the if'al form (أنزلنا) which indicates considering the whole and the sudden, and the taf'il form (نُزِّلَ) which indicates considering the gradual. Perhaps the reason for this is that the focus in His saying وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ is on the relation of the sending down to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) only, without regard to the specific nature of the sending down itself. Therefore, the Reminder was taken as a single whole, and its descent from Him (تعالى) was expressed with inzal (إنزال).

As for the people, what they have of that is the taking, learning, and acting, which was gradual. Therefore, that was intended, and its descent to them was expressed with tanzil (تنزيل).

In the verse is evidence for the authority (حجية) of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) word in clarifying the Quranic verses. As for what some have mentioned—that this applies only to what is other than the explicit (النص) and the apparent (الظاهر) among the ambiguous verses (المتشابهات), or what relates to the secrets of Allah's speech and its interpretation (التأويل)—it is something that should not be listened to.

This applies to his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) own clarification, and to it is joined the clarification of his Household (أهل بيته) due to the mutawatir Hadith al-Thaqalayn and others. As for the rest of the Ummah, whether companions, successors (التابعين), or scholars, there is no authority for their clarification, as the verse does not encompass them and there is no relied-upon text giving authority to their clarification absolutely.

As for His saying, the Exalted: فَسْئَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ, it has already been mentioned that it is a guidance toward the rational ruling of the obligation of the ignorant to refer to the scholar, without the ruling being restricted to one group over another.

All of this pertains to their own clarification received through direct hearing. As for the report narrating it: that which is a mutawatir clarification or surrounded by definitive context and what is joined to it is an authority because it is their clarification. As for what is contrary to the Book, or not contrary but not mutawatir nor surrounded by context, there is no authority in it, due to its not being a clarification in the first case, and the lack of establishing its being a clarification in the second. The detail belongs elsewhere.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَأَمِنَ الَّذِينَ مَكَرُوا السَّيِّئاتِ أَنْ يَخْسِفَ اللهُ بِهِمُ الْأَرْضَ أَوْ يَأْتِيَهُمُ الْعَذابُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَشْعُرُونَ — Do those who devise evil schemes feel secure that Allah will not cause the earth to swallow them, or that the punishment will not come upon them from where they do not perceive? (16:45). This verse and the two verses after it are a warning and a threat to the polytheists—those who worship other than Allah, the Glorified, and legislate for themselves ways (سننا) they follow in life. Whatever deeds they perform, acting independently therein and turning away from the laws of Allah sent down through prophethood, based on the invalid arguments they invented for themselves, are all "evil deeds" (سيئات). Whatever they engage in throughout their lives of movement or stillness, taking or giving, acting or abstaining, while they are in their state of arrogance and delusion, are all sins they commit as a "scheme" (مكراً) against Allah their Lord and against His messengers who call to the adoption of Allah's religion and adherence to His path.

Thus, His saying: السَّيِّئاتِ (evil deeds) is the object of مَكَرُوا (they devised schemes), by including the meaning of "they performed," i.e., they performed evil deeds while scheming. What some have suggested—that "evil deeds" is an adjective standing in for the absolute object (المفعول المطلق), with the implied meaning being "they scheme evil schemes"—is far from the context.

In short, the speech is to threaten the polytheists and warn them of divine punishment, and the polytheists of Mecca are included therein. The speech is branched from what preceded, as indicated by His saying: أَفَأَمِنَ with the branching fa (فاء التفريع).

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: If the manifest signs have indicated that Allah is their Lord, with no partner in His Lordship, and that messengership is not an impossible matter but a call to what contains the welfare of their livelihood and their return, and the good of their world and their Hereafter, brought by men who are like them whom Allah sends and to whom He reveals what the call comprises—then do these who turn away from that and scheme against Allah and His messengers by clinging to these flimsy arguments to pave the way for abandoning Allah's religion and legislating what accords with their desires, and who perform evil deeds, do they feel secure that Allah will not cause the earth to swallow them or that the punishment will not come upon them while they do not perceive? That is, it surprises them without them being alerted to its approach before it descends.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ يَأْخُذَهُمْ فِي تَقَلُّبِهِمْ فَما هُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ — or that He will not seize them in the midst of their activities, for they cannot frustrate [Him]? (16:46). The subject is Allah, the Glorified, and the attribution of "seizing" (الأخذ) to Him is frequent in the Quran. It is also said the pronoun refers to the punishment. "Activity" (التقلب) is the transformation from one state to another, and what is meant is the polytheists' shifting in their evil purposes and deeds, and their transition from one blessing to another among the blessings of the worldly life. He, the Exalted, said: لا يَغُرَّنَّكَ تَقَلُّبُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي الْبِلادِ مَتاعٌ قَلِيلٌ ثُمَّ مَأْواهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَبِئْسَ الْمِهادُ — "Do not be deceived by the activities of the faithless in the towns. It is a brief enjoyment; then their abode shall be hell, and it is an evil resting place." (3:196-197).

Thus, what is meant by "seizing them in the midst of their activities" is that He seizes them in the very midst of the evil deeds they are engaged in as a scheme against Allah and His messengers with punishment. Or the meaning is that He punishes them with the very things they are active in, so the blessing turns into a bane; this is more appropriate in view of His saying: فَما هُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ.

His saying: فَما هُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ (for they cannot frustrate [Him]) is in the position of justifying their being seized in their activities and evil schemes; i.e., because they cannot frustrate Allah in what He intended by overcoming Him or by fleeing from His judgment. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ يَأْخُذَهُمْ عَلى تَخَوُّفٍ فَإِنَّ رَبَّكُمْ لَرَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ — or that He will not seize them by a gradual wasting? Indeed your Lord is most kind and merciful. (16:47). "Fear" (التخوف) is the taking hold of fear in the soul and its settling therein. Thus, "seizing upon fear" is the punishment based on apprehension, such that they perceive the punishment and thus guard against it and beware of it as much as they can through repentance, regret, and the like. Thus, "seizing upon fear" would be the opposite of the punishment coming from where they do not perceive.

It is sometimes said that "seizing upon fear" is the punishment by that which is feared without destruction, such as earthquakes, floods, and others.

It is also said that the meaning of takhawwuf is "diminishment" (التنقص), such that Allah seizes them by the diminishment of blessings one after another gradually, like the removal of security, then rains, then low prices, then health, and so on.

His saying: فَإِنَّ رَبَّكُمْ لَرَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ (Indeed your Lord is most kind and merciful) is in the position of justification; i.e., He seizes them upon fear and descends in their punishment to this type of punishment which is the lightest of the enumerated types because He is Kind and Merciful. In the expression رَبَّكُمْ (your Lord) is an allusion to that. Its being in the position of justification regarding the first two views is clear; as for the third, it is because seizing by diminishment is not devoid of a respite and opportunity in which whoever is alerted may take heed through repentance or otherwise.

The discourse in enumerating the mentioned types of punishment is not intended to be exhaustive, as some have noted, but rather an accounting of some of its types.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا إِلى ما خَلَقَ اللهُ مِنْ شَيْءٍ يَتَفَيَّؤُا ظِلالُهُ عَنِ الْيَمِينِ وَالشَّمائِلِ سُجَّداً لِلَّهِ وَهُمْ داخِرُونَ — Have they not regarded the things Allah has created? Their shadows fall to the right and the left, prostrating to Allah in utter humility. (16:48). What is meant by "regarding" (الرؤية) is visual sight and sensory looking at corporeal things, because what is required is to draw attention to bodies that possess shadows.

Tafayyu' (التفيؤ) is from fay' (الفيء), which is the shadow when it returns. Therefore, it is said that "shadow" (الظل) is what is in the first part of the day until the sun's zenith, and fay' is what occurs after the zenith until the end of the day. It appears that "shadow" is more general than fay', as previously mentioned, and the verse supports this. Thus, tafayyu' is the return of the shadow after its disappearance.

Shama'il (الشمائل) is the plural of shimal (north/left), which is the opposite of the right. It is pluralized in consideration of taking every assumed direction behind the thing and to its left as a separate "left" side. Thus, they are "lefts" (شمائل) that correspond to the "right," just as every thing was considered to have "shadows" (أظلال) by this consideration—taking the shadow in relation to every direction of the right and the lefts as a shadow other than it in relation to another direction—not because the mentioned "thing" is plural in meaning even if it is singular in wording. Dukhur (الدخور) is submission and abasement.

The fact that the intended "regarding" is visual sight is a context that what is meant by "the things Allah has created" (ما خلق الله من شيء)—where "of a thing" (من شيء) is an explanation of "what Allah has created"—is visible things. What follows it of the discourse on the shifting of shadows restricts them to dense bodies that have shadows, such as mountains, trees, buildings, and bodies standing on the earth. Thus, the objection does not arise that "what Allah has created," especially after its explanation by "thing," is not necessarily accompanied by a shadow, such as luminous celestial bodies, transparent bodies, and the accidents of bodies.

To repel this objection, some have made His saying: يَتَفَيَّؤُا ظِلالُهُ etc., a description of "thing" so that the explanation is specific to created things that have afya' and shadows, and this is not without merit.

The verse guides the polytheists—who are deniers of monotheism (التوحيد) and prophethood (النبوة)—to look at the state of bodies that have shadows rotating from their right and their lefts. For they represent their prostration (سجودها) to Allah, their submission to Him, and their abasement before His greatness and majesty. Likewise is the prostration of whatever is in the heavens and the earth of creatures (دابة) and the angels.

They are all prostrating to Allah alone due to their essential compliance with His command, representing submission and abasement through this existential rite and creative worship (العبادة التكوينية).

This is among the clearest proofs that in the world there is one worshipped God, who is Allah, the Glorified, and that it is His right that one prostrate to Him and submit to His command. This is the monotheism and prophethood they deny. For is monotheism anything but the acknowledgment that He, the Glorified, is the God to whom submission is obligatory and to whom one must turn with humility and abasement? And is the religion (الدين) comprised in the call of the prophets and messengers anything but submission to Allah, the Glorified, and compliance with His command in what He willed? So why do they deny that? While they see and know that what is on the earth of the shadows of dense bodies prostrates to Him, and what is in the heavens and the earth of angels and essences are prostrating to Him, complying with His command—even the lords of their idols whom they take as gods besides Allah, for they are either from the angels, the jinn, or the perfected humans, and they are all humble (داخرون) before Him, complying with His command.

So the meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا (Have they not regarded), these polytheists who deny the monotheism of Lordship and the call of prophethood, have they not looked إِلى ما خَلَقَ اللهُ مِنْ شَيْءٍ (at the things Allah has created) of these bodies standing upon the surface of the earth, whether mountain, building, tree, or any upright body, يَتَفَيَّؤُا (whose shadows fall and return and rotate) ظِلالُهُ عَنِ الْيَمِينِ وَالشَّمائِلِ سُجَّداً لِلَّهِ (to the right and the left, prostrating to Allah), falling upon the earth in humility and worship to Him, the Glorified, وَهُمْ داخِرُونَ (while they are humble), submissive and abased.

The discussion on the meaning of the prostration of shadows has already preceded under His saying, the Exalted: وَظِلالُهُمْ بِالْغُدُوِّ وَالْآصالِ (13:15) in the eleventh volume of the book.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ مِنْ دابَّةٍ وَالْمَلائِكَةُ — To Allah prostrates whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth of creatures, and the angels [as well] (16:49) until the end of the two verses. The previous verse mentioned the prostration of shadows, which is a witnessed meaning in them representing the meaning of prostration to Allah. This verse mentions the prostration of whatever is in the heavens and the earth of creatures (دابة)—and a "creature" (الدابة) is that which creeps and moves by transferring from place to place—with the reality of prostration (حقيقة السجود), which is the ultimate submission and humility before greatness and majesty. For the form of prostration, which is the human's falling and landing on his face on the earth, is only considered worship if it is intended to represent this meaning; thus, the reality of prostration is the aforementioned submission.

Included in the generality of "creature" is the human, and likewise the jinn, because He, the Glorified, describes them in His speech with that which implies they have a creeping motion just as other creatures among humans and animals do. He, the Glorified, did not include the angels in the generality of "creature" but singled them out for mention. In this is an allusion—that what is attributed to them in His speech, the Exalted, of descending, ascending, going, and coming, whose appearance is displacement and spatial movement, is not of the type of creeping and spatial transfer that creatures have—which is not hidden.

So His saying: وَلِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ مِنْ دابَّةٍ means: to Him they submit and comply with an essential submission and compliance which is the reality of prostration; thus, it is His right, the Exalted, to be worshipped and prostrated to.

In the verse is an indication that in the heavens, other than the earth, there are some creatures that inhabit them and live therein.

His saying: وَالْمَلائِكَةُ وَهُمْ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ (and the angels [as well], and they are not arrogant): Arrogance (الاستكبار) and pride (التكبر) from a human is to consider himself great and place himself in the position of greatness when he is not so; therefore, it is counted among the vices. However, pride (التكبر) is sometimes applied to what Allah, the Glorified, has of majesty (الكبرياء) by right, and He is the Great, the Most High (الكبير المتعال). Thus, He, the Exalted, is Great, Proud (متكبر), but it is not said: "Arrogant" (مستكبر). Perhaps this is so in consideration of the word, for istikbar (استكبار), according to its original form, is the seeking of greatness, and its necessity is that this is not attained for the seeker from himself; he only seeks greatness and superiority over others as a claim, so it was blameworthy. As for takabbur (التكبر), it is the manifestation of majesty, whether it belongs to him in himself, as for Allah, the Glorified—which is the true pride—or it was only a claim and delusion, as in others.

It thus becomes clear that arrogance (الاستكبار) is always blameworthy. As for the arrogance of a creature over another creature, it is because poverty and need have encompassed them both, and neither of them owns for himself any benefit or harm, nor for others. Thus, the arrogance of one over the other is a departure from his limit, a transgression of his bounds, injustice, and tyranny.

As for the arrogance of the creature over the Creator, it cannot be completed except with the creature's claim of independence and self-sufficiency for himself, and his heedlessness of the station of his Lord. For the relationship between the servant and his Lord is the relationship of abasement and glory, and poverty and wealth. As long as the servant is not oblivious to this relationship and does not fail to witness the station of his Lord, his arrogance over his Lord is inconceivable. For the small, lowly one standing before the Great, the Most High, while witnessing the smallness of himself and his abasement, and the majesty of the One before him and His glory, it is not possible for him to see for himself majesty and glory unless he is overtaken by heedlessness and oblivion.

Since majesty and loftiness belong to Allah entirely, his claim of majesty and loftiness is a triumph from him over his Lord, a usurpation from Him of His station, and an arrogance and seeking of superiority over Him as a claim. This is arrogance according to the essence (الذات), and it is followed by arrogance according to the act (الفعل), which is that he does not comply with His command nor desist from His prohibition. For as long as he does not see for himself an independent will before the divine will, differing from it, he does not see for himself to oppose Him in His command and prohibition.

Accordingly, His saying: وَهُمْ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ (and they are not arrogant) in defining the angels, while the speech is in the context of servanthood (العبودية), is evidence that they are not arrogant toward their Lord; thus, they are not heedless of Him, the Exalted, nor do they fail in the consciousness of His station and witnessing Him.

He has used the negation of arrogance absolutely, without restricting it to being according to the essence or according to the act. Thus, it provided that they are not arrogant toward Him in essence or act; i.e., they are not heedless of Him, the Glorified, nor do they disdain His worship, nor do they oppose His command. To clarify this absolute and comprehensive nature, He followed it with an explanation of it in His saying: يَخافُونَ رَبَّهُمْ مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ ما يُؤْمَرُونَ (They fear their Lord [who is] above them, and they do what they are commanded), and He pointed thereby to the negation of arrogance from them in essence and act.

The clarification of that is that His saying: يَخافُونَ رَبَّهُمْ مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ (They fear their Lord [who is] above them) establishes for them fear of their Lord. Allah, the Glorified, has nothing with Him but good; there is no evil with Him, nor any cause of evil to be feared, unless the evil and its cause are with the servant. The object of fear has been taken as "their Lord," not His punishment, the Exalted, or the disobedience of His command, as in His saying: وَيَرْجُونَ رَحْمَتَهُ وَيَخافُونَ عَذابَهُ — "and hope for His mercy and fear His punishment" (17:57).

So this fear (المخافة) is the fear of Him, the Exalted. Although He has nothing but good, and fear is only of an expected evil, its reality is the being affected, the brokenness, and the smallness. The being affected of the weak before the Strong, manifest in His power, and the brokenness of the small, lowly one before the Great, the Most High, conquering with His majesty and loftiness, is necessary. Thus, their fear is their essential being affected by what they witness of the station of their Lord, and they are never heedless of it.

What we have mentioned is supported by the qualification of His saying: يَخافُونَ رَبَّهُمْ with His saying مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ (from above them), for in it is an allusion that His being, the Exalted, above them, conquering them, and being lofty in relation to them, is the cause of their fear. This is nothing but the fear of His station, the Exalted, not of His punishment; thus, it is an essential fear and returns to the negation of arrogance from their essences.

As for His saying: وَيَفْعَلُونَ ما يُؤْمَرُونَ (and they do what they are commanded), it is an allusion to their lack of arrogance in the station of act. It has already been mentioned that if one is not arrogant toward Him, the Exalted, in essence, one is not arrogant toward Him in act. Thus, they do not disobey Allah, the Glorified, in any command; rather, they do what they are commanded. In the phrasing of His saying: يُؤْمَرُونَ (they are commanded) in the passive voice, there is a glorification and magnification of His station, the Glorified, which is not hidden.

It thus becomes clear that the angels are a type of Allah's creation who are not overtaken by heedlessness of the station of their Lord, nor does oblivion, error, or forgetfulness regarding that occur to them, nor does any preoccupation distract them from it. They do not will except what Allah, the Glorified, wills.

He, the Glorified, specifically singled out the angels from among the prostrators mentioned in the verse by mentioning their state, defining their attributes, and detailing their servanthood because most of the gods of the idolaters were from the angels, such as the god of the heaven, the god of the earth, the god of sustenance, the god of beauty, and others. And to indicate that they—despite the claim of the idolaters—are the most profound of Allah's creation in His servanthood and worship.

Among the strange deductions is what some have deduced from the verse: that the angels are legally responsible (مكلفون), oscillating between fear and hope like us. As for its indication of legal responsibility (التكليف), it is due to the presence of the "command" (الأمر). As for their oscillation between fear and hope, it is because the verse mentioned their fear, and fear necessitates hope.

This is clearly invalid. As for the "command," it has appeared in His speech, the Exalted, in instances where there is certainly no legal responsibility, such as the heaven and the earth and others. He, the Exalted, said: فَقالَ لَها وَلِلْأَرْضِ ائْتِيا طَوْعاً أَوْ كَرْهاً قالَتا أَتَيْنا طائِعِينَ — "Then He said to it and to the earth, ‘Come! willingly or unwillingly.’ They said, ‘We come obediently.’" (41:11), and He said: وَيَوْمَ يَقُولُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ — "and the day He says, ‘Be!’ and it is." (6:73).

As for the necessity of hope from fear, the concomitance is only between the fear of the descent of punishment and the occurrence of the disliked, and hope. It has already been mentioned that what is in the verse is only the fear of awe and glorification, in the sense of the being affected of the weak by the Strong, and the brokenness of the small, contemptible one before the Great, the Large, manifest over him with His greatness and majesty. There is no opposition between fear in this sense and hope.

The verse has also been used as evidence that the angels are superior to humans. The flaw in this is that it is possible to infer their superiority over the disobedient among humans and their disbelievers who lack the mentioned attributes, since it is in the context of praise. As for others, the verse does not address them, neither in affirmation nor negation. The detailed discourse on the angels will come in a suitable place, if Allah wills.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ اللهُ لا تَتَّخِذُوا إِلهَيْنِ اثْنَيْنِ إِنَّما هُوَ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ فَإِيَّايَ فَارْهَبُونِ — Allah has said, ‘Do not take two gods. He is only one God. So fear Me alone.’ (16:51). Rahbah (الرهبة) is fear and is the opposite of raghbah (desire), just as fear (الخوف) is the opposite of hope (الرجاء).

The speech is conjoined to His saying: وَلِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ (To Allah prostrates...). It is also said it is conjoined to His saying: وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الذِّكْرَ (We have sent down the Reminder to you...). And it is said to His saying: ما خَلَقَ اللهُ (what Allah has created...) in the manner of the saying: "I fed her straw and cold water," meaning: "and I gave her cold water to drink." The implied meaning in the verse would be: "Have they not regarded what Allah has created of things, and have they not heard what Allah has said: لا تَتَّخِذُوا etc.?" The first is the correct view.

His saying: لا تَتَّخِذُوا إِلهَيْنِ اثْنَيْنِ (Do not take two gods) is intended—and Allah knows best—as a prohibition against exceeding the one God by taking another with Him. Thus, it includes two and whatever is above that in number. This is supported by its reinforcement with His saying: إِنَّما هُوَ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ (He is only one God). And "two" (اثْنَيْنِ) is an adjective for "two gods" (إِلهَيْنِ), just as "one" (واحِدٌ) is an adjective for "God" (إِلهٌ), brought for clarification and explanation.

In other words, the focus is related to the prohibition of taking another with Him, whether it be one or more than one. However, since every number they chose for the God above two must be reached through two—for no number above two is realized except after the realization of two—He prohibited taking two and sufficed with it as a prohibition against every number above one.

It is possible that the consideration of "two" is in view of what was their habit and sunnah. For they believe in the God of creation and origination, who is the one to whom creation belongs only, and He is the God of gods and the originator of all; and in the God of worship, who is the one to whom Lordship and governance (التدبير) belong. This meaning is more suitable to the sentences that follow it.

Accordingly, the meaning is: لا تَتَّخِذُوا إِلهَيْنِ اثْنَيْنِ (Do not take two gods): the God of creation and the God of governance to whom worship belongs. Rather, He—i.e., the God—is one God to whom both creation and governance belong, because every governance ends in origination. Since I am the Creator, the Originator, I am the Governor who must be worshipped; so fear Me alone and worship Me alone.

From here, the reason for the branching of His saying: فَإِيَّايَ فَارْهَبُونِ (So fear Me alone) from what preceded it becomes clear, and that it is a subtle deduction. The sentence provides restriction (الحصر) by placing the object first in the manner of ishtighal (اشتغال). The restriction is a "restriction of reversal" (قصر قلب), not a "restriction of isolation" (قصر إفراد) as their speech implies. For the idolaters do not worship Allah and their gods other than Allah; they only worship their gods alone, excusing themselves by saying that Allah, the Glorified, is not encompassed by knowledge nor reached by understanding, so it is not possible to turn to Him in worship. Thus, it is obligatory to worship the noble or the powerful among His creation, such as the angels and the perfected among humans and jinn. For they are the governors of the world's affairs; through worship, their good is attained and their evil is guarded against. This is the meaning of seeking proximity to Allah through their intercession (الشفاعة).

It appears that the command to fear (الرهبة) is a metaphor for the command to worship. Fear was specifically mentioned to agree with what preceded in the discourse on the prostration of all—which is the basis for the legislation of worship—regarding the fear of the angels. Accordingly, it appears that what is meant by fear is the fear of majesty and awe, not the fear of punishment and penalty. So understand that.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلَهُ الدِّينُ واصِباً أَفَغَيْرَ اللهِ تَتَّقُونَ — To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth, and to Him belongs the religion, perpetually. Will you then fear other than Allah? (16:52). He said in al-Mufradat: "Wasab (الوصب) is the persistent illness... He, the Exalted, said: وَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ واصِبٌ — 'and for them is a perpetual punishment' (37:9), and وَلَهُ الدِّينُ واصِباً (and to Him belongs the religion, perpetually). It is a threat to whoever takes two gods and an alert that the recompense of whoever does that is a persistent, severe punishment."

And "religion" (الدين) here would be "obedience" (الطاعة), and the meaning of "perpetual" (واصباً) is "everlasting" (الدائم); i.e., it is the right of the human to obey Him always in all his states, as He described the angels where He said: لا يَعْصُونَ اللهُ ما أَمَرَهُمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ ما يُؤْمَرُونَ — "they do not disobey Allah in what He commands them, and they do what they are commanded." (66:6). It is said: wasaba al-dinu wasuban, meaning it became lasting; and wasaba al-dinu, meaning it became obligatory; and mafazatun wasibatun, meaning a distant desert with no end. End of quote.

The verse and what follows it argue for His oneness, the Exalted, in Divinity (الألوهية) in the sense of being the Worshipped by right, and that the religion belongs to Him alone. No one has the right to legislate any of that, nor to be obeyed in what he legislated. Thus, the verse and what follows it are in the position of justifying His saying: وَقالَ اللهُ لا تَتَّخِذُوا إِلهَيْنِ اثْنَيْنِ until the end of the verse, and an argument for its content, and a return after return to what has already been explained of monotheism and prophethood which the polytheists deny.

So His saying: وَلَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ (To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth) is an argument for His uniqueness, the Exalted, in Lordship (الربوبية). For whatever is in the heavens and the earth is owned by Him in the reality of the meaning of ownership (الملك). For everything in the witnessed world, with its attributes and acts, is established by Him, the Exalted, existing by His origination and manifest by His manifestation. It cannot be disconnected from Him for even a moment. Thus, things are established by Him as the property is established by its owner, owned by Him with a real ownership that accepts neither change nor transfer, as is the characteristic of real ownership, such as a human's ownership of his hearing and sight, for example.

If it is so, then He, the Exalted, is the Governor (المدبر) of the world's affairs. For there is no meaning to the world being owned by Him with this ownership and then someone else becoming independent in governing its affairs and disposing of it, while He, the Exalted, is isolated from what He created and owned. And if He is the Governor of its affairs, then He is the Lord (الرب) of it, for the Lord is the Owner-Governor. And if He is the Lord, then He is the one who must be feared and to whom submission must be given through worship.

His saying: وَلَهُ الدِّينُ واصِباً (and to Him belongs the religion, perpetually), i.e., always and necessarily. That is because when He, the Exalted, was the Lord who owns things and governs their affairs—and it is of the duty of governance that the human world follow a sunnah by which its following reaches its goal and which guides it to its felicity, and this sunnah and way is what the Quran calls "religion" (دينا)—it was obligatory that He, the Exalted, be the one established over the setting of this sunnah and the legislation of this way. Thus, He, the Exalted, is the Owner of the religion, as He said: وَلَهُ الدِّينُ واصِباً, and it is upon Him to legislate what rectifies the governance, as He said previously: وَعَلَى اللهِ قَصْدُ السَّبِيلِ — "With Allah rests the guidance to the straight path" (16:9).

It is said: What is meant by "religion" is obedience; it is said: ownership; it is said: recompense. For each of these, there is a reason not hidden from the reflector. The most appropriate is what we have presented, because it is more consistent and suitable with the context of the preceding and following verses discussing the monotheism of Lordship and the legislation of religion through revelation and messengership.

His saying: أَفَغَيْرَ اللهِ تَتَّقُونَ (Will you then fear other than Allah?) is a rhetorical question of denial—branched from both sentences, apparently. The meaning is: If it is so, do you fear and worship other than Him, the Exalted? While he owns nothing and governs no matter such that he should be worshipped, and he has no right to legislate a religion such that he should be obeyed in what he set and legislated.

His saying, the Exalted: (And whatever blessing you have, it is from Allah; then when adversity visits you, unto Him you groan) [16:53].
