This is another exposition of His oneness, the Exalted, in Lordship (الربوبية) — the absolute sovereignty and nurturing care of Allah over all creation. Upon this, the Glorified bases His censure and rebuke of them for their association of partners (الشرك) with Allah and for their legislating matters of their own accord without His permission or pleasure. The discourse continues in this vein for several subsequent verses.

What is meant by adversity (الضر) is a wretched state resulting from the loss of a blessing (النعمة) by which one's condition is rectified. Groaning (الجؤار) — with a damma on the jim — refers to the sound made by wild beasts; it is used here metaphorically for raising the voice in supplication, entreaty, and seeking aid, likening the former to the latter.

Regarding His saying: (And whatever blessing you have, it is from Allah), the speech is intended as a universal statement. It is not merely a claim without proof, for that has been clarified in the preceding verses. Furthermore, the listeners concede and affirm this, as evidenced by their groaning and seeking His aid when adversity touches them through the loss of one of the blessings.

The meaning, then, is that all the blessings you possess are from His bestowal upon you, and you are aware of this. Then, if any adversity or slight ill-fortune befalls you, you raise your voices in humble entreaty and groan to Him, not to anyone else. Had anyone else done you a favor, you would have turned to them. Thus, He, the Glorified, is the Bestower of blessings and the Remover of adversity; so why is it that you do not devote your worship (العبادة) exclusively to Him, nor obey Him?

Seeking aid (الاستغاثة) from Him, the Exalted, and entreating Him when calamities strike and hardships descend — at which point hope in apparent causes (الأسباب الظاهرية) is severed — is a necessity (ضرورية) about which there is no doubt. For even if a human being does not adhere to a religion or believe in Allah, the Glorified, his hope is never severed during hardships when he returns to what he finds within himself. There is no hope except that there is one in whom hope is placed. It is a necessity that the realization of anything that involves the meaning of attachment (التعلق) — such as love, hate, will, dislike, attraction, and their likes — in the external world (الخارج) is impossible except with the realization of the object of that attachment in the external world. If there were no object of will (مراد) in the external world, no will (إرادة) would be realized from a willer (مريد); if there were no sought object (مطلوب), there would be no seeking (طلب); and if there were no attractor (جاذب) that attracts, an attracted object (مجذوب) that is drawn could not be conceived. This is the case for all existential concepts whose being is not devoid of a relation (نسبة).

Thus, the attachment of a human being's hope to deliverance from affliction when causes are severed is proof that he perceives a Cause above these severed causes — one who is not incapacitated by the magnitude of events or the suddenness of disasters, from whom the human being is never severed, and who never ceases, perishes, errs, or forgets.

This is something the human being finds within himself and which his primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — dictates, even if preoccupation with apparent causes distracts him from it, and the enjoyments of life and the ornaments of sensible matter (المادة المحسوسة) draw him toward themselves. However, when affliction surrounds him, stratagems fail him, the paths of salvation are blocked, the apparent causes are utterly defeated, and obstacles vanish from his sight — leaving no distraction to divert him nor preoccupation to engage him — that which the causes had hidden becomes manifest to him. He witnesses what he had been heedless of, and his soul attaches itself to Him; He is the Cause above every cause, and He is Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه).

His saying, the Exalted: (Then when He removes the adversity from you, behold, a group of you ascribe partners to their Lord) [16:54]. This is the beginning of His censure and rebuke of them, concluding with a threat against them. They are deserving of this because the removal of adversity following their seeking of aid and their natural return to their Lord demands that they affirm His oneness in Lordship after the truth has been revealed to them by the repelling of affliction and the descent of mercy. However, a group of them are overtaken by wretchedness (الشقوة), so they return to attaching themselves to causes. At that moment, the dormant vices of their character awaken, stirring up their whims, and they associate others with their Lord, including the causes they cling to. The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: (So that they may be ungrateful for what We have given them. So enjoy yourselves; soon you will know!) [16:55]. The lam is for the consequence (للغاية); that is, they only associate partners with their Lord so that they may be ungrateful for the blessing We gave them by removing the adversity from them, and not show gratitude to Him.

Making ingratitude (الكفر) for the blessing the consequence of their polytheism (الشرك) is based on the claim that they have no goal in the course of their lives except ingratitude for Allah's blessing and failing to thank Him for what He has bestowed. Their preoccupation with the senses and matter has bequeathed to their hearts a habit of attachment to apparent causes and the attribution of divine blessings to them. They have made these causes a thick veil over the recognition of their primordial nature, causing them to forget the oneness (توحيد) of their Lord in His Lordship. Thus, with every blessing, they mention its apparent causes instead of Allah; they attach themselves to them, fear their cessation, and submit to them instead of Allah. It is as if they — indeed, they truly — have no goal other than ingratitude for Allah's blessing and the absence of gratitude.

Thus, disbelief (الكفر) in Allah, the Glorified, is their general goal in every state they manifest and every action they perform. When they associate partners with their Lord after the removal of adversity by submitting to other causes, they only do so to be ungrateful for what He has given them of blessings.

Since this ingratitude of theirs — which is a persistent disbelief they insist upon and an arrogance (استكبار) against Allah — and He, the Exalted, has said: (If you are grateful, I will surely enhance you [in blessing], but if you are ungrateful, My punishment is indeed severe) [14:7], the mention of this provoked Divine Wrath. Thus, He turned from addressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) while they were described in the third person (الغيبة) to addressing them directly and threatening them without an intermediary, saying: (So enjoy yourselves; soon you will know!).

He did not mention what they would enjoy to indicate by its absolute (الإطلاق) nature that they will be held accountable for everything they enjoyed and that none of it will benefit them. Nor did He mention what they would "know" — which is inevitably something that will grieve them — so that they remain ignorant of it until it befalls them suddenly, and there appears to them from Allah what they had not expected. This contains an intensification of the threat.

Some have mentioned that the lam in His saying: (So that they may be ungrateful for what We have given them) is the lam of command (لام الأمر), and what is intended by it is a threat by way of demonstrating their helplessness (التعجيز), but this is an affected interpretation (تكلف).

His saying, the Exalted: (And they assign a share of what We have provided them to what they do not know. By Allah, you will surely be questioned about what you used to fabricate) [16:56]. It has been mentioned that this is coordinated (معطوف) with their other crimes indicated by the previous verses. The implied meaning is that they commit the crimes We have recounted and assign a share to what they do not know. It appears that the ma (ما) in (to what they do not know) is a relative pronoun (موصولة) referring to their deities, and the plural pronoun refers to the polytheists (المشركين), while the object of (they do not know) is omitted. The meaning is: the polytheists assign to their deities — whose condition they do not know to be either harmful or beneficial — a share of what We have provided them.

What is meant by this "assigning" is what the Glorified mentioned in Surah al-An'am by His saying: وَجَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ مِمَّا ذَرَأَ مِنَ الْحَرْثِ وَالْأَنْعامِ نَصِيباً فَقالُوا هذا لِلَّهِ بِزَعْمِهِمْ وَهذا لِشُرَكائِنا فَما كانَ لِشُرَكائِهِمْ فَلا يَصِلُ إِلَى اللهِ وَما كانَ لِلَّهِ فَهُوَ يَصِلُ إِلى شُرَكائِهِمْ ساءَ ما يَحْكُمُونَ — "They designate for Allah a share of the crops and livestock He has created, and say, ‘This is for Allah,’ so they claim, ‘and this is for our partners.’ But what is for their partners does not reach Allah, and what is for Allah reaches their partners. Evil is the judgment they make." [6:136]. This is what they have mentioned, and it is not free from affectation.

It is possible that it is coordinated with what preceded in His saying: (they ascribe partners), and the implied meaning is: "when a group of you ascribe partners to their Lord and assign a share of what We have provided them to what they do not know." What is meant by "what they do not know" are the apparent causes to which they attribute effects independently (على سبيل الاستقلال), while they are ignorant of the reality of their condition and have no certain knowledge that they harm or benefit, despite seeing them fail to produce an effect at times.

The reason it is attributed to them that they assign a share of their provision to these causes — even though they attribute provision to them independently without mentioning Allah alongside them, which would imply a total denial of His effect, the Exalted, rather than associating Him with them — is because they possess an innate knowledge (علم فطري) that Allah, the Glorified, has an effect in the matter. It was mentioned of them just now that they groan to Him when adversity touches them. When this admission of theirs is considered alongside their attribution of effect to the causes, it results in the causes being, in their view, partners with Allah in provision, having a share in it. Then He threatened them with His saying: (By Allah, you will surely be questioned about what you used to fabricate).

His saying, the Exalted: (And they assign daughters to Allah — immaculate is He! — while for themselves they have what they desire) [16:57]. This is another reproach of them for a judgment they made out of ignorance without knowledge. They showed respect for themselves but behaved with ill-manners and audacity toward Allah, the Glorified, by choosing sons for themselves and disliking daughters, yet attributing them to Allah, the Glorified.

So His saying: (And they assign daughters to Allah — immaculate is He!) refers to their taking deities besides Allah, or some deities, as females, and their saying that they are the daughters of Allah. It has been said that the tribes of Khuza'ah and Kinanah used to say that the angels are the daughters of Allah.

Brahmanical and Buddhist idolatry, as well as the Sabians, affirmed many deities from the angels and jinn as females, and that they are the daughters of Allah. In the Noble Quran: وَجَعَلُوا الْمَلائِكَةَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ عِبادُ الرَّحْمنِ إِناثاً — "They have made the angels, who are servants of the All-beneficent, females." [43:19], and He, the Exalted, said: وَجَعَلُوا بَيْنَهُ وَبَيْنَ الْجِنَّةِ نَسَباً — "They have set up a kinship between Him and the jinn." [37:158].

The Imam [al-Razi] said in his Tafsir regarding the reason for this: "I think they called them daughters because of their being hidden from sight, like women; just as they took the sun to be feminine because its disk is hidden by its overwhelming light and brightness from the eyes, like secluded women. It is not necessary for this naming to be consistent such that it would apply to the jinn for being hidden from sight without being feminine." (End of summary).

Another mentioned: "The reason for the feminization is their being hidden from sight while being in a place that strangers cannot reach; thus they are like daughters for whom a man is protective, housing them in a secure and firm place. Although the jinn are hidden from sight, it is not in this manner." (End).

These two explanations do not exceed the level of mere rhetorical preference (الاستحسان). If you were to review the views of the idolaters in their diversity — a portion of which was presented in the tenth volume of this book — you would know that the Arabs were not the originators of this belief; rather, it has an ancient root in the views of the ancient idolaters in India, Egypt, Babylon, Greece, and Rome.

Careful examination of the foundations of their views reveals that they used to take the angels — to whom the aspects of good in the world are traced — and the jinn — to whom evils are attributed — as deities whom they worshipped out of hope and fear. These high principles and universal powers (القوى الكلية) that they carry — or in other words, of which they are manifestations — are divided into active (فاعلة) and passive (منفعلة). They considered the meeting of the active and passive among them as a marriage and union, the active being a father, the passive a mother, and the result of their union a child. The children are divided into sons and daughters. Thus, among the deities are those who are mothers and daughters, and those who are fathers and sons.

So, if some of the Arab idolaters said that all the angels are daughters of Allah, it was a statement in which they intended to imitate those before them out of ignorance and without verification.

His saying: (while for themselves they have what they desire). The apparent context is that it is coordinated with (daughters to Allah), and the implied meaning is: "and they assign for themselves what they desire." That is, they affirm daughters for Allah, the Glorified, by believing the angels to be His daughters, while they affirm for themselves what they desire — which are sons — by killing the daughters and burying them alive (الوأد). The result is that they are satisfied for Allah with what they are not satisfied with for themselves.

It has been said that (what they desire) is a delayed subject (مبتدأ مؤخر) and (for them) is a preceding predicate (خبر مقدم), and the sentence is coordinated with (they assign). According to this, the sentence is intended for rebuke or mockery.

They justified this by saying that coordinating the sentence with (daughters to Allah) is not permissible because it violates the rule: that a transitive verb which reaches its object directly or via a preposition, if its subject is an attached nominative pronoun, cannot reach that same pronoun directly or via a preposition except with a separator. For example, if Zayd struck himself, one does not say: Zayd struck him or You struck you; and if he was angry with himself, one does not say: Zayd was angry with him. Rather, it is said: Zayd struck himself or He struck none but him, and Zayd was angry with himself or He was angry with none but him — except in the category of verbs of "supposition" (ظن) and what is attached to them like "loss" and "absence," where it is permissible to say: Zayd thought him strong (meaning himself).

Based on this, if His saying: (while for themselves they have what they desire) were coordinated with His saying: (daughters to Allah), it would have been mandatory to say: "and for their own selves (لأنفسهم) what they desire." (End of summary).

The truth is that adherence to this rule is only to prevent ambiguity (اللبس), and the intervention of a preposition between the two pronouns constitutes a separation. In the Noble Quran: وَهُزِّي إِلَيْكِ بِجِذْعِ النَّخْلَةِ — "Shake the trunk of the palm tree toward yourself" [19:25], and وَاضْمُمْ إِلَيْكَ جَناحَكَ — "And draw your hand to your side" [28:32]. Some have rejected the rule entirely because it is invalidated by these two verses. They also answered with other points that we have no need to mention; whoever desires them should refer to the commentaries.

His saying, the Exalted: (When one of them is brought the news of a female [newborn], his face becomes darkened and he is choked with grief) [16:58]. The darkening of the face is a metaphor for anger, and the "choked with grief" (الكظيم) is one who swallows his rage. The sentence is a circumstantial clause (حالية); that is, they attribute daughters to their Lord while the state is such that if one of them is given the news of a female — and it is said to him: "A daughter has been born to you" — his face turns black with rage while he swallows his anger.

His saying, the Exalted: (He hides from the people because of the evil of the news he has been given...) [16:59] to the end of the verse. Hiding (التواري) is to conceal oneself, derived from "behind" (الوراء). Ignominy (الهون) is humiliation and disgrace, and burying (الدس) is concealment.

The meaning: This person who has been given the news of a daughter hides from the people because of the "evil" of the news he has received according to his belief. He ponders his affair: shall he keep what he has been given news of — which is the daughter — despite the humiliation of keeping and protecting her, or shall he hide her in the dust? This was their custom regarding female newborns, as it is said that one of them would dig a small pit, and if the newborn was female, he would place her in the pit and heap dust upon her until she died beneath it. They used to do this for fear of poverty for them, or that those who were not their equals would desire them.

The first time this appeared among them was when the tribe of Bani Tamim raided Khosrow, who defeated them and took their women and children captive. He brought them into the royal palace and took the girls as maidservants and concubines. After a while, they made peace and sought the return of the captives. The girls were given the choice to return to their families, but several of them refused. This enraged the men of Bani Tamim, so they resolved that no female would be born to them except that they would bury her alive (وأدوها). Then some of those below them followed them in this, and the practice of burying daughters alive (وأد البنات) became widespread among them.

His saying: (Behold! Evil is the judgment they make) refers to their judgment that He has daughters while they have sons — not because of the inherent lowliness of daughters or the nobility of sons in reality, but rather the meaning of this judgment in their view is that Allah should have what they dislike while they have what they love. It has been said: what is meant by the judgment is their judgment regarding the obligation of burying daughters alive and that keeping them is an ignominy. The first of the two views is more fitting and appropriate for the following verse.

His saying, the Exalted: لِلَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ مَثَلُ السَّوْءِ وَلِلَّهِ الْمَثَلُ الْأَعْلى وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ (For those who do not believe in the Hereafter is an evil attribute, and to Allah belongs the highest attribute, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. 16:60). The attribute (المثل) refers to a quality or description (الصفة); from this, a popular proverb (المثل السائر) is so named because it is a description that circulates upon tongues and is applied in every situation that suits or resembles it.

The word "evil" (السوء) — with a fatḥah on the sīn and a sukūn on the wāw — is the verbal noun (مصدر) of "to be bad" (ساء يسوء), just as "evil" (السُّوء) — with a ḍammah — is its noun. The annexation of "attribute" to "evil" serves to categorize, for things are described either from the perspective of their goodness (حسن) or from the perspective of their evil and ugliness (قبح). Thus, attributes are of two types: the good attribute and the evil attribute.

Goodness and ugliness may sometimes stem from innate creation (الخلقة), where a human has no hand or choice, such as beauty of face or deformity of build. At other times, they attach to a person due to voluntary actions (الأعمال الاختيارية), such as the goodness of justice and the ugliness of oppression. Reason (العقل) only praises or blames that which belongs to the second category, not the first. Therefore, praise and blame essentially revolve around acting according to what the human primordial nature (الفطرة الإنسانية) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — deems good and commands. This includes actions that lead a person to the happiness of his life and the abandonment of their opposites, which is what the true religion (الدين الحق) contains of primordial laws.

It is well known that the human temperament (الطبع) has no deterrent against committing an evil act except the pain of accountability and the severity of punishment, and the person's submission to the fact that such punishment will occur and be fulfilled. As for blame, it turns into praise if the act becomes widespread and ceases to be considered a reprehensible (منكر) deed.

From here, it becomes clear that belief in the Hereafter (الإيمان بالآخرة) and submission to the Reckoning (الحساب) and Requital (الجزاء) is the sole foundation that guarantees safeguarding a human from committing evil deeds and protects him from the attachment of any blame or disgrace. It is the source that rectifies human actions in a way that compels him to adhere to the path of happiness. No other foundational knowledge (المعارف الأصلية) can produce this effect, not even monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — to which every other principle ultimately returns.

To this, His saying, the Exalted, points: وَلا تَتَّبِعِ الْهَوى فَيُضِلَّكَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِما نَسُوا يَوْمَ الْحِسابِ (and do not follow desire, or it will lead you astray from the way of Allah. Indeed those who stray from the way of Allah—there is a severe punishment for them because of their forgetting the Day of Reckoning. 38:26).

Thus, the lack of belief in the Hereafter and the belittling of the matter of Reckoning and Requital is the source and destination of every evil deed. Conversely, belief in the Hereafter is the origin of every good deed and the fountainhead of every blessing. Every evil attribute (مثل سوء) and quality of ugliness that attaches to a human only comes to him by way of forgetting the Hereafter, just as every good attribute and quality of praise is the opposite.

Through what has preceded, the subtle point (النكتة) in His saying: لِلَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ مَثَلُ السَّوْءِ (For those who do not believe in the Hereafter is an evil attribute) becomes apparent. He had been describing them in the previous verses with polytheism (الشرك), but when He intended to explain that they possess the evil attribute, He substituted that with the description of their lack of belief in the Hereafter.

Those who do not believe in the Hereafter are the primary source for the occurrence of every evil attribute and quality of ugliness, for its criterion—which is the denial of the Hereafter—is an inseparable trait of theirs. If some of those who believe in the Hereafter are afflicted by an evil attribute, it only attaches to them due to forgetting the Day of Reckoning, while the deniers are the root of that.

This concerns evil qualities that reason deems ugly and blameworthy. However, there are other "evil" attributes that reason does not deem ugly but which the temperament (الطبع) dislikes, such as femininity among some people, the birth of daughters among others, financial poverty, illness, death, extinction (الفناء), incapacity (العجز), and ignorance (الجهل). These are shared between the believer and the disbeliever. There are also other analytical attributes such as poverty, need, deficiency, non-existence (العدم), and contingency (الإمكان) — the state of being that requires a cause to exist — which are not exclusive to humans but are shared among all contingent beings (الممكنات) and permeate all creation. The disbeliever is characterized by them just as others are. Thus, the disbeliever is subject to being described by every evil attribute—some specific to him and some shared between him and others, as explained in detail.

Allah, the Glorified, is transcendent (منزه) above being described by any of these qualities which are evil attributes (أمثال السوء). As for the evil attributes resulting from evil deeds—which reason deems ugly and blameworthy, and which are summarized by oppression (الظلم)—He does not oppress anything. The Exalted said: وَلا يَظْلِمُ رَبُّكَ أَحَداً (and your Lord does not wrong anyone. 18:49) and said: وَهُوَ الْحَكِيمُ الْعَلِيمُ (and He is the All-wise, the All-knowing. 43:84). Whatever decree He ordains or act He performs is the very requirement of wisdom (الحكمة); nothing else would be appropriate regarding the prevailing system of existence.

As for the evil attributes that the temperament dislikes or reason analytically identifies, there is no way for them to reach Him, the Exalted. For He is the Absolute Mighty (عزيز مطلق) whose side is immune to the infiltration of any abasement (ذلة). He possesses all power (القدرة), so no incapacity (عجز) affects Him; He possesses all knowledge (العلم), so no ignorance (جهل) occurs to Him; and He possesses pure life (الحياة), so neither death nor extinction threatens Him. He is transcendent above every deficiency and non-existence, so He is not described by the qualities of bodies (الأجسام) which involve deficiency, loss, inadequacy, or frailty. The verses regarding these meanings are numerous and clear, requiring no citation.

He, the Glorified, possesses such loftiness and purity that He is not described by any of the evil attributes that characterize others. Not only is He transcendent to this degree, but He is also transcendent above being described by any of the good attributes (الأمثال الحسنة) and noble, beautiful qualities in the sense that they characterize others—such as life, knowledge, power, might, greatness, and majesty. For the good, perfect qualities found in contingent beings are limited, finite, tainted by poverty and need, and mixed with loss and deficiency. However, the attributes belonging to Him, the Glorified, are pure perfection (محض الكمال), and their reality is unlimited, infinite, and untainted by deficiency or non-existence. He has a life not threatened by death, a power not touched by fatigue or incapacity, a knowledge not accompanied by ignorance, and a might with which there is no abasement.

Thus, to Him belongs the highest attribute (المثل الأعلى) and the most beautiful quality. The Exalted said: وَلَهُ الْمَثَلُ الْأَعْلى فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ (To Him belongs the highest attribute in the heavens and the earth. 30:27) and said: لَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى (To Him belong the most beautiful names. 20:8). Attributes (الأمثال) include the lowly and the lofty; and among the lofty, some are "highest" (أعلى) and some are not. The "highest" is His attribute, the Exalted. Names (الأسماء) include the evil and the good; and among the good, some are "most beautiful" (أحسن) and some are not. To Allah belongs that which is most beautiful. Understand this.

It has become clear from the preceding the meaning of His attribute being "highest," and that His saying: وَلِلَّهِ الْمَثَلُ الْأَعْلى (and to Allah belongs the highest attribute) is phrased for exclusivity (الحصر). That is, to Allah alone belongs the attribute that is highest, to the exclusion of the attribute that is evil and lowly, and to the exclusion of the attribute that is good and lofty—referring to the qualities of perfection that characterize contingent beings but are not "highest."

It has also become clear that the "highest attribute" that appears for Him, the Exalted, from the previous explanation, is the negation of all evil qualities from Him, as He said: لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ (Nothing is like Him. 42:11), and from the affirmative qualities (الصفات الثبوتية), every good quality with its limits and deficiencies negated.

His saying: وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ (and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise) is phrased to provide exclusivity and to justify what preceded it. That is, He is the One who possesses all might (العزة), so no abasement touches Him at all, because every abasement is the loss of some might, and He does not lack any might. He possesses all wisdom (الحكمة), so no ignorance affects Him, because ignorance is the loss of some wisdom, and He lacks nothing of wisdom.

Since there is no way for abasement or ignorance to reach Him, He is not described by any qualities of deficiency, nor is He characterized by any traits of blame or evil attributes. However, the disbeliever is abased in his essence and ignorant in himself; thus, qualities of deficiency attach to and accompany him, and he is described by traits of blame and evil attributes. Therefore, "for those who do not believe in the Hereafter is an evil attribute."

The believer, although abased in his essence and ignorant in himself like the disbeliever, has entered into the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah. Thus, his Lord has granted him might through His might and made him prevail over ignorance by supporting him with a spirit from Him. The Exalted said: وَاللهُ وَلِيُّ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ (and Allah is the Guardian of the believers. 3:68), and said: وَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ وَلِرَسُولِهِ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (and all might belongs to Allah and His Apostle and the believers. 63:8), and said: أُولئِكَ كَتَبَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْإِيمانَ وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِرُوحٍ مِنْهُ (He has written faith into their hearts and strengthened them with a spirit from Him. 58:22).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ يُؤاخِذُ اللهُ النَّاسَ بِظُلْمِهِمْ ما تَرَكَ عَلَيْها مِنْ دَابَّةٍ (Were Allah to take mankind to task for their wrongdoing, He would not leave any living being upon it... 16:61) to the end of the verse. The pronoun in "upon it" (عَلَيْها) refers to the earth, as "mankind" (النَّاسَ) indicates it.

It is not far-fetched to claim that the context indicates that "living being" (دابة) refers only to the human being, from the perspective of his crawling and moving (يدب ويتحرك). The meaning would be: If Allah were to take mankind to task for their wrongdoing, continuing the accountability, He would not leave on the earth any human who crawls and moves. As for the majority of people, they would perish due to their wrongdoing. As for the rarest few—the Prophets and the Imams who are infallible (المعصومون) from wrongdoing—they would not exist due to the prior perishing of their fathers and mothers.

The commentators have taken "living being" (الدابة) in the verse in its general sense, which is everything that crawls upon the earth of humans and animals. Thus, the meaning of the verse becomes that if He were to take them to task for their wrongdoing, He would destroy mankind and every animal on earth. This raises the question: If it is the human who perishes due to his wrongdoing, why should the rest of the animals perish when they have no wrongdoing, or do they perish due to the wrongdoing of the human?

The most plausible answer given is the statement of some, with our refinement: If Allah were to take them to task for their wrongdoing—whether disbelief or disobedience—the general population would perish due to their wrongdoing except the infallible ones. As for the infallible ones, despite their rarity and small number, they would not exist due to the prior perishing of their fathers and mothers. When humans perish and procreation ceases, the "living beings" of other animals would perish because they were created for the benefits and interests of the servants, as suggested by His saying, the Exalted: خَلَقَ لَكُمْ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً (It is He who created for you all that is in the earth. 2:29).

They have other views in defending the verse on the assumption that "living being" is general, but there is no benefit in recounting them. Whoever desires them should refer to the lengthy commentaries.

Some have used the verse to argue against the infallibility (عصمة) of the Prophets (peace be upon them). The response is that the verse does not indicate more than that if Allah were to take people to task for wrongdoing, all people would perish and the species would become extinct. However, it does not indicate that everyone who perishes does so only because of his own wrongdoing. It is possible that the majority perish due to their wrongdoing, and the minority perish due to the extinction of their fathers and mothers, as mentioned before. Thus, the verse does not indicate that wrongdoing encompasses every individual, including the Prophets and the infallible ones; it only indicates that the extinction encompasses everyone.

It has been said in response that "mankind" refers to the wrongdoers among them, by the evidence of His saying: "for their wrongdoing" (بِظُلْمِهِمْ), so it does not include the infallible ones from the start.

It has also been answered that "wrongdoing" (الظلم) is more general than disobedience (المعصية)—which is the violation of a masterly command—and includes "leaving the better option" (ترك الأولى), which is the violation of an advisory command. This sometimes occurred from the Prophets (peace be upon them), as narrated from Adam and his wife: قالا رَبَّنا ظَلَمْنا أَنْفُسَنا (They said, ‘Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves.’ 7:23) and other Prophets. Thus, the good deeds of the righteous are the "sins" of those drawn near (المقربين). In this case, the generality of "wrongdoing" in the verse including the Prophets does not indicate a lack of their infallibility from disobedience in the sense of violating a masterly command.

It has also been answered that the destruction of all people would occur by Allah withholding the descent of rain upon the earth due to the wrongdoing of the wrongdoers among mankind. Thus, the wrongdoers, the saints, and the animals would all perish. For when punishment descends, it does not differentiate between the wretched and the felicitous; it would be a vengeance and exemplary punishment for the enemy, and a trial and increase in reward for others.

All three answers are incomplete. As for the first: restricting "mankind" to the wrongdoers would necessitate restricting the destruction to them, as claimed. Thus, the destruction would not encompass the infallible ones, and there would be no reason then for the destruction of the rest of the living beings created for the human. Therefore, His saying: "He would not leave any living being upon it" would not be sound, as is not hidden.

As for the second: the verses, given their context, discuss wrongdoing in the sense of polytheism and other masterly disobediences. Generalizing "wrongdoing" in the verse to include "leaving the better option"—especially considering the end of the verse: وَلكِنْ يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى (but He respites them until a specified time)—which clearly implies a threat, does not suit the context.

As for the third: there is no evidence from the wording for what was mentioned in it.

His saying: وَلكِنْ يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى فَإِذا جاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ ساعَةً وَلا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ (but He respites them until a specified time. When their time comes, they shall not defer it by a single hour nor shall they advance it. 16:61) is an emendation (استدراك) from a suppressed premise indicated by the conditional clause at the beginning of the verse. The implied meaning is: He does not hasten in taking them to task, but He delays them until a specified time (أجل مسمى). The "specified time" regarding an individual human is his inevitable death; regarding a nation (الأمة), it is the day of its extinction; and regarding mankind in general, it is the blowing of the Trumpet and the rising of the Hour. Each of these has a mention in His speech, the Exalted. He said: وَمِنْكُمْ مَنْ يُتَوَفَّى مِنْ قَبْلُ وَلِتَبْلُغُوا أَجَلاً مُسَمًّى (and some of you are taken away before, and that you may complete a specified term. 40:67); and He said: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ فَإِذا جاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ ساعَةً وَلا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ (There is a time for every nation: when their time comes, they shall not defer it by a single hour nor shall they advance it. 7:34); and He said: وَلَوْ لا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ (Had it not been for a prior decree from your Lord, the matter would have been decided between them until a specified time. 42:14).

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَجْعَلُونَ لِلَّهِ ما يَكْرَهُون وتَصِفُ أَلْسِنَتُهُمُ الْكَذِبَ أَنَّ لَهُمُ الْحُسْنى (They ascribe to Allah what they [themselves] dislike, and their tongues utter the lie that the best of things is for them... 16:62) to the end of the verse. This is a return to the polytheists' attribution of daughters to Him, the Exalted, while choosing sons for themselves, though they dislike daughters and love and prefer sons.

Thus, His saying: وَيَجْعَلُونَ لِلَّهِ ما يَكْرَهُونَ (They ascribe to Allah what they dislike) means daughters. His saying: وَتَصِفُ أَلْسِنَتُهُمُ الْكَذِبَ (and their tongues utter the lie) means their tongues report the false news, which is: أَنَّ لَهُمُ الْحُسْنى (that the best of things is for them), meaning the good outcome of life, which is that sons should succeed them. It has been said that "the best" (الحسنى) refers to Paradise, on the assumption of the truth of the Resurrection and the truthfulness of the Prophets in what they report, as He narrated from them in His saying: وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْناهُ رَحْمَةً مِنَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ضَرَّاءَ مَسَّتْهُ لَيَقُولَنَّ هذا لِي وَما أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قائِمَةً وَلَئِنْ رُجِعْتُ إِلى رَبِّي إِنَّ لِي عِنْدَهُ لَلْحُسْنى (And if We let him taste a mercy from Us after some adversity that has visited him, he will surely say, ‘This is my due! I do not think the Hour will ever set in. And even if I am returned to my Lord, I will indeed have the best with Him.’ 41:50). This view would be acceptable were it not for the end of the verse and its upcoming meaning.

His saying: لا جَرَمَ أَنَّ لَهُمُ النَّارَ وَأَنَّهُمْ مُفْرَطُونَ (Undoubtedly, the Fire shall be their lot, and they shall be the first to be sent [into it]. 16:62). That is, those sent forward to the punishment of the Fire. It is said "he preceded" (فرط) and "he exceeded" (أفرط), meaning he went forward. Excess (الإفراط) is extravagance in going forward, just as negligence (التفريط) is falling short in it. The "forerunner" (الفرط) — with two fatḥahs — is the one who precedes the caravan to prepare the lodging and water. It is said: "he sent him forward" (أفرطه).

Since their saying—that Allah has what they dislike and they have the best—was a lie and fabrication in the sense of claiming they preceded their Lord to the best and left for Him what they dislike, He threatened them with the reality of this claim as a recompense for their lie. That is, the Fire is for them and they are truly sent forward to it. This is His saying: لا جَرَمَ أَنَّ لَهُمُ النَّارَ (Undoubtedly, the Fire shall be their lot) etc.

His saying, the Exalted: تَاللَّهِ لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا إِلَى أُمَمٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ أَعْمَالَهُمْ فَهُوَ وَلِيُّهُمُ الْيَوْمَ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ "By Allah, We certainly sent [apostles] to nations before you, but Satan made their deeds seem fair to them. So he is their patron today, and there is a painful punishment for them." (16:63). The apparent context suggests that "today" (اليوم) refers to the day the verse was revealed. The meaning of Satan being their patron (ولياً) on that day is their consensus upon deviation (الضلال) during the time of revelation. The promised punishment refers to the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, as is apparent from most verses that threaten with punishment.

The meaning is: By Allah, We certainly sent Our messengers to nations before you—such as the Jews, Christians, and Magians, among those who have not become extinct like ʿĀd and Thamūd—but Satan made their deeds seem fair to them, so they followed him and turned away from Our messengers. Thus, he is their patron today while they are united in deviation, and for them in the Day of Resurrection is a painful punishment.

Al-Zamakhshari allowed, based on this interpretation, that the pronoun in "their patron" (وليّهم) refers to Quraysh. The meaning would then be that Satan made the deeds of past nations seem fair to them, and he is today the patron of Quraysh. However, this is far-fetched as it necessitates a discrepancy in the referents of the pronouns.

It is also possible that "nations" refers to the past and perished nations; thus, Satan’s patronage over them "today" refers to them being among the partisans of Satan in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — and they have a painful punishment there.

It has also been said: "today" refers to the duration of this world, for it is the day of patronage, while the punishment is on the Day of Resurrection.

It has also been said: it refers to the Day of Resurrection; it is there that Satan is their patron and they have a painful punishment.

It has also been said: it refers to the day Satan made their deeds seem fair to them, which would be a case of narrating a past state (حكاية الحال الماضية).

The closest of these views is the first, followed by the next, and so on. And Allah knows best.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ إِلَّا لِتُبَيِّنَ لَهُمُ الَّذِي اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ "We did not send down the Book to you except [for the purpose] that you may clarify for them what they differ about..." (16:64). The pronoun "them" (لهم) refers to the polytheists (المشركين). "What they differ about" refers to the truth (الحق) regarding belief and action. Thus, "clarification" (التبين) means elucidation and disclosure to complete the proof (إتمام الحجة). The evidence for what we have mentioned is the distinction made for the affair of the believers and their separate mention in His saying: وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ "...and as a guidance and mercy for a people who have faith." (16:64).

The meaning is: This is the state of people regarding their differences in true knowledge and divine laws; We have not sent down the Book to you except to disclose to these disputants the truth about which they differed, so that the proof may be completed against them, and so it may be a guidance (الهدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — and a mercy (الرحمة) for a people who believe, whom Allah guides thereby to the truth and encompasses with mercy through faith and action.

(Commentary on Narrations)

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from ʿAbd al-Rahman ibn Kathir, who said: I said to Abu ʿAbdillah (peace be upon him) regarding: فَاسْأَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِن كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ "Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know." (16:43). He said: الذكر محمد ونحن أهله المسئولون — "The Reminder (الذكر) is Muhammad, and we are his people who are to be questioned." (The hadith).

I say: He (peace be upon him) is referring to the saying of the Exalted: قَدْ أَنزَلَ اللَّهُ إِلَيْكُمْ ذِكْرًا رَّسُولًا "...Allah has already sent down to you a reminder, an apostle..." (65:10-11). There are many narrations with this meaning.

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from al-Barqi, with his chain from ʿAbd al-Karim ibn Abi al-Daylam, from Abu ʿAbdillah (peace be upon him), who said: The Majestic in His mention said: فَاسْأَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِن كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ "Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know." He said: الكتاب الذكر وأهله آل محمد عليه السلام ـ أمر الله عز وجل بسؤالهم ولم يأمر بسؤال الجهال ـ وسمى الله عز وجل القرآن ذكرا فقال تبارك وتعالى وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الذِّكْرَ لِتُبَيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ ما نُزِّلَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ ـ وقال تعالى : وَإِنَّهُ لَذِكْرٌ لَكَ وَلِقَوْمِكَ وَسَوْفَ تُسْئَلُونَ — "The Book is the Reminder (الذكر), and its people are the Progeny of Muhammad (peace be upon him). Allah, Mighty and Majestic, commanded that they be questioned and did not command the questioning of the ignorant. Allah, Mighty and Majestic, named the Quran a 'Reminder,' for the Blessed and Exalted said: 'We have sent down to you the Reminder that you may clarify for the people what has been sent down to them, and that they may reflect' (16:44). And the Exalted said: 'It is indeed a reminder for you and for your people, and you will be questioned' (43:44)."

I say: This is an argumentation for them being the "People of the Reminder" (أهل الذكر) based on the fact that the Reminder is the Quran, and they are its people because they are the people of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The two verses at the end of the speech serve as evidence for that, as explicitly stated in other narrations. There are other traditions with the same meaning as this hadith.

In Tafsir al-ʿAyyashi, from Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Jaʿfar (peace be upon him), who said: I said to him that some people among us claim that the saying of Allah, the Exalted: فَاسْأَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِن كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ "Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know," refers to the Jews and Christians. He said: إذا يدعونكم إلى دينهم — "Then they would invite you to their religion." He then said, pointing with his hand to his chest: نحن أهل الذكر ونحن المسئولون — "We are the People of the Reminder, and we are the ones to be questioned." Abu Jaʿfar (peace be upon him) said: الذكر القرآن — "The Reminder is the Quran."

I say: A similar explanation was narrated from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) in the assembly of al-Maʾmun.

It has already been mentioned that the address in the verse, according to what the context provides, is to the polytheists (المشركين) among the idolaters who deemed the messengership impossible. They were commanded to ask the People of the Reminder—who are the people of the Heavenly Books: Did Allah ever send men from among mortals as messengers to whom He revealed? It is well known that since the polytheists did not accept from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), there would be no sense in referring them to others among the people of the Quran, for they did not acknowledge the Quran as a Reminder from Allah. Thus, it is determined that those to be questioned, in view of the specific occasion of the verse, are the People of the Book, especially the Jews.

However, if the saying: فَاسْأَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِن كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ "Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know," is taken in itself, regardless of the specific occasion—and this is the nature of the Quran, as it is known that the occasion does not restrict the application—then the statement is general in terms of the asker, the one asked, and the subject of the question. The asker is anyone who may be ignorant of any of the true knowledge (المعارف) or legal issues (المسائل) among those burdened with religious duties (المكلفين). The subject of the question is all knowledge and issues that an ignorant person might not know. As for the one asked, although general in concept (المفهوم), they are specific in terms of the external referent (المصداق), and they are the People of the House (أهل البيت) of the Prophet (peace be upon him).

This is because if the "Reminder" (الذكر) refers to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), as in the verse of Surah al-Talaq, then they are the People of the Reminder. If it refers to the Quran, as in the verse of Surah al-Zukhruf, then it is a reminder for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his people—and they are his people, or the most certain of his people—so they are its people and its elite, and they are the ones to be questioned. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) paired them with the Quran and commanded the people to hold fast to both in the mutawatir (متواتر) — successively narrated and undeniably authentic — Hadith of the Two Weighty Things (حديث الثقلين), saying: إنهما لن يفترقا حتى يردا علي الحوض — "Indeed, the two shall never separate until they return to me at the Pond." (The hadith).

Evidence that their speech (peace be upon them) stems from the perspective we mentioned is their lack of engagement with the specific details of the verse's occasion.

From what we have presented, the fallacy of the objection raised by some against these hadiths becomes clear—namely, that the polytheists who were commanded to ask would not accept from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), so how could they accept from his Household?

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Jabir that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: لا ينبغي للعالم أن يسكت على علمه ، ولا ينبغي للجاهل أن يسكت على جهله وقد قال الله ـ فَسْئَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ ـ فينبغي للمؤمن أن يعرف عمله على هدى أم على خلافه — "It is not fitting for the scholar to remain silent regarding his knowledge, nor is it fitting for the ignorant to remain silent regarding his ignorance, for Allah has said: 'Ask the People of the Reminder if you do not know.' Thus, it is fitting for the believer to know whether his action is based on guidance or its opposite."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding the saying of the Exalted: أَفَأَمِنَ الَّذِينَ مَكَرُوا السَّيِّئَاتِ "Do those who devise evil schemes feel secure..." (16:45) until His saying: بِمُعْجِزِينَ "...[they cannot] frustrate [Him]?" (16:46). He (peace be upon him) said: إذا جاءوا وذهبوا في التجارات فيأخذهم في تلك الحالة أَوْ يَأْخُذَهُمْ عَلى تَخَوُّفٍ قال : قال : على تيقظ — "When they come and go in trade, He seizes them in that state, 'or that He should seize them through gradual diminishment' (16:47)." He said: "Meaning, while they are alert (تيقظ)."

In Tafsir al-ʿAyyashi, from Samaʿah, from Abu ʿAbdillah (peace be upon him), who said: I asked about the saying of Allah: وَلَهُ الدِّينُ وَاصِبًا "...and to Him belongs the religion (الدين) — the system of submission and worship — constantly (واصباً)." (16:52). He said: واجبا — "Obligatorily (واجباً)."

In al-Maʿani, with its chain from Hannan ibn Sadir, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) in a hadith where he said: وَلِلَّهِ الْمَثَلُ الْأَعْلَى — "And to Allah belongs the highest attribute (المثل الأعلى) (16:60)—that which nothing resembles, and which cannot be described or imagined."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying: وَلَوْ يُؤَاخِذُ اللَّهُ النَّاسَ بِظُلْمِهِم "Were Allah to take mankind to task for their wrongdoing..." (16:61). Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Hurayrah that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: لو أن الله يؤاخذني وعيسى بن مريم بذنوبنا ـ وفي لفظ : بما جنت هاتان الإبهام والتي تليها ـ لعذبنا ما يظلمنا شيئا — "If Allah were to take me and Jesus, son of Mary, to task for our sins—and in another wording: for what these two, the thumb and the one next to it, have committed—He would have punished us without wronging us in the least."

I say: This hadith contradicts what is established by the Book and the Sunnah regarding the infallibility (العصمة) — the divine protection from error and sin — of the Prophets (peace be upon them). There is no basis for interpreting it as referring to "leaving the better option" (ترك الأولى) among sins, for there is no punishment for that.

Ayahs

وَٱللَّهُ أَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَحْيَا بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَآ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَسْمَعُونَ (٦٥)
Allah sends down water from the sky with which He revives the earth after its death. There is indeed a sign in that for a people who listen. (65)
وَإِنَّ لَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ لَعِبْرَةً ۖ نُّسْقِيكُم مِّمَّا فِى بُطُونِهِۦ مِنۢ بَيْنِ فَرْثٍ وَدَمٍ لَّبَنًا خَالِصًا سَآئِغًا لِّلشَّـٰرِبِينَ (٦٦)
There is indeed a lesson for you in the cattle: We give you a drink pleasant to those who drink, pure milk, which is in their bellies, between [intestinal] waste and blood. (66)
وَمِن ثَمَرَٰتِ ٱلنَّخِيلِ وَٱلْأَعْنَـٰبِ تَتَّخِذُونَ مِنْهُ سَكَرًا وَرِزْقًا حَسَنًا ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (٦٧)
From the fruits of date palms and vines you draw wine and goodly provision. There are indeed signs in that for people who exercise their reason. (67)
وَأَوْحَىٰ رَبُّكَ إِلَى ٱلنَّحْلِ أَنِ ٱتَّخِذِى مِنَ ٱلْجِبَالِ بُيُوتًا وَمِنَ ٱلشَّجَرِ وَمِمَّا يَعْرِشُونَ (٦٨)
And your Lord inspired the bee [saying]: ‘Make your home in the mountains, and on the trees and the trellises that they erect. (68)
ثُمَّ كُلِى مِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ فَٱسْلُكِى سُبُلَ رَبِّكِ ذُلُلًا ۚ يَخْرُجُ مِنۢ بُطُونِهَا شَرَابٌ مُّخْتَلِفٌ أَلْوَٰنُهُۥ فِيهِ شِفَآءٌ لِّلنَّاسِ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (٦٩)
Then eat from every [kind of] fruit and follow meekly the ways of your Lord.’ There issues from its belly a juice of diverse hues, in which there is a cure for the people. There is indeed a sign in that for a people who reflect. (69)
وَٱللَّهُ خَلَقَكُمْ ثُمَّ يَتَوَفَّىٰكُمْ ۚ وَمِنكُم مَّن يُرَدُّ إِلَىٰٓ أَرْذَلِ ٱلْعُمُرِ لِكَىْ لَا يَعْلَمَ بَعْدَ عِلْمٍ شَيْـًٔا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ قَدِيرٌ (٧٠)
Allah has created you, then He takes you away, and there are some among you who are relegated to the nethermost age so that he knows nothing after [having possessed] some knowledge. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-powerful. (70)
وَٱللَّهُ فَضَّلَ بَعْضَكُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ فِى ٱلرِّزْقِ ۚ فَمَا ٱلَّذِينَ فُضِّلُوا۟ بِرَآدِّى رِزْقِهِمْ عَلَىٰ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُمْ فَهُمْ فِيهِ سَوَآءٌ ۚ أَفَبِنِعْمَةِ ٱللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ (٧١)
Allah has granted some of you an advantage over others in [respect of] provision. Those who have been granted an advantage do not give over their provision to their slaves so that they become equal in its respect. What, do they deny the blessing of Allah? (71)
وَٱللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنْ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَزْوَٰجًا وَجَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنْ أَزْوَٰجِكُم بَنِينَ وَحَفَدَةً وَرَزَقَكُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ ۚ أَفَبِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ يُؤْمِنُونَ وَبِنِعْمَتِ ٱللَّهِ هُمْ يَكْفُرُونَ (٧٢)
Allah made for you mates from your own selves and appointed for you children and grandchildren from your mates, and We provided you with all the good things. What, do they believe in falsehood while they deny the blessing of Allah? (72)
وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَمْلِكُ لَهُمْ رِزْقًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ (٧٣)
They worship besides Allah what has no power to provide them with anything from the heavens and the earth, nor are they capable [of anything]. (73)
فَلَا تَضْرِبُوا۟ لِلَّهِ ٱلْأَمْثَالَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٧٤)
So do not draw comparisons for Allah: indeed Allah knows and you do not know. (74)
۞ ضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا عَبْدًا مَّمْلُوكًا لَّا يَقْدِرُ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ وَمَن رَّزَقْنَـٰهُ مِنَّا رِزْقًا حَسَنًا فَهُوَ يُنفِقُ مِنْهُ سِرًّا وَجَهْرًا ۖ هَلْ يَسْتَوُۥنَ ۚ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ۚ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٧٥)
Allah draws a parable: a slave owned [by his master] who has no power over anything, and another one [a free man] whom We have provided a goodly provision and who spends out of it secretly and openly. Are they equal? All praise belongs to Allah. But most of them do not know. (75)
وَضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا رَّجُلَيْنِ أَحَدُهُمَآ أَبْكَمُ لَا يَقْدِرُ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ وَهُوَ كَلٌّ عَلَىٰ مَوْلَىٰهُ أَيْنَمَا يُوَجِّههُّ لَا يَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ ۖ هَلْ يَسْتَوِى هُوَ وَمَن يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْعَدْلِ ۙ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٧٦)
Allah draws [another] parable: Two men, one of whom is dumb, having no power over anything and who is a liability to his master: wherever he directs him he does not bring any good. Is he equal to someone who enjoins justice and is [steady] on a straight path? (76)
وَلِلَّهِ غَيْبُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَمَآ أَمْرُ ٱلسَّاعَةِ إِلَّا كَلَمْحِ ٱلْبَصَرِ أَوْ هُوَ أَقْرَبُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٧٧)
To Allah belongs the Unseen of the heavens and the earth. The matter of the Hour is just like the twinkling of an eye, or [even] swifter. Indeed Allah has power over all things. (77)

(Commentary)

This is a return, following a previous return, to enumerating the divine blessings and favors, deducing monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the Resurrection (البعث) from them, and pointing toward the matter of legislation (التشريع), which is prophethood (النبوة).

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَأَحْيا بِهِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها — "Allah sends down water from the sky and with it He revives the earth after its death" (16:65), etc. He intends the vegetation of the earth after it had ceased with the onset of winter, by means of the water from the sky, which is rain. Thus, the roots and seeds of plants begin to grow after their dormancy. This is a life of the same genus as animal life, even if it is weaker. Modern research has clarified that plants possess the same germs of life (جراثيم الحياة) as animals, though they differ in form (صورة) and effect (أثرا).

And His saying: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِقَوْمٍ يَسْمَعُونَ — "There is indeed a sign in that for a people who listen" (16:65). What is meant by "listening" (السمع) is the acceptance of that which is meant to be accepted of speech. For the rational person seeking the truth, when he hears that in which truth is expected, he pays heed and listens to it in order to comprehend and retain it. The Exalted said: الَّذِينَ يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقَوْلَ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ أَحْسَنَهُ أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَداهُمُ اللهُ وَأُولئِكَ هُمْ أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ — "those who listen to the word [of Allah] and follow the best [interpretation] of it. They are the ones whom Allah has guided, and it is they who possess intellect" (39:18).

Thus, when the account of Allah sending down rain and His reviving the earth after its death is mentioned to one who possesses the disposition (قريحة) to accept the truth, it constitutes for him a sign (آية) of the Resurrection—and that He who revived the earth is surely the Reviver of the dead.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّ لَكُمْ فِي الْأَنْعامِ لَعِبْرَةً — "There is indeed a lesson for you in the cattle" (16:66), etc. The "chyme" (الفرث) is the dregs that descend into the rumen and intestines; once it is expelled, it is dung (سرجين) and is no longer called farth. "Palatable" (السائغ) is the active participle of sawgh; it is said: "the food or drink was palatable" (ساغ) when it passes easily through the throat.

And His saying: وَإِنَّ لَكُمْ فِي الْأَنْعامِ لَعِبْرَةً means: there is for you in camels, cows, and sheep a matter by which you can take lesson (تعتبروا) and be admonished. He then explained that matter by His saying: نُسْقِيكُمْ مِمَّا فِي بُطُونِهِ — "We give you to drink of that which is in their bellies" (16:66), meaning the bellies of the aforementioned cattle (الأنعام), treating the plural as a single entity.

And His saying: مِنْ بَيْنِ فَرْثٍ وَدَمٍ — "from between chyme and blood" (16:66). The chyme is in the rumen, while the milk of cattle is located in the rear of the belly between the legs. Blood flows through the arteries and veins, which surround both. Thus, the milk is taken as something that is "between" the chyme and the blood, as if by virtue of its proximity to each and the gathering of all within the animal. This is like saying, "I chose Zayd from among (من بين) the people, called him, and brought him out from among them," when he was gathered with them in one place and was adjacent to them, even if he was sitting at the edge of the group and not in their midst. The meaning is that I distinguished him from among them when he was previously undistinguished.

The meaning is: We give you to drink from what is in its bellies a milk that emerges from between chyme and blood, pure (خالصا), neither mixed nor tainted by them, nor carrying any of their taste or smell, palatable (سائغا) for the drinkers. This is a lesson for whoever would take lesson and a means to knowledge of the perfection of Power and the efficacy of Will—and that He who purified the milk from between chyme and blood is surely capable of resurrecting man and reviving him after he has become decayed bones and his parts have vanished into the earth.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْ ثَمَراتِ النَّخِيلِ وَالْأَعْنابِ تَتَّخِذُونَ مِنْهُ سَكَراً وَرِزْقاً حَسَناً — "And of the fruits of the date palms and vines, you derive from them an intoxicant and a good provision" (16:67) to the end of the verse. It is stated in al-Mufradat: "Intoxicant (السكر) — with a damma on the sin — is a state that intervenes between a man and his intellect..." until he said: "and al-sakar — with two fathas — is that from which intoxication comes; the Exalted said: تَتَّخِذُونَ مِنْهُ سَكَراً وَرِزْقاً حَسَناً." End quote.

It is stated in al-Majma': "Intoxicant (السكر) in the language has four aspects: First, that which intoxicates of drink. Second, that which is eaten of food; the poet said: 'You made the faults of the noble ones an intoxicant (sakara),' meaning you made their disparagement your food. Third, tranquility (السكون), from which comes 'a tranquil night' (ليلة ساكرة), meaning still; the poet said: 'It is neither cold nor still (sakira)'; and it is said: 'the wind became still' (سكرت). He said: 'And he made the eye of the midday heat still (taskuru).' Fourth, the verbal noun from your saying: 'he became intoxicated' (سكر سكرا), and from it comes 'dazzling' (التسكير), which is confusion, in His saying: سُكِّرَتْ أَبْصارُنا — 'Our vision has been bedazzled' (15:15)." End quote. It appears that the primary meaning is the loss of intellect through the use of that which necessitates it, and the other meanings mentioned are derived from it through a type of metaphor (استعارة) and semantic extension (توسع).

And His saying: وَمِنْ ثَمَراتِ النَّخِيلِ وَالْأَعْنابِ is either a nominal sentence coordinated with His saying: وَاللهُ أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً, like His saying in the previous verse: وَإِنَّ لَكُمْ فِي الْأَنْعامِ لَعِبْرَةً. The implied structure would be: "And from the fruits of date palms and vines is that which — or something which — you derive from it..." They said: The Arabs often omit the relative pronoun ma (ما الموصولة); an example is His saying: وَإِذا رَأَيْتَ ثَمَّ رَأَيْتَ نَعِيماً وَمُلْكاً كَبِيراً — "As you look about, you will see bliss and a great kingdom" (76:20), where the implied meaning is "you will see that which is there." Or the implied structure is: "And from the fruits of date palms and vines is something you derive from it," based on the impermissibility of omitting the relative pronoun while retaining the relative clause, as held by the Basran grammarians.

Alternatively, it is a verbal sentence coordinated with His saying: أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ, as in the following verse: وَأَوْحى رَبُّكَ. The implied meaning would be: "He created for you" or "He gave you from the fruits of date palms and vines." His saying: تَتَّخِذُونَ مِنْهُ etc., would then be an appositive (بدل) or a new sentence (استئناف), as if someone asked: "What do we benefit from it?" and it was said: "You derive from it an intoxicant and a good provision." The use of the singular pronoun in مِنْهُ (from it) is by interpreting the mentioned items as a single category, similar to His saying: مِمَّا فِي بُطُونِهِ in the previous verse.

And His saying: تَتَّخِذُونَ مِنْهُ سَكَراً وَرِزْقاً حَسَناً means: you derive from the mentioned fruits of date palms and vines that which is an intoxicant (سكرا), such as various types of wine, and a "good provision" (رزقا حسنا), such as dates, raisins, molasses, and other things used as sustenance.

There is no indication in the verse for the permissibility of using intoxicants, nor for the approval of their use—if it does not indeed indicate a type of disapproval by contrasting it with "good provision." Rather, the verse is enumerating what they benefit from of the fruits of date palms and vines; it is a Meccan verse addressing the polytheists and calling them to monotheism.

Accordingly, the verse does not contain a legal ruling (حكم تكليفي) such that it could be abrogating (ناسخ) or abrogated (منسوخ). This reveals the error of the view that it was abrogated by the verse in Surah al-Ma'idah, as attributed to Qatadah.

The author of Ruh al-Ma'ani expressed a strange view when he said: "The interpretation of sakar as wine (الخمر) is what is narrated from Ibn Mas'ud, Ibn Umar, Abu Razin, al-Hasan, Mujahid, al-Sha'bi, al-Nakha'i, Ibn Abi Layla, Abu Thawr, al-Kalbi, and Ibn Jubayr, along with many others. The verse was revealed in Mecca, and wine at that time was permissible, consumed by both the righteous and the wicked. Its prohibition occurred in Medina by consensus, though they differed as to whether it was before the Battle of Uhud or after it. The verse that prohibited it is: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّمَا الْخَمْرُ وَالْمَيْسِرُ وَالْأَنْصابُ وَالْأَزْلامُ رِجْسٌ مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ فَاجْتَنِبُوهُ — 'O you who have faith! Indeed wine, gambling, idols, and the divining arrows are abominations of Satan’s doing, so avoid them' (5:90). According to a group, what is here is abrogated by it, and this was narrated by more than one of the aforementioned, such as al-Nakha'i, Abu Thawr, and Ibn Jubayr.

It was also said: It was revealed before and there is no abrogation, based on what is narrated from Ibn Abbas that sakar is vinegar (الخل) in the Ethiopic language, or based on what is reported from Abu Ubaydah that sakar is food enjoyed as a snack, like dried fruits; he cited the verse: 'You made the honor of the noble ones an intoxicant (sakara)...' until he said: 'The Hanafis held the view of non-abrogation, saying: What is meant by sakar is non-intoxicating beverages (الأنبذة). They argued that Allah, the Exalted, bestowed favor upon His servants with what He created for them of that, and favor is only bestowed through what is permissible. Thus, this would be evidence for the permissibility of drinking date-wine (النبيذ) that does not reach the level of intoxication; once it leads to intoxication, it is not permitted.'" End of the relevant portion.

As for what he mentioned regarding wine, we have detailed the discussion on that in the commentary on the verses of prohibition in Surah al-Ma'idah. We established evidence there that wine was prohibited before the Hijrah, and Islam was known for its prohibition and the prohibition of adultery among the polytheists in general. Its prohibition was revealed in Surah al-A'raf, which was certainly revealed before Surah al-Nahl, and in Surahs al-Baqarah and al-Nisa', both of which were revealed before Surah al-Ma'idah.

The verse revealed in al-Ma'idah was only revealed to intensify the prohibition and to deter some Muslims who were failing to comply with the ruling of prohibition, as occurs in the narrations. This is what is indicated by the saying "consumed by both the righteous and the wicked," and the wording of the verses points to this when it says: فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُنْتَهُونَ — "Will you then desist?" (5:91).

As for what he narrated from Ibn Abbas that sakar in the Ethiopic language means vinegar, it is not to be relied upon. While the use of non-Arabic words is not rare in the Quran—as has been said regarding istabraq, jahannam, zaqqum, and others—it is only permissible where there is no obstacle of confusion or ambiguity. As for a word like sakar, which in the Arabic language means wine and in Ethiopic means vinegar, this is not the case. How could it be permissible to attribute to the most eloquent of speech that it abandoned al-khall (vinegar), which is good Arabic, and used in its place an Ethiopic word that signifies in Arabic the opposite of its meaning?

As for what he attributed to Abu Ubaydah, its status has already been mentioned at the beginning of the discussion, so refer back to it.

As for what he attributed to the Hanafis—that what is meant by sakar is nabidh and that the verse indicates the permissibility of drinking a small amount of it as long as it does not reach the level of intoxication because it is mentioned as a favor—the response is that the verse indicates nothing more than that they "derive from it an intoxicant." As for bestowing favor (الامتنان) upon them for that, it is far from the indication of the verse. Rather, what was counted among the blessings were the fruits of date palms and vines, not everything they made from them, whether permissible or prohibited. Had there been a bestowal of favor in that, it would not have been contrasted with "good provision," which by its contrast indicates a type of reproach for their deriving an intoxicant from it, as acknowledged by al-Baydawi and others.

Furthermore, the word sakar in the verse is not restricted to being nabidh or wine, nor to a small amount that does not reach the level of intoxication, nor anything else. If the deriving of sakar were the object of the bestowal of favor—which indicates permissibility—the verse would be explicit in the lawfulness of all of it, and then it would not accept abrogation at all. For the language of bestowal of favor does not accept a time limit after which it is lifted. How could it be permissible for Allah to count something as one of His blessings and bestow favor upon people with it, and then after a while count it as an abomination and of Satan's doing, as in the verse of al-Ma'idah, except by way of bada' (بداء) — the appearance of a new divine decree — in its impossible sense regarding the Exalted?

Then He, the Glorified, concluded the verse by His saying: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ — "There is indeed a sign in that for a people who exercise their intellect" (16:67), as an encouragement to exercise the intellect and to reflect deeply upon the matter of plants and their fruits.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَوْحى رَبُّكَ إِلَى النَّحْلِ أَنِ اتَّخِذِي مِنَ الْجِبالِ بُيُوتاً — "And your Lord revealed to the bee: ‘Build homes in the mountains...’" (16:68) to the end of the two verses. Revelation (الوحي) — as al-Raghib said — is a swift indication. This can be through speech by way of a sign, or by a sound devoid of composition, or by a gesture and the like. What is gathered from the instances of its use is that it is the conveying of a meaning in a manner hidden from those other than the one intended to be informed. Thus, inspiration (الإلهام) by conveying a meaning to the understanding of an animal through the path of instinct (الغريزة) is a type of revelation (وحي). Likewise, the occurrence of a meaning in the soul through a vision (رؤيا), or through whispering (الوسوسة), or by gesture—all of these are revelation. It has been used in His speech, the Exalted, in each of these meanings, such as His saying: وَأَوْحى رَبُّكَ إِلَى النَّحْلِ (16:68), and His saying: وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلى أُمِّ مُوسى — "We revealed to Moses’ mother..." (28:7), and His saying: إِنَّ الشَّياطِينَ لَيُوحُونَ إِلى أَوْلِيائِهِمْ — "Indeed the devils inspire their friends..." (6:121), and His saying: فَأَوْحى إِلَيْهِمْ أَنْ سَبِّحُوا بُكْرَةً وَعَشِيًّا — "so he signaled to them to glorify [Allah] morning and evening" (19:11). Among the types of revelation is the divine speaking to His prophets and messengers; the Exalted said: وَما كانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَنْ يُكَلِّمَهُ اللهُ إِلَّا وَحْياً — "It is not [fitting] for any human that Allah should speak to him except by revelation..." (42:51). Religious etiquette in Islam has established that the term "revelation" (الوحي) should not be applied to anything other than the divine speaking to the prophets and messengers.

It is stated in al-Majma': "al-dhulul (ذلل) is the plural of dhalul (ذلول); it is said: 'a submissive beast' (دابة ذلول) between submission (dhull) and 'a submissive man' (رجل ذلول) between submission (dhull) and humility (dhilla)." End quote.

And His saying: وَأَوْحى رَبُّكَ إِلَى النَّحْلِ means: He inspired it through the path of its instinct (غريزته) which He deposited in its constitution. The matter of the bee—which is the honey wasp—in its social life, its conduct, and its craft is truly wondrous. Perhaps the marvelous nature of its affair is what necessitated turning the address from them to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) when He said: وَأَوْحى رَبُّكَ (And your Lord revealed).

And His saying: أَنِ اتَّخِذِي مِنَ الْجِبالِ بُيُوتاً وَمِنَ الشَّجَرِ وَمِمَّا يَعْرِشُونَ — "‘Build homes in the mountains, and in the trees, and in what they erect’" (16:68). This is the content of the revelation that was revealed to it. It appears that what is meant by "what they erect" (ما يعرشون) is what they build for the honeycombs.

And His saying: ثُمَّ كُلِي مِنْ كُلِّ الثَّمَراتِ — "‘Then eat from every [kind of] fruit’" (16:69). The command to eat from all fruits, even though they mostly land on flowers, is because they eat from the materials of the fruits when they first form in the bellies of the flowers and as they grow and ripen.

And His saying: فَاسْلُكِي سُبُلَ رَبِّكِ ذُلُلاً — "‘and follow the ways of your Lord, [which have been] made easy [for you]’" (16:69). Its derivation from the command to eat supports that what is meant is its return to its homes to deposit therein what it has prepared of the honey taken from the fruits. The attribution of the "ways" (السبل) to the "Lord" (الرب) is to indicate that all of it is by divine inspiration.

And His saying: يَخْرُجُ مِنْ بُطُونِها شَرابٌ مُخْتَلِفٌ أَلْوانُهُ — "There issues from their bellies a drink of varying colours" (16:69), etc. This is a new sentence after mentioning the sum of what it was commanded, explaining what results from its striving in obeying the command of Allah, the Glorified, submissively (ذللا). It is that there issues from their bellies—meaning the bellies of the bees—a "drink" (شراب), which is honey, "of varying colours" (مختلف ألوانه), including white, yellow, bright red, and that which tends toward black, فِيهِ شِفاءٌ لِلنَّاسِ — "in which there is a cure for the people" (16:69) from most diseases.

The detailed discussion of the life of the bee—this intelligent insect that has built its life upon a wondrous and virtuous civilization whose marvels can hardly be counted and whose subtleties cannot be fully encompassed—and then what it prepares through its extreme striving and the properties it contains, is beyond the scope of this book. One should refer to the appropriate sources for that research.

Then He concluded the verse by His saying: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ — "There is indeed a sign in that for a people who reflect" (16:69). The expression has varied in these verses: He specified the sign in the revival of the earth after its death for "a people who listen," in the fruits of date palms and vines for "a people who exercise their intellect," and in the matter of the bee for "a people who reflect."

Perhaps the reason for this is that looking into the matter of death and life, by its nature, is a source of lesson (عبرة) and admonition (موعظة), which is more suited to "listening." Looking into the fruits—in terms of what benefits man in his existence through demonstrative reasoning from the path of the continuity of governance and the connection of partial systems and their return to a single general system that can only be maintained by a single Governor—is more suited to the "intellect" (العقل). The matter of the bee in its life contains wondrous subtleties that are not revealed to man except through deep "reflection" (التفكر); thus, it is a sign for those who reflect.

We have previously pointed out the various shifts in address (التفاتات) in the verses of this Surah. The core of them in these verses returns to addressing the polytheists out of mercy and compassion for their state while they do not know, and turning away from addressing them due to their disbelief and denial toward addressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). This is clearly observed in the verses of the Surah; the address within it continues to fluctuate between the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the polytheists, shifting from him to them and from them to him.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ خَلَقَكُمْ ثُمَّ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ وَمِنْكُمْ مَنْ يُرَدُّ إِلى أَرْذَلِ الْعُمُرِ لِكَيْ لا يَعْلَمَ بَعْدَ عِلْمٍ شَيْئاً — "Allah has created you, then He takes you away, and some of you are relegated to the most abject age, so that they do not know anything after [having possessed] knowledge" (16:70), etc. "The most abject" (الأرذل) is a superlative from radhala, which is badness or vileness; al-radhl is the lowly and the bad. What is meant by "the most abject age" (أرذل العمر), by the context of His saying: "so that they do not know..." etc., is the age of senility and decrepitude in which there is a decline in the powers of consciousness and perception. This varies according to different temperaments and generally begins from the age of seventy-five.

The meaning is: Allah created you, O people, then He takes you away in a middle age, and some of you are relegated to the age of decrepitude, ending up knowing nothing after having possessed knowledge due to the weakness of the faculties. This is a sign that your life and death, as well as your consciousness and knowledge, are not in your hands; otherwise, you would have chosen remaining over passing away, and knowledge over its absence. Rather, that—with its wondrous system—goes back to His knowledge and His power, the Exalted. For this reason, He justified it by His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ عَلِيمٌ قَدِيرٌ — "Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-powerful" (16:70).

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ فَضَّلَ بَعْضَكُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ فِي الرِّزْقِ — "Allah has graced some of you above others in provision" (16:71) to the end of the verse. Gracing some people over others in provision (الرزق) — that which sustains life — may be in terms of quantity, such as the wealthy person graced with much wealth over the poor person. Or it may be in terms of quality, such as some of them being independent in disposing of it while others are under their charge, like the free master being independent in owning what is in his hand and disposing of it, unlike his slave who has no right to dispose of anything except by his permission. The same applies to young children in relation to their guardian, and cattle and livestock in relation to their owner.

His saying: فَمَا الَّذِينَ فُضِّلُوا بِرَادِّي رِزْقِهِمْ عَلى ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُهُمْ — "Yet those who have been graced do not hand over their provision to their slaves" (16:71) is a context indicating that what is meant is the second type of gracing. It is that some of them were graced with freedom and independence in owning what they were provided, and they do not choose to hand over what they were provided through their independence and freedom to those whom they own and whose provision they also own. Nor do they bestow upon them the blessing they were given so that they might become equal and partners, thereby nullifying their ownership and losing their lordship.

This is a blessing they do not overlook, nor do they return it to others. It is from none other than Allah, the Glorified.
