For the matter of lordship and slavery, even if it is among the social affairs that emerged from the opinions of people and the social norms current in their societies, it has natural, ontological (تكوينية) roots that prompted their opinions to consider it, like all other general social matters.

A witness to this is that the advanced nations long ago announced the abolition of the custom of slavery, and then other nations from the East and elsewhere followed them. Yet they still respect its meaning to this day, even if they have abolished its form; they carry out its essence even if they have abandoned its name. They will continue to do so, for it is not within the power of man to close the door of competition and dominance. We have previously presented a discussion on this meaning at the end of the sixth volume of this book, so let whoever wishes refer to it.

The fact that this meaning is a blessing from Allah is because it is for the benefit of human society that some should have authority over others, so that the strong may improve the weak through sound governance and complete them.

Accordingly, His saying: فَهُمْ فِيهِ سَواءٌ — "so that they may be equal in it" (16:71) is branched from the negation in His saying: فَمَا الَّذِينَ فُضِّلُوا بِرَادِّي رِزْقِهِمْ (Yet those who have been graced do not hand over their provision), rather than the negation itself. The meaning is: they do not hand over their provision to their slaves such that they would be equal in it and partners, for in that would be the disappearance of their lordship. It is also possible that it is an interrogative sentence from which the interrogative particle has been omitted, containing a denial (إنكار) that the graced and those graced over them are equal in that. If they were equal, the graced one would not refrain from handing over his provision to the one he was graced over, for in that is an indication that it is a blessing Allah has specified for him.

Therefore, He followed it secondly by His saying: أَفَبِنِعْمَةِ اللهِ يَجْحَدُونَ — "Do they then deny the blessing of Allah?" (16:71), which is a reproachful interrogation (استفهام توبيخي) branched from the preceding denial. What is meant by "the blessing of Allah" is this very gracing mentioned.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: Allah has distinguished between you by gracing some of you over others in provision; some of you are free and independent in disposing of it, and some of you are slaves following them, not disposing except by permission. Those who were graced are not about to hand over their provision—which they were provided by way of freedom and independence—to their slaves so that these graced ones and those graced over them would be equal in provision. They are not equal; rather, it is a blessing specific to the graced ones. Do they then deny the blessing of Allah?

This is what the apparent meaning of the verse provides, given the surrounding contexts. The context is one of enumerating blessings.

The meaning of the verse has sometimes been established in another way:

It was said: The meaning is that they do not share their wealth and wives with their slaves so that they would be equal in that, and they see that as a deficiency for themselves. How then do they associate My slaves with Me in My kingdom and My authority, worshipping them and seeking proximity to them as they worship Me and seek proximity to Me, as they did with Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon him)?

They said: The verse is in the mold of His saying, the Exalted: ضَرَبَ لَكُمْ مَثَلاً مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ هَلْ لَكُمْ مِنْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُكُمْ مِنْ شُرَكاءَ فِي ما رَزَقْناكُمْ فَأَنْتُمْ فِيهِ سَواءٌ — "He draws for you a similitude from your own selves: Do you have among your slaves any partners in the provision We have given you, so that you are equal in it..." (30:28). They said: The verse was revealed regarding the Christians of Najran.

The response to this is that the context of the verse is one of enumerating blessings to deduce monotheism, not one of contradiction and reproach; there is no trace of that in it.

Furthermore, the verse is among those revealed in Mecca, and how far is that from the arrival of the Christians of Najran in Medina in the sixth year of the Hijrah or after it? Comparing this verse to the verse in Surah al-Rum is a comparison with a difference due to the different contexts: the context of this verse is an argument by mentioning a blessing, while the context of the verse in al-Rum is a reproach for polytheism (الشرك).

It was also said: The meaning is that these whom Allah graced in provision among the free people do not provide for their servants and slaves; rather, Allah, the Exalted, is the Provider of both the owners and the owned. For what the master spends on his slave, he only spends from what Allah has provided them; thus, Allah is the Provider of them all, and they are equal in it.

The summary of this is that His saying: فَهُمْ فِيهِ سَواءٌ takes the place of an implied digression (إضراب). The implied meaning is that the masters do not hand over their own provision to their slaves in what they spend on them; rather, Allah provides for the slaves through the hands of their masters, and they are equal in the provision from Allah.

The response to this is that the meaning thus established necessitates that the equality at the end nullifies the ruling of gracing at the beginning, and the significance of His saying: أَفَبِنِعْمَةِ اللهِ يَجْحَدُونَ would not be sound under it.

It was also said: What is meant is that the masters do not hand over the provision in their hands to their masters so that they might be equal in enjoying it.

The response to this is that it then returns to the idea that man prevents others from having authority over the provision he owns. In that case, specifying this to slaves would be redundant. If it were justified on the grounds that he does not hand it over to him because of his authority over his slaves, it would return to the meaning we first presented, and the blessing counted would be the grace from the perspective of the master's ownership of his slave and what he possesses of provision.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَزْواجاً وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ بَنِينَ وَحَفَدَةً — "Allah has made for you mates from your own selves, and He has made for you sons and grandsons from your mates" (16:72) to the end of the verse. It is stated in al-Mufradat: "Allah the Exalted said: وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ بَنِينَ وَحَفَدَةً. Hafada (حفدة) is the plural of hafid (حافد), which is one who moves quickly in service, whether they are relatives or strangers. The commentators said: they are the grandsons and their like, because their service is more sincere..." until he said: "al-Asma'i said: the root of al-hafd is the quickening of the pace." End quote.

In al-Majma': "The root of al-hafd is speed in work..." until he said: "From it, assistants are called hafada because of their speed in obedience." End quote. What is meant by hafada in the verse are the assisting servants from among the sons, due to His saying: وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ. Therefore, some interpreted His saying: بَنِينَ وَحَفَدَةً as young children and grown ones, and some as sons and grandsons (الأسباط), who are the sons of sons.

The meaning is: Allah has made for you mates from your own selves whom you find comfort in and are intimate with, and He has made for you from your mates, through procreation, sons and grandsons and assistants whose service you seek for your needs and through whom you repel hardships from yourselves. And He has provided you with "good things" (الطيبات), which are what you find pleasant of the enjoyments of life and obtain without treatment or labor, like water and fruits, or with treatment and labor, like food and clothing and the like. The "from" (مِنْ) in مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ is for partitivity (التبعيض), which is apparent.

Then He reproached them by His saying: أَفَبِالْباطِلِ — "Do they then believe in falsehood" (16:72)—which are the idols and images, and from that is the claim of daughters for Allah, and the laws legislated for them by their leaders, the leaders of misguidance—يُؤْمِنُونَ وَبِنِعْمَتِ اللهِ هُمْ يَكْفُرُونَ — "and deny the blessing of Allah?" (16:72). The blessing is the making of mates from their own selves and the making of sons and grandsons from their mates, for that is among the greatest and most manifest of blessings, being an ontological foundation upon which human society is built. Through it, the principle of cooperation and mutual support among individuals appears, and the matter of sharing labors and efforts is organized for them, making it easy for them to attain their happiness in this world and the Hereafter.

If man were to cut this ontological bond with which Allah has blessed him and abandon this beautiful means—even if he were to resort to any other means—his gathering would vanish and his unity would be scattered, and in that would be the destruction of humanity.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ ما لا يَمْلِكُ لَهُمْ رِزْقاً مِنَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ شَيْئاً وَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ — "They worship besides Allah what has no power to provide them with any provision from the heavens and the earth, nor are they able to do so" (16:73). This is coordinated with the position of the previous sentence. The meaning is: they deny the blessing of Allah and worship besides Allah what does not own, etc.

They have mentioned that "provision" (رِزْقاً) is a verbal noun and "anything" (شَيْئاً) is its object; the meaning is: it does not own for them the providing of anything. It was also said: "provision" is in the sense of the thing provided, and "anything" is an appositive (بدل) for it. It was also said: "anything" is a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق), and the implied meaning is: it does not own anything of ownership. The best of these views is the middle one.

It could be said: مِنَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ شَيْئاً is an appositive for رِزْقاً, and it is a "substitution of the whole for the part" (بدل الكل من البعض), signifying a sense of digression and intensification. The meaning is: they worship what does not own for them provision; rather, it does not own for them anything in the heavens and the earth.

And His saying: وَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ means: nor are they able to own provision or anything. It is possible that the object is omitted, making it function as an intransitive verb, meaning: they have no ability at all.

The verse has combined the consideration of both aspects regarding the idols: from the perspective that they are made of stone, wood, gold, or silver, they are non-rational, and in this regard it was said: ما لا يَمْلِكُ (what does not own), etc. From the perspective that they consider them gods besides Allah and worship them—and worship is only for a rational being—they are placed, in their claim, in the rank of rational beings, and in this regard it was said: وَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ (nor are they able).

In the verse, there is a return to the transition (التخلص) to explain the purpose of enumerating the blessings, which is monotheism and the proof of prophethood in the sense of legislation and the Return (المعاد). This continues through four verses: in the first, He forbids them from striking similitudes for Allah, the Glorified; in the second, He strikes a similitude by which His oneness in Lordship is clarified; in the third, a similitude by which the matter of prophethood and legislation is clarified; and in the fourth, He addresses the matter of the Return.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلا تَضْرِبُوا لِلَّهِ الْأَمْثالَ إِنَّ اللهَ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ — "So do not coin similitudes for Allah. Indeed Allah knows and you do not know" (16:74). What immediately comes to mind is that what is meant by "coining similitudes" (ضرب الأمثال) is the conventional description through representative metaphor (الاستعارة التمثيلية). This is the applying of attributes to Him, the Exalted, through a type of comparison, such as their saying: He has daughters like man, and the angels are His daughters, and that there is kinship and marriage between Him and the Jinn, and how He will revive bones when they are decayed, and so on. This is the well-known meaning of this phrase in His speech, the Exalted. It has already preceded in the previous verses His saying: لِلَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخيرةِ مَثَلُ السَّوْءِ وَلِلَّهِ الْمَثَلُ الْأَعْلى — "For those who do not believe in the Hereafter is an evil description, and to Allah belongs the highest description" (16:60).

The meaning is: since the matter is as mentioned, do not describe Him, the Glorified, by that which you liken to others and compare to His creation, because Allah knows and you do not know the realities of things nor His essence, the Exalted.

It was said: What is meant by "coining" (الضرب) is making, and by "similitudes" (الأمثال) what is the plural of mathal in the sense of a peer (الند). Thus, His saying: فَلا تَضْرِبُوا لِلَّهِ الْأَمْثالَ is in the sense of His saying in another place: فَلا تَجْعَلُوا لِلَّهِ أَنْداداً — "so do not set up equals to Allah" (2:22). This is a remote meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: ضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً عَبْداً مَمْلُوكاً لا يَقْدِرُ عَلى شَيْءٍ — "Allah draws a similitude: a slave owned [by another] who has no power over anything" (16:75) to the end of the verse. The similitude struck in the verse posits a slave who is owned and has no power over anything, and another who has been provided by Allah with a good provision from which he spends secretly and openly. Then it asks: "Are they equal?" The consideration of the contrast between the two posited cases implies that each side is restricted by the opposite of the attribute in the other, with the attributes clarifying one another.

The posited slave is owned (مملوك), not an owner (مالك), neither of himself nor of any of the enjoyments of life, and he is unable to dispose of any wealth. The one posited in contrast is free, owning himself, and Allah has provided him with a good provision from which he spends secretly and openly, possessing the power to dispose in all ways.

His saying: هَلْ يَسْتَوُونَ — "Are they equal?" (16:75) is a question about their equality. It is self-evident that the answer is the negation of equality, and through it, it is established that Allah, the Glorified—who is the Owner of everything and the Bestower of all blessings—is not equal to any of His creation, who own neither themselves nor others and have no power over any disposal. Thus, their saying that there are other gods with Allah who are from His creation is false.

The expression يَسْتَوُونَ (are they equal - plural) rather than yastawiyan (dual) is to indicate that what is meant is the genus, without being specific to a particular master and slave, as has been said.

His saying: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ — "All praise belongs to Allah" (16:75) means: to Him, exalted is His Name, belongs the genus of praise and its reality. It is the commendation for a voluntary beauty, because the beauty of blessing is from Him, and only the beautiful is praised. Thus, to Him, the Exalted, belongs all praise, just as to Him belongs its genus—so understand that.

The sentence is part of the completion of the argument. Its summary is that the owned one who has no power to dispose of anything or bestow anything is not equal to the Owner who owns the provision and has power to dispose of it, so He disposes and bestows as He wills. Allah, the Glorified, is the One praised with every praise, since there is no blessing except that it is from His creation. To Him belongs every attribute for which He is praised, such as creation, provision, mercy, forgiveness, excellence, and bestowal. To Him belongs every beautiful commendation, while what they worship besides Him is owned and has no power over anything. Thus, He, the Glorified, is the Lord alone, without any other.

It has been said: The praise in the verse is gratitude for His blessings, the Exalted. It was also said: It is praise for the completion and strength of the argument. It was also said: It is a prompting for the servants, and its meaning is: they said, "All praise belongs to Allah who guided us to His monotheism and guided us to gratitude for His blessings." these are views that are not to be given weight.

And His saying: بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ — "But most of them do not know" (16:75), meaning most of the polytheists do not know that all blessing is for Allah, and that none other than Him owns anything or has power over anything. Rather, they establish for their "guardians" (أوليائهم) something of ownership and power by way of delegation (التفويض), so they worship them out of greed and fear. This is the state of most of them; as for the few among the elite, they have knowledge of the truth but they deviate from it out of transgression and stubbornness.

It has become clear from what preceded that the verse is a similitude struck regarding Allah, the Glorified, and those whom they claim as partners to Him in Lordship. It was said: It is a similitude representing the state of the forsaken disbeliever and the successful believer. For the disbeliever, due to the nullification of his deeds and the lack of regard for his actions, is like the owned slave who has no power over anything; no excellence is counted for him even if he spends and goes to extremes. This is in contrast to the believer whom Allah grants success for His pleasure and whose efforts He appreciates; he spends from the good he has, secretly and openly.

The response to this is that it does not suit the context of argumentation (الاحتجاج) of the verses. It has already been mentioned that the verse is one of three consecutive verses that address the purpose of enumerating divine blessings. It reminds of monotheism through a similitude that compares the state of the One who bestows all blessings with the state of the one who owns nothing and has no power over anything, thus concluding that the Lord is the Bestower, and none other.

His saying, the Exalted: وَضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً رَجُلَيْنِ أَحَدُهُما أَبْكَمُ — "Allah draws a similitude: two men, one of whom is dumb..." (16:76) to the end of the verse. It is stated in al-Majma': "The dumb (الأبكم) is the one born mute, who neither understands nor is understood. It was also said: the dumb is the one who cannot speak. The 'burden' (الكل) is the weight; it is said: 'he was burdened (كل) by the matter' when it became heavy upon him and he did not rouse himself for it. A knife 'became dull' (كلت) when its blade became thick. His tongue 'became tied' (كل) when it did not rouse for speech due to its thickness and the loss of its sharpness. The root of it is the thickness that prevents penetration. 'Directing' (التوجيه) is sending in a direction of the path; it is said: 'I directed him to such a place' and he headed toward it." End quote.

His saying: وَضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً رَجُلَيْنِ is another comparison between two posited men who are opposites in their mentioned attributes.

His saying: أَحَدُهُما أَبْكَمُ لا يَقْدِرُ عَلى شَيْءٍ means: he is deprived of understanding speech and making others understand through speech because he is dumb, neither hearing nor speaking. He lacks all the actualities and advantages that man acquires through the path of hearing, which is the widest of the senses in scope. Through it, man is able to know the news of those who passed, what is hidden from sight of events, and what is in the minds of people; he learns sciences and crafts. Through it, he is able to convey the sublime and subtle meanings he perceives to others. The dumb person is not strong enough to perceive any of these except for the very little that sight assists with through the aid of gestures.

Thus, His saying: لا يَقْدِرُ عَلى شَيْءٍ (has no power over anything) has its generality specified by the dumbness; i.e., he has no power over anything that the non-dumb person has power over, which is the sum of what the dumb person is deprived of in receiving and conveying information.

His saying: وَهُوَ كَلٌّ عَلى مَوْلاهُ means: he is a weight and a dependent upon the one who manages and directs his affairs, for he cannot manage his own affair. His saying: أَيْنَما يُوَجِّهْهُ لا يَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ means: to whatever direction his master sends him for a need of his own or a need of his master, he is unable to fulfill it. He cannot benefit others just as he does not benefit himself. This—meaning His saying: أَحَدُهُما أَبْكَمُ لا يَقْدِرُ عَلى شَيْءٍ etc.—is the similitude of one of the two men. He, the Glorified, did not mention the similitude of the other because knowledge of it is obtained from His saying: هَلْ يَسْتَوِي هُوَ وَمَنْ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ etc. In this is a subtle conciseness (إيجاز).

His saying: هَلْ يَسْتَوِي هُوَ وَمَنْ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَهُوَ عَلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ — "Is he equal to one who enjoins justice and is on a straight path?" (16:76). In this is an indication of the attribute of the posited man and a question about their equality when compared, or the lack thereof.

As for the attribute, He mentioned for him the ultimate of what a non-dumb person can be adorned with of good and perfection that beautifies his soul and extends to others. This is justice (العدل), which is the adherence to the middle path in actions and the avoidance of excess (الإفراط) and deficiency (التفريط). For the enjoining of justice, if it proceeds according to its reality, necessitates that righteousness (الصلاح) becomes established in the soul of the person and then spreads over his actions, so he adheres to moderation in matters. Then he loves its spreading over the actions of other people, so he enjoins them with justice—which is, as you have known, the absolute avoidance of excess and deficiency; i.e., righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) in a sense broader than justice among the subjects.

Then He described him by His saying: وَهُوَ عَلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ — "and is on a straight path" (16:76). This is the clear way that guides its travelers to their goal without crookedness. The person who, in the journey of his life, is on a straight path (صراط مستقيم) proceeds in his actions according to the human primordial nature (الفطرة الإنسانية) without some of his actions contradicting others or him failing in anything he sees as truth. In short, there is no failure or inconsistency in his actions.

Describing this posited man who enjoins justice as being on a straight path signifies, firstly, that his enjoining of justice is not of the type of enjoining people with righteousness while forgetting himself; rather, he is upright in his states and actions, performing justice just as he enjoins it.

Secondly, his enjoining of justice is not an innovation from him without an origin upon which it is built; rather, he is in himself upon the straight path, and its necessity is that he loves that for others, so he enjoins them to adhere to the middle of the road and avoid the two edges of excess and deficiency.

As for the question—meaning what is in His saying: هَلْ يَسْتَوِي هُوَ وَمَنْ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ etc.—it is a question that has no answer but negation, without doubt. Through it, it is established that what they worship besides Allah of idols and images—which is stripped of power, unable to be guided by itself nor to guide others—is not equal to Allah, the Exalted, who is on a straight path in Himself, guiding others by sending messengers and legislating laws.

From this, it appears that this similitude struck in the verse is in the sense of His saying, the Exalted: أَفَمَنْ يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ أَحَقُّ أَنْ يُتَّبَعَ أَمَّنْ لا يَهِدِّي إِلَّا أَنْ يُهْدى فَما لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ — "Is He who guides to the truth worthier to be followed, or he who is not guided unless he is shown the way? What is the matter with you? How do you judge?" (10:35). Allah, the Glorified, is on a straight path in His attributes and His acts. Among the rectitude of His path is that He makes for what He created of things goals toward which they head, so that the creation is not in vain, as He said: وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما باطِلاً — "We did not create the sky and the earth and whatever is between them in vain" (38:27). And that He guides each to its specific goal just as He created it and made for it a goal, as He said: الَّذِي أَعْطى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى — "He who gave everything its creation and then guided it" (20:50). Thus, He guides man to a direct way, as He said: وَعَلَى اللهِ قَصْدُ السَّبِيلِ — "With Allah rests the [guidance to the] straight path" (16:9), and He said: إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ — "Indeed We have guided him to the way" (76:3).

This is the root of the argument for prophethood and legislation. Its completion has passed in the discussions on prophethood in the second volume and in the stories of Noah in the tenth volume of the book.

Thus, it is concluded that the purpose of the similitude struck in the verse is the establishment of an argument for monotheism, with an indication toward prophethood and legislation.

It was said: It is a similitude struck regarding the one from whom good is hoped and the one from whom it is not hoped. The root of all good is from Allah, the Exalted; how then can equality be made between Him and anything else in worship?

The response to this is that the instance is more specific than that. It is a similitude struck regarding the one who is upon good in himself and enjoins justice, and that is His affair, the Exalted, and none other. Furthermore, they do not make equality between Him and others in worship; rather, they leave Him and worship others.

It was said: It is a similitude struck regarding the believer and the disbeliever. The dumb one is the disbeliever, and the one who enjoins justice is the believer. The response to this is that the correctness of applying the verse to the believer and the disbeliever—indeed, to everyone who enjoins justice and everyone who remains silent from it—and its applicability to them is one thing. Its significance by virtue of its occurrence in the context of enumerating blessings and arguing for monotheism and the principles that follow it is another thing. What it signifies, considering this aspect, is that the subject of the similitude is Allah, the Glorified, and what they worship besides Him, and none other.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِلَّهِ غَيْبُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَما أَمْرُ السَّاعَةِ إِلَّا كَلَمْحِ الْبَصَرِ أَوْ هُوَ أَقْرَبُ إِنَّ اللهَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ — "To Allah belongs the Unseen of the heavens and the earth. The matter of the Hour is but like a twinkle of an eye, or even swifter. Indeed Allah has power over all things" (16:77). The Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — is contrasted with the manifest (الشهادة) in the usage of the Noble Quran. It has been repeated therein: عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهادَةِ — "the Knower of the Unseen and the manifest." It has been mentioned repeatedly that they are two relative matters: a single thing is "unseen" and "absent" in relation to one thing, and "manifest" and "witnessed" in relation to another.

Since among things are those that possess aspects by which they appear to others and aspects by which they are hidden—meaning they contain both an unseen and a manifest—the attribution of the Unseen and the manifest to a thing is sometimes in the sense of the lam (possession). For example, "the Unseen of the heavens and the earth" would be what is absent from them, outside of their boundaries. To this category belongs the attribution for a type of specificity, as in His saying: فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً — "and He does not reveal His Unseen to anyone" (72:26).

At other times, it is in the sense of "from" (من) or what is close to it. Thus, what is meant by "the Unseen of the heavens and the earth" is the Unseen that they contain in a certain way, in contrast to the manifest that they contain. In other words, what is hidden from understandings of their affair in contrast to what appears of them.

The Hour (الساعة) is from the Unseen of the heavens and the earth in this second sense:

Firstly: Because He, the Glorified, counts it in His speech as being from the Unseen, and it is not outside the affair of the heavens and the earth; thus, it is from the Unseen in this sense.

Secondly: Because the attributes with which He describes it only suit this second sense, such as its being a day in which Allah informs them of that wherein they used to differ, and the day when the secrets are tested, and a day in which man is addressed with the like of His saying: لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ — "‘You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today’" (50:22), and a day in which they address their Lord saying: رَبَّنا أَبْصَرْنا وَسَمِعْنا فَارْجِعْنا — "‘Our Lord! We have seen and heard; so send us back...’" (32:12). In short, it is a day in which what was hidden of realities in this world appears as a visual manifestation. It is known that these realities are not outside the heavens and the earth; rather, they are established with them.

How so? When He, the Exalted, says: وَلِلَّهِ غَيْبُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ, He establishes it as a possession for Himself. His ownership is not of the conventional ownership (الملك الاعتباري) that attaches to every illusory or arbitrary matter; rather, it is a real ownership (ملك حقيقي) that attaches to an established matter. Thus, they have a type of establishment, even if we assume our ignorance of the reality of their establishment.

The Quranic witnesses for what we have mentioned are many. He, the Glorified, has counted the life of this world as the enjoyment of delusion, and play and diversion. He repeated that most people do not know what the Day of Resurrection is. He mentioned that the abode of the Hereafter is the [true] life, and that they will know that Allah is the Manifest Truth, and there will appear to them from Allah what they had not expected—among other things contained in the verses in their various expressions.

In short, the Hour is from the Unseen of the heavens and the earth. The verse—meaning His saying: وَلِلَّهِ غَيْبُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ—establishes His ownership, the Exalted, of this Unseen itself, not just His knowledge of it. He did not say: "And to Allah belongs the knowledge of the Unseen of the heavens and the earth." The context of the verse indicates that the sentence—meaning His saying: وَلِلَّهِ غَيْبُ etc.—is a preamble and preparation for His saying: ما أَمْرُ السَّاعَةِ إِلَّا كَلَمْحِ الْبَصَرِ etc. Thus, the sentence is presented for the purpose of argument.

Accordingly, the meaning of the verse returns to the fact that Allah, the Glorified, owns the Unseen of the heavens and the earth with an ownership that allows Him to dispose of it as He wills, just as He owns their manifest. And how could it be otherwise? The Unseen of a thing does not separate from its manifest; it is existent and established with it, and to Him belongs the creation and the command. The promised Hour is not an impossible matter such that power would not attach to it; rather, it is from the Unseen of the heavens and the earth, and its reality is hidden from understandings today. Thus, it is among that upon which His ownership, the Exalted, is settled, and He has the right to dispose of it by hiding it one day and manifesting it another.

It is not difficult for Him, the Exalted; for its matter is but like a twinkle of an eye or even swifter than that, because Allah has power over all things.

From here, it appears that His saying: وَما أَمْرُ السَّاعَةِ إِلَّا كَلَمْحِ الْبَصَرِ أَوْ هُوَ أَقْرَبُ إِنَّ اللهَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ is presented not to establish the origin of the Hour or its possibility, but to negate its difficulty and the hardship in its establishment, and the insignificance of its matter before Him, the Glorified.

Thus, His saying: وَما أَمْرُ السَّاعَةِ إِلَّا كَلَمْحِ الْبَصَرِ أَوْ هُوَ أَقْرَبُ means: in relation to Him. Otherwise, He, the Glorified, has magnified its matter by that which makes every grave matter seem insignificant before it, and He described it with attributes that no other equals, saying: ثَقُلَتْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "It is heavy in the heavens and the earth" (7:187).

The comparison of its matter to a twinkle of an eye is only because the twinkle—which is the extending of the sight and sending it for vision—is the lightest of actions for man and the shortest of them in time. Thus, it is a comparison according to the understanding of the listener. For this reason, He followed it by His saying: أَوْ هُوَ أَقْرَبُ (or even swifter). From such a context, a digression (إضراب) is understood. It is as if He, the Exalted, is saying: "Its matter, in terms of the lightness of the burden, insignificance, and ease in relation to Us, resembles the twinkling of one of you with his eye. I have only likened it to that out of regard for your state and to bring it closer to your understanding; otherwise, the matter is swifter than that." As He said regarding it: وَيَوْمَ يَقُولُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ — "and the day He says, ‘Be!’ and it is" (6:73). Thus, the matter of the Hour, in relation to His power and His will, the Exalted, is like the matter of the easiest and most insignificant of creation.

The Exalted has justified this by His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ "Indeed Allah has power over all things" (16:77). His power (القدرة) — the divine attribute of being able to bring any possible thing into existence — over all things necessitates that all things be equal in relation to Him.

Beware of the illusion that the universality of power (عموم القدرة) does not necessitate the removal of differences between things in terms of their relation [to that power]. One might think that the scarcity or abundance of intermediary causes (الأسباب المتوسطة), conditions (الشرائط), and impediments (الموانع) between the agent and his action inevitably has an effect in this regard. For example, a human being is capable of breathing and also of carrying a weight he can bear, yet these two are not equal in relation to him; and so on by analogy.

Such a notion arises from a neglect of the true meaning of the universality of power. To clarify: the power within us is a restricted power (قدرة مقيدة). For instance, a human's power to eat food—which is his state of having a causal relation (نسبة الفاعلية) to it—is conditioned in its effect by the actual existence of food externally, its being before him, its being accessible, the absence of any preventer (whether human or otherwise), and the physical tools of the action (like the hand and mouth) being free from any ailment, among other things. What the human actually possesses is the will (الإرادة); anything beyond that consists of intermediaries, conditions, and impediments that are, in reality, outside his power and serve as constraints that restrict it. When a human intends to exercise his power to eat, he must prepare those matters by which his power is restricted in its effect, such as obtaining the food, placing it near him, removing impediments, and employing bodily organs.

It is evident that the scarcity or abundance, proximity or distance, and similar qualities of these factors cause the action to vary in ease or difficulty, and the power to appear weak or strong. Thus, it is the restriction of power (تقيد القدرة) that causes this variation.

As for His power, the Exalted, it is the very essence (عين ذاته) of His Being, whose existence is necessary (واجب الوجود) and whose non-existence is impossible. If this were the case, then were His power to be restricted by any condition—such as the existence of a cause or condition, or the absence of an impediment—it would cease to exist upon the cessation of its restriction, which is impossible. Therefore, His power, the Exalted, is absolute (مطلقة), neither limited by any boundary nor restricted by any condition. It is universal (عامة), relating to everything equally, without one thing being more difficult or easier for Him than another, or one thing being closer or further from Him than another. Rather, the difference exists only between the things themselves when compared to one another.

To put it another way: there is nothing that is not in need of Him, the Glorified, for its existence (الوجود). If we consider every existing thing—such that nothing is excluded—and relate them to Him, the Exalted, they would all be objects of His power. There is no third factor that could serve as a restriction for His power, whether a cause, a condition, or the absence of an impediment; otherwise, that factor would be a partner in the effect (شريكا في التأثير)—exalted is He far above that.

As for the intermediary causes, conditions, and impediments that exist between the things themselves, these necessitate the restriction of some things by others, not the restriction of the Universal Divine Power (القدرة العامة الإلهية) that relates to them. Furthermore, the power relates to the thing in its restricted state, not in the absolute. This means that the object of power is "Zayd, whose father is so-and-so, whose mother is so-and-so, who exists at such-and-such a time and place," and so on. Thus, the existence of Zayd, with all his connections, is the existence of the entire world.

The power relating to him relates to the whole in the same way. There is but one single power relating to the whole, through which everything is brought into existence in its specific station (موطنه الخاص). This power is absolute and unrestricted; there is no variation among things in relation to it, for variation exists only between the things themselves.

It has become clear from the preceding that the universality of power necessitates the removal of variation between things in relation to it—in terms of ease, difficulty, and the like. This noble verse is among the most brilliant of Quranic verses, through which several points are clarified:

First: That the reality of the Resurrection (المعاد) — the return of all beings to Allah for judgment — is the manifestation of the reality of things after their concealment.

Second: That the Divine Power relates to all things in an equal manner, without any variation in ease, difficulty, proximity, distance, or otherwise.

Third: That things, according to their reality, are interconnected in their existence such that the creation of one is the creation of all, and the whole is the object of a single power in which there is no other influencer.

Yes, there is another perspective, simpler than this, which views things through the system of causes and effects (نظام الأسباب والمسببات). Allah has confirmed this in His speech, as previously explained in the discussion on miracles (الإعجاز) in the first volume of this book. From this perspective, things are distinct from one another, and the existence of some depends on the existence or absence of others; thus, they precede or follow, become easy or difficult, and causes serve as intermediaries between them and Him, the Exalted, while He is the Agent (الفاعل) through the mediation of causes. This is a simple perspective.

Many exegetes have mentioned regarding His saying: وَلِلَّهِ غَيْبُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ "To Allah belongs the Unseen of the heavens and the earth" (16:77), that there is an omitted word in the possessive construction, the implied meaning being: "To Allah belongs the knowledge of the Unseen..." However, this would necessitate a break in the connection between this sentence and what follows it, as there would be no link between the knowledge of the Unseen and the ease of the matter of the Hour. Consequently, the sentence would become a redundant digression that the discourse could do without.

The claim of some—in an attempt to resolve this redundancy—that the beginning and end of the verse establish knowledge and power, and that together they complete the creation of the Hour, is of no use. This is because people's doubt regarding the Hour stems from the perspective of power, as they consider it impossible; thus, there is no need to resort to the issue of knowledge to prove it. This is evidenced by other verses that establish the possibility of the Resurrection through the universality of power.

Others have mentioned that what is meant is the knowledge of their Unseen, not by implying the word "knowledge" in the speech—such that it could be argued that the default is its absence—but because the annexation of "the Unseen" (الغيب) — that which is hidden from the senses and the intellect — to "the heavens and the earth" implies that what is meant are the unknown matters within them, whether occurring now or later. Thus, His ownership of them, the Exalted, is His ownership of the knowledge of them.

The flaw in this is that the final premise is rejected, as previously explained. Furthermore, the problem of the lack of connection between the two sentences remains.

Additionally, some have mentioned in justifying the reasoning derived from His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ "Indeed Allah has power over all things," that among these "things" is the establishment of the Hour in the quickest possible time, and He is capable of that.

The flaw here is that this does not suffice to justify what is understood from the restriction (الحصر) — the rhetorical device of limiting a quality to a subject — through negation and affirmation. It would only suffice to justify it if the verse had said that Allah will make the matter of the Hour like the twinkling of an eye, despite the possibility of it being otherwise. Reflect on this.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding the saying of the Exalted: فِي بُطُونِهِ مِنْ بَيْنِ فَرْثٍ وَدَمٍ "...from within its bellies, from between dross and blood" (16:66), he said: He (peace be upon him) said: "The dross (الفرث) is what is in the rumen."

In al-Kafi, from Ali ibn Ibrahim, from his father, from al-Nawfali, from al-Sakkuni, who said: Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "No one ever chokes while drinking milk, because Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: لَبَناً خالِصاً سائِغاً لِلشَّارِبِينَ '...pure milk, pleasant to those who drink it' (16:66)."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain of narration from a man, from Hariz, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: وَأَوْحى رَبُّكَ إِلَى النَّحْلِ "And your Lord revealed to the bee..." (16:68). He said: "We are the bees (النحل) to whom Allah revealed—'to take houses in the mountains'—He commanded us to take followers (شيعة) from among the Arabs; وَمِنَ الشَّجَرِ 'and from the trees'—He says: from the non-Arabs (العجم); وَمِمَّا يَعْرِشُونَ 'and from what they erect'—from the clients (الموالي). And 'that which comes forth from their bellies is a drink of varying colors' is the knowledge (العلم) that comes forth from us to you."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning, and they fall under the category of jary (الجري) — the application of Quranic verses to new instances or individuals over time. This is evidenced by the application in some of these narrations of "the bee" to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), "the mountains" to the Quraysh, "the trees" to the Arabs, "what they erect" to the clients, and "what comes forth from their bellies" to knowledge.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain of narration from Ali ibn al-Mughirah, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "When a servant reaches a hundred years, that is the most abject stage of life (أرذل العمر)."

In al-Majma', it is narrated from Ali (peace be upon him) that the most abject stage of life is seventy-five years; a similar report is narrated from the Prophet.

I say: This was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Tabari from Ali (peace be upon him). It also narrated from Ibn Mardawayh from Anas from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) a detailed tradition indicating that the most abject stage of life is a hundred years.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abd al-Rahman al-Ashall, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ بَنِينَ وَحَفَدَةً "...and made for you sons and grandsons from your wives" (16:72). He said: "The grandsons (الحفدة) are the sons of the daughter, and we are the grandsons of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)."

In the same source, from Jamil ibn Darraj, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding "the grandsons" (الحفدة), he said: "They are the helpers (العون) from among them," meaning the sons.

In al-Majma', regarding the meaning of "the grandsons" (الحفدة): "They are a man's sons-in-law through his daughters." He said: "This is what is narrated from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him)."

I say: There is no contradiction between these narrations, as previously explained in the commentary.

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain of narration from Shu'ayb al-Aqrqufi, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the divorce and marriage of a slave, he said: "He has neither divorce nor marriage—have you not heard Allah the Exalted saying: عَبْداً مَمْلُوكاً لا يَقْدِرُ عَلى شَيْءٍ '...a slave owned [by another] who has no power over anything' (16:75)?" He said: "He has no power over divorce or marriage except by the permission of his master."

I say: There are several narrations with this meaning from the Shi'i sources.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding the saying of the Exalted: هَلْ يَسْتَوِي هُوَ وَمَنْ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ "Is he equal to one who enjoins justice...?" (16:76). He said: He (peace be upon him) said: "How can this one be equal to the one who enjoins justice—the Commander of the Faithful and the Imams (peace be upon them)?" In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibn Shahr Ashub, from Hamzah ibn Ata, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: هَلْ يَسْتَوِي هُوَ وَمَنْ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ the verse; he said: "He is Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him), and he is on a straight path."

I say: These two narrations are of the jary type and are not among the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول) at all, for the reasons mentioned in the previous commentary.

The same applies to what is narrated through Sunni channels: that His saying: ضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً عَبْداً مَمْلُوكاً "Allah draws a parable: a slave owned [by another]..." (16:75) was revealed concerning Hisham ibn Amr—who used to spend his wealth secretly and openly—and his slave Abu al-Jawza, who used to forbid him. Similarly, it is narrated that the verse was revealed concerning Uthman ibn Affan and a slave of his.

Likewise, regarding His saying: وَضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً رَجُلَيْنِ "And Allah draws a parable of two men..." (16:76), it is narrated that the dumb one is Ubayy ibn Khalaf, and the one who enjoins justice is Hamzah and Uthman ibn Maz'un. It is also narrated that the dumb one is Hashim ibn Amr ibn al-Harith al-Qurashi—who was of little good and hostile to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). It is also narrated that the dumb one is Abu Jahl and the one enjoining justice is Ammar. It is also narrated that the one enjoining justice is Uthman ibn Affan and the dumb one is a disbelieving client of his named Usayd ibn Abi al-Ays; and so on.

Ayahs

وَٱللَّهُ أَخْرَجَكُم مِّنۢ بُطُونِ أُمَّهَـٰتِكُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَجَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ وَٱلْأَفْـِٔدَةَ ۙ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (٧٨)
Allah has brought you forth from the bellies of your mothers while you did not know anything. He made for you hearing, eyesight, and hearts so that you may give thanks. (78)
أَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ إِلَى ٱلطَّيْرِ مُسَخَّرَٰتٍ فِى جَوِّ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَا يُمْسِكُهُنَّ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٧٩)
Have they not regarded the birds disposed in the air of the sky: no one sustains them except Allah. There are indeed signs in that for a people who have faith. (79)
وَٱللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنۢ بُيُوتِكُمْ سَكَنًا وَجَعَلَ لَكُم مِّن جُلُودِ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ بُيُوتًا تَسْتَخِفُّونَهَا يَوْمَ ظَعْنِكُمْ وَيَوْمَ إِقَامَتِكُمْ ۙ وَمِنْ أَصْوَافِهَا وَأَوْبَارِهَا وَأَشْعَارِهَآ أَثَـٰثًا وَمَتَـٰعًا إِلَىٰ حِينٍ (٨٠)
It is Allah who has made your homes as a place of rest for you and He made for you homes out of the skins of the cattle which you find portable on the day of your shifting and on the day of your halt, and out of their wool, fur and hair [He has appointed] furniture and wares [enduring] for a while. (80)
وَٱللَّهُ جَعَلَ لَكُم مِّمَّا خَلَقَ ظِلَـٰلًا وَجَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْجِبَالِ أَكْنَـٰنًا وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ سَرَٰبِيلَ تَقِيكُمُ ٱلْحَرَّ وَسَرَٰبِيلَ تَقِيكُم بَأْسَكُمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُتِمُّ نِعْمَتَهُۥ عَلَيْكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُسْلِمُونَ (٨١)
It is Allah who made for you the shade from what He has created, and made for you retreats in the mountains, and made for you garments that protect you from heat, and garments that protect you from your [mutual] violence. That is how He completes His blessing upon you so that you may submit [to Him]. (81)
فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنَّمَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (٨٢)
But if they turn their backs [on you], your duty is only to communicate in clear terms. (82)
يَعْرِفُونَ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ يُنكِرُونَهَا وَأَكْثَرُهُمُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (٨٣)
They recognize the blessing of Allah and then deny it, and most of them are faithless. (83)
وَيَوْمَ نَبْعَثُ مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيدًا ثُمَّ لَا يُؤْذَنُ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَلَا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ (٨٤)
The day We shall raise a witness from every nation, the faithless will not be permitted [to speak], nor will they be asked to propitiate [Allah]. (84)
وَإِذَا رَءَا ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ فَلَا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمْ وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ (٨٥)
And when the wrongdoers sight the punishment, it shall not be lightened for them, nor will they be granted any respite. (85)
وَإِذَا رَءَا ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ شُرَكَآءَهُمْ قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ شُرَكَآؤُنَا ٱلَّذِينَ كُنَّا نَدْعُوا۟ مِن دُونِكَ ۖ فَأَلْقَوْا۟ إِلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقَوْلَ إِنَّكُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ (٨٦)
When the polytheists sight their partners, they will say, ‘Our Lord! These are our partners whom we used to invoke besides You.’ But they will retort to them, ‘You are indeed liars!’ (86)
وَأَلْقَوْا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلسَّلَمَ ۖ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (٨٧)
They will submit to Allah on that day, and what they used to fabricate will forsake them. (87)
ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ زِدْنَـٰهُمْ عَذَابًا فَوْقَ ٱلْعَذَابِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يُفْسِدُونَ (٨٨)
Those who are faithless and bar from the way of Allah—We shall add punishment to their punishment because of the corruption they used to cause. (88)
وَيَوْمَ نَبْعَثُ فِى كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيدًا عَلَيْهِم مِّنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ ۖ وَجِئْنَا بِكَ شَهِيدًا عَلَىٰ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ۚ وَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ تِبْيَـٰنًا لِّكُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُسْلِمِينَ (٨٩)
The day We raise in every nation a witness against them from among themselves, We shall bring you as a witness against these. We have sent down the Book to you as a clarification of all things and as guidance, mercy and good news for the Muslims. (89)

(Commentary)

The verses mention several other divine blessings, then turn the discourse toward what they reveal regarding the truth of His Oneness in Lordship (الربوبية), the Resurrection (البعث), and Prophethood (النبوة) and Legislation (التشريع), similar to the preceding group of verses we have presented.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ أَخْرَجَكُمْ مِنْ بُطُونِ أُمَّهاتِكُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ شَيْئاً — "Allah has brought you forth from the bellies of your mothers while you did not know anything" (16:78) to the end of the verse. Al-Ummahat (الأمهات) is the plural of Umm (أم) [mother]; the ha (الهاء) is augmentative, similar to ahraqa (أهراق) [he poured] whose root is araqa (أراق). Sometimes it appears as ummat (أمات). It is said that ummahat is used for humans and ummat for other animals. Al-Af’idah (الأفئدة) is a plural of paucity for fu’ad (الفؤاد), which is the heart (القلب) and the intellect (اللب); no plural of multitude has been formed for it.

His saying: وَاللهُ أَخْرَجَكُمْ مِنْ بُطُونِ أُمَّهاتِكُمْ — "Allah has brought you forth from the bellies of your mothers" is an allusion to birth (التولد). The phrase لا تَعْلَمُونَ شَيْئاً — "while you did not know anything" is a circumstantial clause (حال) referring to the pronoun of the second person plural; meaning: He brought you out of their wombs through birth while your souls were devoid of these pieces of information that you later acquired through the channels of sense (الحس), imagination (الخيال), and intellect (العقل).

The verse supports the view held by psychologists that the tablet of the soul (لوح النفس) is devoid of information at the beginning of its formation, and then it is inscribed upon little by little—as has been said. This excludes the soul's knowledge of itself (علم النفس بذاتها), for that is not conventionally referred to as "knowing something" (يعلم شيئا). The evidence for this is His saying, the Exalted, in the preceding verses regarding the one who is returned to the most abject age: لِكَيْ لا يَعْلَمَ بَعْدَ عِلْمٍ شَيْئاً — "so that he does not know anything after [having possessed] knowledge" (16:70). It is necessary that in such a state, he is still aware of himself.

Some have argued using the generality of the verse that presential knowledge (العلم الحضوري) — the direct, unmediated knowledge of a thing's essence without a mental form — meaning the human being's knowledge of himself, is, like other forms of acquired knowledge (العلوم الحصولية) — knowledge gained through mental concepts and sensory data — absent at the start and occurs only afterward. He then critiqued the proofs for the soul's knowledge of itself being presential with strange arguments.

The response is that the generality of the verse is directed toward acquired knowledge (العلم الحصولي), and the aforementioned verse bears witness to this.

His saying: وَجَعَلَ لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ وَالْأَبْصارَ وَالْأَفْئِدَةَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ — "and He gave you hearing, sight, and hearts, so that you may give thanks" (16:78) is an allusion to the principles of knowledge (مبادئ العلم) with which He blessed man. The principle of conception (التصور) is sensation (الحس), the primary modes of which are hearing and sight, even if others like touch, taste, and smell exist. The principle of thought (الفكر) is the heart (الفؤاد).

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ يَرَوْا إِلَى الطَّيْرِ مُسَخَّراتٍ فِي جَوِّ السَّماءِ ما يُمْسِكُهُنَّ إِلَّا اللهُ — "Have they not regarded the birds, disposed [to fly] in the air of the sky: no one sustains them except Allah?" (16:79) etc. It is stated in al-Majma‘: The air (الجو) is the atmosphere distant from the earth. End quote. He says: Have they not looked at the birds while they are subjected (مسخرات) to Allah, the Glorified, in the air of the sky and the atmosphere distant from the earth? Then He began a new sentence, pointing to the result of this observation: ما يُمْسِكُهُنَّ إِلَّا اللهُ — "no one sustains them except Allah."

Attributing the sustaining (الإمساك) to Allah, the Glorified, and negating it from others—despite the existence of natural causes (أسباب طبيعية) acting therein, and His speech, the Exalted, confirms the law of cause and effect (ناموس العلية والمعلولية) — is only because the bird's suspension in the air without falling, however it may be and to whatever cause it is attributed, is such that the bird, its cause, and the link between them are all dependent upon His handiwork, the Exalted. He is the One who bestows existence (المفيض للوجود) upon the bird, its cause, and the link between them. Thus, He is the Cause who truly bestows its existence, even if its proximate natural cause (سببه الطبيعي القريب) accompanies it and the bird depends upon it.

The meaning of its dependence in its existence upon its cause is not that its cause grants it existence after having received its own existence from Him, the Exalted. Rather, it means that this effect (المسبب) depends in its receiving existence from Him, the Exalted, upon its cause receiving existence from Him, the Exalted, prior to that. Some explanation of this has preceded recently.

This is the meaning of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — in the Quran. The evidence for this from the wording of the text includes such verses as: أَلا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ — "Look! All creation and command belong to Him" (7:54), and His saying: أَنَّ الْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً — "that power belongs entirely to Allah" (2:165), and His saying: اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — "Allah is the creator of all things" (39:62), and His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ — "Indeed Allah has power over all things" (16:77).

The evidence for what we have presented regarding the meaning of negation and affirmation in the verse is His saying, the Exalted: مُسَخَّراتٍ — "disposed [to fly]". For subjection (التسخير) — the act of compelling a thing to serve a specific purpose by divine decree — is only realized by one cause overcoming another in its action according to what the overcoming cause intends. Thus, in the word itself, there is an indication that the subjected entity possesses a type of causality (السببية).

In reality, the flight of a bird in the air of the sky is no more wondrous than the human being remaining stationary on the earth. All of it traces back to His handiwork, the Exalted, in equal measure. However, man's familiarity with certain matters and his frequent encounter with them causes the faculty of inquiry (قريحة البحث) to become dormant. When he encounters something that contradicts what he is familiar with—like an exception to a universal rule—he becomes alert to it, and the faculty is drawn to investigate it. Man sees heavy earthly bodies resting upon the earth, attracted to it. When he finds the bird, for instance, breaking the universality of this rule by its flight, he marvels at it and expands his inquiry to find its cause. Truth (الحق) has a share in this inquiry, and this is one of the reasons why these types of matters are taken in the Quran as materials for argumentation.

His saying: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ — "There are indeed signs in that for a people who have faith" (16:79); meaning, in their being subjected in the air of the sky. For the bird, while in the air, flaps its wings, glides, spreads them, folds them, remains still, moves, ascends, and descends—all of which are signs for a people who believe, as Allah has mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ بُيُوتِكُمْ سَكَناً — "It is Allah who has made your homes a place of rest for you" (16:80) to the end of the verse. In al-Mufradat: The house (al-bayt البيت) is the shelter of a human at night, because it is said: bata (بات) [he stayed the night], just as it is said: zhalla (ظل) [he stayed the day]. Then, a dwelling may be called a bayt without considering the night in it. Its plural is abyat (أبيات) and buyut (بيوت), but buyut is more specific to dwellings and abyat to poetry. He said: This applies to what is made of stone, clay, wool, and fur. End of the relevant portion.

Al-Sakan (السكن) is that which one finds rest in. Al-Zha‘n (الظعن) is departing or traveling, which is the opposite of staying. Al-Suf (الصوف) is for sheep, al-wabar (الوبر) is for camels—like hair (al-sha‘r الشعر) for humans—and what belongs to goats is also called sha‘r like humans. Al-Athath (الأثاث) refers to abundant household furnishings; a single item is not called athath. It is said in al-Majma‘: There is no singular for athath, just as there is no singular for mata‘ (متاع). End quote. Al-Mata‘ is more general than athath, as it refers to anything that is enjoyed (يتمتع به) and is not restricted to what is in the house.

His saying: وَاللهُ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ بُيُوتِكُمْ سَكَناً — "It is Allah who has made your homes a place of rest for you"; meaning, He made some of your houses a place of rest for you to dwell in. For some houses are not for resting, such as those made for storing wealth, stockpiling goods, and so on. His saying: وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ جُلُودِ الْأَنْعامِ بُيُوتاً — "and made for you homes from the hides of livestock" etc.; meaning, from their skins after tanning, which are leather mats and hides. بُيُوتاً — "homes" refers to domes and tents. تَسْتَخِفُّونَها — "which you find light"; meaning, you consider them light in terms of carrying. يَوْمَ ظَعْنِكُمْ — "on the day of your departure" and travel, وَيَوْمَ إِقامَتِكُمْ — "and on the day of your settlement" without travel or departure.

His saying: وَمِنْ أَصْوافِها وَأَوْبارِها وَأَشْعارِها — "and from their wool, their fur, and their hair" etc., is coordinated with the position of مِنْ جُلُودِ — "from the hides". Meaning: and He made for you مِنْ أَصْوافِها — "from their wool" which is for sheep, وَأَوْبارِها — "their fur" which is for camels, وَأَشْعارِها — "and their hair" which is for goats, أَثاثاً — "furnishings" that you use in your homes, وَمَتاعاً — "and goods" that you enjoy إِلى حِينٍ — "for a while", a limited time. It is said that in this is an allusion that these things are fleeting and perishing, so a rational person should not prefer them over the bliss of the Hereafter.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِمَّا خَلَقَ ظِلالاً — "It is Allah who has made for you shades from what He has created" (16:81) to the end of the verse. The two prepositional phrases, لَكُمْ — "for you" and مِمَّا خَلَقَ — "from what He has created", are related to the verb "made" (جعل). The attribution of shades (الظلال) to what He created is because shade is a privative matter (أمر عدمي) realized as a consequence of something else. Nevertheless, they are among the great blessings that Allah has bestowed upon man and other animals and plants. The benefit of shade for man and others is no less than the benefit of light. Were it not for shade—the shade of the night, the shade of buildings, trees, caves, and others—no living thing would survive on the face of the earth.

His saying: وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْجِبالِ أَكْناناً — "and He has made for you shelters in the mountains". Al-Kin (الكن) is that which a thing is concealed by, such that a shirt is a kin for its wearer. The aknan (أكنان) of the mountains are the caves and holes found within them.

His saying: وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ سَرابِيلَ تَقِيكُمُ الْحَرَّ — "and He has made for you garments to protect you from the heat"; meaning, shirts that preserve you from the heat. It is said in al-Majma‘: He did not say "and protect you from the cold" because what protects from heat protects from cold. Heat was specified, even though their protection from cold is greater, because those addressed were people of heat in their lands, so their need for what protects from heat was greater—narrated from ‘Ata’.

He said: Furthermore, the Arabs find the mention of one of two things sufficient to indicate the other due to it being known. The poet said:

And I know not, when I head toward a land Seeking good, which of the two shall meet me

He alluded to evil without mentioning it because it is implied. This was mentioned by al-Farra’. End quote.

Perhaps part of the reason for mentioning heat and sufficing with it is that early humans inhabited the hot regions of the earth, so the intensity of heat was more immediate to them than the intensity of cold. Their realization to adopt garments (السراويل) was for protection from that which they were more closely afflicted by, which was heat. Allah knows best.

His saying: وَسَرابِيلَ تَقِيكُمْ بَأْسَكُمْ — "and garments to protect you from your [mutual] violence". It is apparent that what is meant is iron armor and the like.

His saying: كَذلِكَ يُتِمُّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُسْلِمُونَ — "Thus does He complete His blessing upon you so that you may submit [to Him]". This is a reminder of His favor upon them by completing the blessings He mentioned. The hoped-for goal from this was their submission (إسلام) to Allah out of recognition of these blessings. For it is expected and anticipated from one who recognizes the blessings and their completion upon him that he should submit to the will of his Benefactor and not meet Him with arrogance, because a Benefactor of this status intends no evil for him.

His saying, the Exalted: فإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَإِنَّما عَلَيْكَ الْبَلاغُ الْمُبِينُ — "But if they turn away, your duty is only to communicate in clear terms" (16:82). It is said in al-Majma‘: Al-Balagh (البلاغ) is the noun and al-tabligh (التبليغ) is the verbal noun, like al-kalam (الكلام) and al-taklim (التكليم). End quote.

When He finished mentioning the blessings intended to be mentioned and arguing with them, He concluded them with that whose implication is reproach, blame, and a threat for disbelief (الكفر). It includes the mention of His Oneness in Lordship, the Return (المعاد), and Prophethood. He began this by explaining the duty of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in his mission, which is communication (البلاغ). He said: فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا — "But if they turn away"; meaning, it follows from this explanation—which contains nothing but an invitation to what is for their welfare in this life and the next, without being followed by compulsion or coercion—that if they turn away and refuse to listen to it or be guided by it, فَإِنَّما عَلَيْكَ الْبلاغُ الْمُبِينُ — "your duty is only to communicate in clear terms", the clear communication in which there is no ambiguity or concealment, because you are a messenger, and the messenger has no duty but that.

In the verse is a consolation for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and an explanation of his duty.

His saying, the Exalted: يَعْرِفُونَ نِعْمَتَ اللهِ ثُمَّ يُنْكِرُونَها وَأَكْثَرُهُمُ الْكافِرُونَ — "They recognize the blessing of Allah then they deny it, and most of them are faithless" (16:83). Recognition (المعرفة) and denial (الإنكار) are opposites, like knowledge and ignorance. This is the evidence that what is meant by denial—which is the absence of recognition—is its necessary implication, which is denial in the realm of action (الإنكار في مقام العمل), namely the lack of faith in Allah, His Messenger, and the Last Day, or verbal rejection despite recognition in the heart. However, His saying: وَأَكْثَرُهُمُ الْكافِرُونَ — "and most of them are faithless" restricts the rejection (الجحود) to most of them, as will come, so the first meaning remains for "denial."

His saying: وَأَكْثَرُهُمُ الْكافِرُونَ — "and most of them are faithless". The entry of the definite article al (ال) on الْكافِرُونَ indicates perfection; meaning they are disbelievers in the divine blessings or in what they indicate of monotheism and other things entirely, but most of them are "perfect" in their disbelief, and that is through stubborn rejection, persistence in it, and barring from the way of Allah.

The meaning is: they recognize the blessing of Allah as being a blessing from Him, the requirement of which is that they believe in Him, His Messenger, and the Last Day, and submit in action. Then, when they reach the point of action, they act according to the effects of denial rather than recognition. Most of them do not suffice with mere practical denial but add to it the perfection of disbelief, stubbornness against the truth, rejection, and persistence in it.

What we have presented is sufficient for you regarding the lengthy discussions of the commentators on the meaning of His saying: وَأَكْثَرُهُمُ الْكافِرُونَ — "and most of them are faithless", despite the fact that they are all disbelievers by their denial. Some said: He only said أَكْثَرُهُمُ — "most of them" because among them were those against whom the proof (الحجة) had not been established, such as those who had not reached the age of responsibility, or were mentally impaired, or the call had not reached them, so the name of disbelief does not apply to them.

The response is that these people are outside the scope of the verse's absolute sense entirely, for it mentions as a reproach and threat that they deny the blessing of Allah after they have recognized it. If these people were denying it, they would be disbelievers thereby; and if they did not deny it, they would certainly not enter into the absolute sense of the verse. And how can it be correct to say that the proof was not established against them, when the proof is nothing but the blessing that Allah counts and they recognize?

The saying of some: He only said وَأَكْثَرُهُمُ الْكافِرُونَ because He knew that among them were those who would eventually believe. The response is that this is a claim for which there is no evidence.

The saying of some: What is meant by "most" is "all," and He only turned away from "some" out of contempt for mentioning them—this is attributed to al-Hasan al-Basri, and it is a strange saying.

It is said: In the verse is evidence for the falsity of the view of the Necessitarians (المجبرة) that Allah has no blessing upon the disbeliever, and that all He has done with them is only abandonment and vengeance, because He, the Glorified, explicitly stated in this verse the opposite of their view. End quote.

The truth is that blessing (النعمة) has two considerations: one is its being a blessing, i.e., pleasant and suitable to the state of the one blessed from the perspective of his being in the path of creation (صراط التكوين), i.e., in terms of his physical happiness. The other is from the perspective of the one blessed being in the path of legislation (صراط التشريع), i.e., in terms of his spiritual human happiness, such that the blessing is such that its recognition necessitates his faith in Allah, His Messenger, and the Last Day, and its use in the way of Allah's pleasure. The believer is blessed with both types of blessings, while the disbeliever is blessed in this world with the first group but deprived of the second. In His speech, the Glorified, there are many witnesses that testify to this.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَ نَبْعَثُ مِنْ كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيداً ثُمَّ لا يُؤْذَنُ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَلا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ — "The day We shall raise a witness from every nation, then the faithless will not be permitted [to speak], nor will they be asked to conciliate" (16:84). It is said in al-Majma‘: Az-Zajjaj said: Al-‘atb (العتب) is displeasure. It is said: ‘ataba ‘alayhi (عتب عليه) when he found displeasure with him. If he discusses with him what he was displeased about, they say: ‘atabahu (عاتبه). If he returns to his pleasure, it is said: a‘taba (أعتب). The noun is al-‘utba (العتبي), which is the return of the one blamed to what pleases the blamer. Ista‘tabahu (استعتبه) means he asked him to return to pleasure. End quote.

His saying: وَيَوْمَ نَبْعَثُ مِنْ كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيداً — "The day We shall raise a witness from every nation". The context implies that what is meant by this day is the Day of Resurrection. By these witnesses, one of whom is raised from every nation, are meant the witnesses of deeds (شهداء الأعمال) who bore the realities of the deeds of their nations in the world; they are called to testify and they testify against them on the Day of Resurrection. Some discussion on the meaning of this witnessing has preceded in the commentary on His saying: لِتَكُونُوا شُهَداءَ عَلَى النَّاسِ وَيَكُونَ الرَّسُولُ عَلَيْكُمْ شَهِيداً — "that you may be witnesses to mankind, and that the Apostle may be a witness to you" (2:143) in the first volume of the book.

There is no indication in the wording of the verse that what is meant by the witness of the nation is its Prophet, nor that what is meant by the nation is the nation of the Messenger. It is possible that someone other than the Prophet from his nation, such as the Imam, is the witness, as indicated by the previous verse of al-Baqarah and His saying, the Exalted: وَجِيءَ بِالنَّبِيِّينَ وَالشُّهَداءِ — "and the prophets and the witnesses shall be brought" (39:69). Accordingly, what is meant by "every nation" is the nation of the raised witness and the people of his time.

His saying: ثُمَّ لا يُؤْذَنُ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَلا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ — "then the faithless will not be permitted [to speak], nor will they be asked to conciliate". The mention of raising the witnesses of the nations is evidence that they testify against their nations regarding what they did in the world, and a clue that what is meant by the negation of permission for the disbelievers is that they are not permitted to speak—which is inevitably for making excuses. The negation of permission to speak is only a preparation for the witnesses to give their testimony, as alluded to by other verses such as His saying: الْيَوْمَ نَخْتِمُ عَلى أَفْواهِهِمْ وَتُكَلِّمُنا أَيْدِيهِمْ وَتَشْهَدُ أَرْجُلُهُمْ — "Today We shall seal their mouths, and their hands shall speak to Us, and their feet shall bear witness" (36:65), and His saying: هذا يَوْمُ لا يَنْطِقُونَ وَلا يُؤْذَنُ لَهُمْ فَيَعْتَذِرُونَ — "This is a day they will not speak, nor will they be permitted to offer excuses" (77:35-36).

Furthermore, the context of His saying: ثُمَّ لا يُؤْذَنُ — "then [they] will not be permitted" etc., implies that what is meant by this is the negation of any ruses by which evil might be avoided on that day, and an explanation that there is no way to rectify what they missed or fix what was corrupted of their deeds in the world on that day. This would be one of two things: making excuses or resuming action. As for the second, it is covered by His saying: وَلا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ — "nor will they be asked to conciliate", leaving for the first—which is making excuses through speech—nothing but His saying: ثُمَّ لا يُؤْذَنُ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا — "then the faithless will not be permitted [to speak]".

From here it becomes clear that His saying: وَلا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ — meaning they are not asked to please Allah and satisfy Him—is an explanation of the impossibility of rectifying what they missed by renewing action and returning to hearing and obedience. For the day is a day of recompense, not a day of action, and there is no way for them to return backward to the world to do righteous deeds and be rewarded for them.

The Glorified has explained this in other places of His speech in a different manner, such as His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ يُكْشَفُ عَنْ ساقٍ وَيُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ ، خاشِعَةً أَبْصارُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ وَقَدْ كانُوا يُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ وَهُمْ سالِمُونَ — "The day when the shank is bared, and they are called to prostrate themselves, but they cannot [do it]. Their looks will be cast down, and abasement will overcome them, for they used to be called to prostrate themselves while they were yet sound" (68:42-43), and His saying: وَلَوْ تَرى إِذِ الْمُجْرِمُونَ ناكِسُوا رُؤُسِهِمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ رَبَّنا أَبْصَرْنا وَسَمِعْنا فَارْجِعْنا نَعْمَلْ صالِحاً إِنَّا مُوقِنُونَ — "Were you to see when the guilty hang their heads before their Lord [saying], ‘Our Lord! We have seen and heard; so send us back [to the world] that we may act righteously. Indeed we [now] have certainty’" (32:12).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا رَأَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا الْعَذابَ فَلا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمْ وَلا هُمْ يُنْظَرُونَ — "When the wrongdoers behold the punishment, it will not be lightened for them, nor will they be granted any respite" (16:85). The previous verse was in reality aimed at explaining the difference between the Day of Recompense—which is the Day of Resurrection—and other circumstances of recompense in the world, in that the recompense of the Day of Resurrection is not removed or changed by excuse or by conciliation. This verse is an explanation of the difference between the punishment of that day and the worldly punishments that afflict the wrongdoers in the world, for the latter accept some form of lightening or respite by delay, whereas the punishment of the Day of Resurrection accepts neither lightening nor respite.

His saying: وَإِذا رَأَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا الْعَذابَ — "When the wrongdoers behold the punishment". Wrongdoing (الظلم) was mentioned in the relative clause rather than disbelief and the like to indicate the reason and criterion for the judgment. What is meant by "beholding the punishment" is its looming over them and their looming over it after the final judgment, as the context implies. What is meant by the punishment is the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, which is the punishment of the Fire.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: when the matter is decided for their punishment and they loom over the punishment by witnessing the Fire, there is no escape for them from it through lightening or through respite and delay.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا رَأَى الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا شُرَكاءَهُمْ — "When the polytheists behold their partners" (16:86) to the end of the verse. It continues the discourse on the Day of Resurrection. His saying: وَإِذا رَأَى الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا — "When the polytheists behold", who in the convention of the Quran are the worshippers of idols and images, is a clue that what is meant by His saying: شُرَكاءَهُمْ — "their partners" are those whom they associated with Allah based on their claim that they were partners to Allah and as a fabrication. This is also indicated by the end of the verse and the following verse.

Their being named "their partners" while they call them "partners of Allah" is to indicate thereby that they have no partnership except the partnership by their own making according to their illusion. Thus, their associating "their partners" has no reality except that it has no reality.

By this, it becomes clear that the interpretation of "their partners" as idols or false deities, and that they were only counted as "their partners" because they assigned to them a portion of their wealth and livestock, or as devils because they shared with them in wealth and children, or as their partners in disbelief—those who disbelieved like their disbelief—or those who shared with them in the burden of their disbelief; all of that is misplaced, and we shall not dwell on the critique of each one.

His saying: قالُوا رَبَّنا هؤُلاءِ شُرَكاؤُنَا الَّذِينَ كُنَّا نَدْعُوا مِنْ دُونِكَ — "they will say, ‘Our Lord! These are our partners whom we used to invoke besides You’". Its meaning is clear, which is their identification of them to their Lord. There is no need to search for the purpose of the polytheists in identifying them, for the day is a day when wretchedness and punishment have surrounded them from every side, and man in such a state turns to everything that occurs to his mind of ways to strive for the salvation of himself and the relief of his distress.

His saying: فَأَلْقَوْا إِلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلَ إِنَّكُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ — "But they will retort at them, ‘You are indeed liars!’". It is said in al-Majma‘: You say: alqaytu al-shay’ (ألقيت الشيء) when you cast it. Al-laqa (اللقى) is the thing cast. Alqaytu ilayhi maqalah (ألقيت إليه مقالة) when you said it to him, and talaqqaha (تلقاها) when he accepted it. End quote.

The meaning is: their partners retorted to them and called them liars.
