Seeking protection (الاستعاذة) is seeking a refuge (المعاذ). The meaning is: When you recite the Qur'an, seek from Him, the Exalted, as long as you are reciting, that He protect you from the outcast Satan lest he seduce you. Thus, the commanded seeking of protection is the state of the reciter's soul as long as he recites, and he has been commanded to bring it about for himself as long as he recites. As for the reciter's saying: "I seek refuge in Allah from the outcast Satan" (أعوذ بالله من الشيطان الرجيم) or similar wording, it is a cause for bringing about the meaning of seeking protection in the soul, and is not the act itself except by a type of metaphor. He, the Glorified, said: "seek the protection of Allah" (استعذ بالله), and He did not say: "say: I seek refuge in Allah."

Through this, it appears that the view of some—that what is intended by "recitation" is the "intention" for it, making it a loose metaphor (مجاز مرسل) of the type of mentioning the effect and intending the cause—is not without some looseness.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُ لَيْسَ لَهُ سُلْطانٌ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَلى رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ — "Indeed he has no authority over those who have faith and put their trust in their Lord" (16:99). This is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for the command mentioned in the previous verse; i.e., seek protection in Allah during recitation so that He may protect you from him, because he has no authority over one who believes in Allah and puts his trust in Him.

From the verse, it appears, firstly: that seeking protection in Allah is putting trust (التوكل) in Him, for He, the Glorified, replaced "seeking protection" in the reasoning with "putting trust" and negated his authority over those who put their trust.

Secondly: that faith (الإيمان) and trust (التوكل) are the criteria for the truth of servitude (العبودية), as in His saying, the Exalted, to Iblis: إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ — "As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you" (13:42). He negated his authority over His servants, and He has replaced "servants" in this verse with "those who have faith and put their trust in their Lord." Reason supports this, for trust—which is handing over the reins of disposal in one's affairs to another and submitting to what he chooses for one—is the most specific effect of servitude.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما سُلْطانُهُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَوَلَّوْنَهُ وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ بِهِ مُشْرِكُونَ — "His authority is only over those who take him for a patron and those who ascribe partners to Him" (16:100). The three singular pronouns refer to Satan. That is, the authority of Satan is restricted to those who take him as a guardian (وليا) for them who manages their affairs as he wishes, and they obey him; and to those who associate partners through him, since they take him as a guardian besides Allah and a lord obeyed other than Him. For obedience is worship (عبادة), as indicated by His saying: أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي — "Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, 'Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. Worship Me...'" (36:60-61).

Through this, it appears, firstly: that the end of the verse explains its beginning, and that taking as a guardian one whom Allah has not permitted to be taken as such is shirk (شرك) — associating partners with Allah — and worship of other than Him.

Secondly: that there is no intermediary between putting trust in Allah and taking Satan as a guardian and worshipping him. Whoever does not put his trust in Allah is among the guardians of Satan.

It has been said that the singular pronoun in His saying: وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ بِهِ مُشْرِكُونَ — "and those who ascribe partners to Him" refers to Him, the Exalted. The verse would then mean that his authority is over two groups: the polytheists and those among the monotheists who take him as a guardian. However, the necessity of the difference in pronouns refutes this.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا بَدَّلْنا آيَةً مَكانَ آيَةٍ وَاللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما يُنَزِّلُ قالُوا إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُفْتَرٍ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ — "When We change a verse in place of another verse—and Allah knows best what He sends down—they say, 'You are just a fabricator!' Rather, most of them do not know" (16:101). This is an allusion to abrogation (النسخ) — the divine replacement of a previous ruling with a new one — and its wisdom, and an answer to what they accused him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of regarding fabrication against Allah. It is apparent from the context of the verses that the speakers are the polytheists, even if the Jews were the most rigid in negating abrogation. It is possible that the word was something the polytheists picked up from the Jews, as they frequently consulted them regarding the matter of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

His saying: وَإِذا بَدَّلْنا آيَةً مَكانَ آيَةٍ — "When We change a verse in place of another verse": Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "Substitution (الإبدال), changing (التبديل), being changed (التبدل), and seeking replacement (الاستبدال) mean placing one thing in the position of another. It is more general than 'compensation' (العوض), for compensation is that the second becomes yours by giving the first. 'Changing' (التبديل) may be said for alteration (التغيير) absolutely, even if a substitute is not brought." He cited: فَبَدَّلَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا قَوْلاً غَيْرَ الَّذِي قِيلَ لَهُمْ — "But the wrongdoers changed the saying with other than what they had been told" (2:59), and: فَمَنْ بَدَّلَهُ بَعْدَ ما سَمِعَهُ — "And should anyone change it after hearing it" (2:181), and: وَإِذا بَدَّلْنا آيَةً مَكانَ آيَةٍ — "When We change a verse in place of another verse" (16:101), and: بَدَّلْناهُمْ بِجَنَّتَيْهِمْ جَنَّتَيْنِ — "and We gave them in place of their two gardens two [other] gardens" (34:16), and: ثُمَّ بَدَّلْنا مَكانَ السَّيِّئَةِ الْحَسَنَةَ — "Then We changed the evil [state] into a good one" (7:95).

Changing (التبديل) in the sense of alteration (التغيير) differs from changing in its well-known sense in that its first object is the one taken and sought, unlike its well-known sense. Thus, the meaning of His saying: وَإِذا بَدَّلْنا آيَةً مَكانَ آيَةٍ is: We placed the second verse in the position of the first through alteration, so the second, substituted verse is the one remaining and sought.

His saying: وَاللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما يُنَزِّلُ — "and Allah knows best what He sends down" is a metaphor for the fact that the truth has not exceeded its proper place, and that the One who sent it down is the One most worthy of sending it down, for Allah knows more about it than they do. The sentence is a circumstantial clause (حالية).

His saying: قالُوا إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُفْتَرٍ — "they say, 'You are just a fabricator!'": The speech is from the polytheists addressing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and accusing him of fabricating lies against Allah. For changing one saying for another, and being firm on an opinion then turning away from it, is something from which the presence of the Lord of Might is transcendent.

They exaggerated in their saying, as they did not say: "You fabricated in this change and abrogation," but rather said: إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُفْتَرٍ — "You are just a fabricator," restricting him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to fabrication. They used a nominal sentence and called him a fabricator. They built this on the premise that what the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) brought is of one kind, and he attributes all of it to his Lord, saying: "I am only a warner." Thus, if he were a fabricator in one thing, he would be a fabricator in everything; so he is nothing but a fabricator.

His saying: بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ — "Rather, most of them do not know": That is, most of these polytheists who accuse you by saying, "You are just a fabricator," do not know the reality of this change and the wisdom leading to it—as will be revealed in the answer—that divine rulings follow the interests (المصالح) of the servants. Among interests are those that change with the change of situations, conditions, and times. Thus, it is necessary for the ruling to change with the change of its interest; so the ruling whose necessitating interest has departed is abrogated by another ruling whose interest has arisen.

Most of these people are heedless of this matter. As for the few among them, they are aware of the reality of the matter, even if only in general. However, they are arrogant toward the truth and antagonistic to it, and they only cast the word without regard for the side of truth.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ نَزَّلَهُ رُوحُ الْقُدُسِ مِنْ رَبِّكَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيُثَبِّتَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَهُدىً وَبُشْرى لِلْمُسْلِمِينَ — "Say, 'The Holy Spirit has brought it down from your Lord with the truth, to strengthen those who have faith, and as a guidance and good news for the Muslims'" (16:102). An allusion to the meaning of "Spirit" (الروح) preceded at the beginning of the Surah. "Holiness" (القدس) is purity and transcendence. It is apparent that the genitive construction is for specification, meaning a spirit pure from the defilements of matter, transcendent above error, mistake, and misguidance. He is the one called in another place in His speech "the Trustworthy Spirit" (الروح الأمين), and in another place "Gabriel" (جبريل) among the angels. He, the Exalted, said: نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ — "The Trustworthy Spirit has brought it down to your heart" (26:193-194), and He said: مَنْ كانَ عَدُوًّا لِجِبْرِيلَ فَإِنَّهُ نَزَّلَهُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ — "Whoever is an enemy to Gabriel [should know that] it is he who has brought it down to your heart" (2:97).

So His saying: قُلْ نَزَّلَهُ رُوحُ الْقُدُسِ مِنْ رَبِّكَ — "Say, 'The Holy Spirit has brought it down from your Lord'" is a command for the answer. What first comes to mind is that the pronoun refers to the Qur'an from the perspective of its being an abrogator—i.e., the abrogating verse. It is possible that it refers to the Qur'an absolutely. In the use of the intensive form "brought down" (تنزيل) rather than "sent down" (إنزال), there is an allusion to gradualness (التدريج).

The natural flow of the speech would have been to say: "from my Lord" (من ربي), but it was turned away from that to His saying: مِنْ رَبِّكَ — "from your Lord" to indicate the perfection of care and mercy toward him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). It is as if He does not accept the interruption of His address to him, so He seizes the opportunity to speak to him wherever possible, and to indicate that what is intended by the commanded "saying" is informing them of that, not merely pronouncing these words. So understand.

His saying: لِيُثَبِّتَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا — "to strengthen those who have faith": Strengthening (التثبيت) is making steadfastness firm and emphasizing it by casting steadfastness after steadfastness upon them. It is as if by the root of their faith in Allah, His Messenger, and the Last Day, they became steady upon the truth. By the renewal of the ruling according to the renewal of the interest, they are given steadfastness upon steadfastness, without their first steadfastness weakening by continuing upon actions that do not match the interest of the time. For it is clear that one who is commanded to follow a path for the interest of a goal, and takes to following it out of faith in the guiding command, covering a portion of it according to what he is commanded out of regard for the interest of the goal—whether quickly or slowly, by night or by day—then the nature of the interest changes: if the guiding command did not change the manner of following and he continued upon his previous command, the faith of the follower would weaken and its pillars would be stripped away. But if he is commanded with a new manner of following that agrees with the interest and guarantees happiness, his faith increases in steadfastness upon steadfastness.

Thus, in the bringing down of the Qur'an with abrogation and the renewal of the ruling according to the renewal of the interest, there is a strengthening for those who believe and a giving to them of steadfastness upon steadfastness.

His saying: وَهُدىً وَبُشْرى لِلْمُسْلِمِينَ — "and as a guidance and good news for the Muslims": These are those who submit (يسلمون) the ruling to Allah without objection. Thus, the abrogating verse for them is a showing of the way and glad tidings of happiness and Paradise.

The differentiation of effects—by specifying steadfastness (التثبيت) for the believers (المؤمنين), and guidance (الهدى) and good news (البشرى) for the Muslims (المسلمين)—is only due to the distinction between faith (الإيمان) and submission (الإسلام). Faith pertains to the heart, and its portion is steadfastness in knowledge and conviction; whereas submission pertains to outward action and the stage of the physical limbs, and its portion is being guided toward obligatory deeds, and the good news that the ultimate end is Paradise and felicity.

Some discussion regarding abrogation (النسخ) has already passed in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: مَا نَنْسَخْ مِنْ آيَةٍ أَوْ نُنْسِهَا نَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ مِنْهَا أَوْ مِثْلِهَا "Any verse that We abrogate or cause to be forgotten, We bring another which is better than it, or one like it" (2:106), in the first volume of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ "We certainly know that they say, ‘It is only a human that teaches him.’" This is another fabrication of theirs against the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, namely their saying: إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ "It is only a human that teaches him." As suggested by the context of their objection and the response given to it, there was a non-Arab (أعجمي) man there, whose speech was not eloquent, who possessed some knowledge of religious doctrines and prophetic traditions. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله would perhaps meet him, so they accused him of taking what he claimed to be revelation from this man, and that it was this man who was teaching him. This is what Allah, the Exalted, recounted of their saying: إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ "It is only a human that teaches him." There is a conciseness (إيجاز) in this statement, the implied meaning being: "It is only a human who teaches him, and he attributes what he learns from him to Allah as a fabrication against Him." This is evident.

It is well known that responding to this by merely stating that the man’s tongue is non-Arab while the Quran is clear Arabic does not uproot the doubt from its source. This is because it is possible for the man to convey the subject matter to him in his non-Arab tongue, and then he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—through the eloquence of his speech—casts it into the mold of eloquent Arabic. In fact, this is the first thing that comes to mind from their saying: إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ "It is only a human that teaches him," since they expressed this using the term "teaching" (التعليم) rather than "dictation" (التلقين) or "recitation" (الإملاء), and teaching is closer to meanings (المعاني) than it is to words (الألفاظ).

From this, it becomes clear that His saying: لِسَانُ الَّذِي يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ "The tongue of him they hint at..." until His saying "...clear (مُبِينٌ)" is not the sole response to their doubt; rather, the speech that follows it until the end of the two subsequent verses completes the response.

The summary of the response, derived from all three verses, is: As for your accusation that a human teaches him and then he attributes it to Allah as a fabrication—if you mean that he teaches him the Quran in its literal wording by dictating it to him, and that the Quran is his speech and not the speech of Allah, then the answer is that this man’s tongue is non-Arab (أعجمي) while this Quran is clear Arabic (عربي مبين).

And if you mean that the man teaches him the meanings of the Quran—while the wording is inevitably the Prophet’s صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—and he attributes it to Allah as a fabrication against Him, then the answer is that what the Quran contains are true insights (معارف حقة) in which no person of intellect (ذو لب) can doubt, and which the intellects are compelled to accept. Allah has guided the Prophet to them, so he is a believer in the signs of Allah; for had he not been a believer, Allah would not have guided him, and Allah does not guide those who do not believe in His signs. Since he is a believer in the signs of Allah, he does not fabricate lies against Allah, for none fabricate lies against Him except those who do not believe in His signs. Thus, this Quran is not a fabrication, nor is it taken from a human and falsely attributed to Allah, the Glorified.

So His saying: لِسَانُ الَّذِي يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ أَعْجَمِيٌّ وَهَذَا لِسَانٌ عَرَبِيٌّ مُبِينٌ "The tongue of him they hint at is non-Arab, and this is a clear Arabic tongue" is a response to the first part of the doubt, which is that the Quran in its wording was taken from a human through dictation. The meaning is: the tongue of the man toward whom they hint (يُلْحِدُونَ)—meaning they incline toward him and intend him by their saying: إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ "It is only a human that teaches him"—is non-Arab (أعجمي), meaning it is not eloquent or clear; whereas this Quran recited to you is a clear Arabic tongue. How can the production of an eloquent Arabic discourse be imagined from a man whose tongue is non-Arab?

And His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ "Indeed those who do not believe..." until the end of the two verses is a response to the second part of the doubt, which is that he learns the meanings from him and then attributes them to Allah as a fabrication.

The meaning is: Indeed, those who do not believe in the signs of Allah and disbelieve in them, Allah does not guide them to Himself or to His manifest true insights, and for them is a painful punishment. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is a believer in the signs of Allah because he is guided by the guidance of Allah. Only those who do not believe in the signs of Allah fabricate lies and attribute them to Allah, and it is they who are the liars, persisting in falsehood. As for the likes of the Prophet, who believes in the signs of Allah, he does not fabricate lies nor does he lie. Thus, the two verses are an allusion (كناية) to the fact that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is guided by the guidance of Allah and is a believer in His signs, and one like him does not fabricate or lie.

The commentators (المفسرون) have severed these two verses from the first verse, making the first verse the complete response to the doubt. However, you have seen that it does not suffice as a full response.

Furthermore, they interpreted His saying: وَهَذَا لِسَانٌ عَرَبِيٌّ مُبِينٌ "and this is a clear Arabic tongue" as a challenge (تحدي) regarding the miraculous nature (إعجاز) of the Quran in its eloquence. Yet you know that there is no mention in the wording of the verse that the Quran is miraculous in its eloquence, nor is there any trace of a challenge. The extent of what is in it is that it is clear Arabic, and there is no way for a non-Arab to express it or articulate it.

Then they interpreted the two following verses as a threat to those disbelievers in the signs of Allah who accused His Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله of fabrication, promising them a painful punishment, and turning the accusation of fabrication and lying back upon them—that they are more deserving of fabrication and lying since they do not believe in the signs of Allah, for Allah has not guided them.

They then spoke, based on this premise, regarding the individual words of the two verses in a manner that further distances them from the true meaning. You have already seen that this leads to the response being insufficient in uprooting the objection from its source.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad narrated from ‘Uthman ibn Abi al-‘As, who said: "I was sitting with the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله when his gaze became fixed. He then said: 'Gabriel came to me and commanded me to place this verse in this position of the Surah: إِنَّ اللهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَالْإِحْسَانِ "Indeed Allah enjoins justice and kindness..." until His saying "...take admonition (تَذَكَّرُونَ)."'"

I say: It was also narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas from ‘Uthman ibn Maz‘un رضي الله عنه.

In al-Majma‘: A narration states that ‘Uthman ibn Maz‘un said: "I had embraced Islam out of shyness before the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—due to how often he would present Islam to me—but Islam had not settled in my heart. One day, I was with him while he was in a state of contemplation. His gaze turned toward the sky as if he were seeking to understand something. When that state left him, I asked him about his condition. He said: 'Yes, while I was speaking to you, I saw Gabriel in the air. He brought me this verse: إِنَّ اللهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَالْإِحْسَانِ "Indeed Allah enjoins justice and kindness..."' He recited it to me until its end, and then Islam settled in my heart.

I went to his uncle Abu Talib and informed him. He said: 'O people of Quraysh, follow Muhammad and you will be guided, for he only commands you toward noble character (مكارم الأخلاق).' I then went to al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah and recited this verse to him. He said: 'If Muhammad said it, then how excellent is what he said; and if his Lord said it, then how excellent is what He said.' He said: So Allah revealed: أَفَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي تَوَلَّى وَأَعْطَى قَلِيلاً وَأَكْدَى 'Did you see him who turned away, and gave a little, then stopped?'" (the tradition continues).

In the same source, from ‘Ikrimah, who said: "The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله recited this verse to al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah. He said: 'O son of my brother, repeat it.' So he repeated it. He said: 'Indeed, it has a sweetness, and it has a grace; its top is fruitful and its bottom is lush, and it is not the speech of a human.'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain of narration from Isma‘il ibn Muslim from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام regarding the verse: "Allah has no command for His servants except justice (العدل) and kindness (الإحسان)."

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibn Babawayh with his chain of narration from ‘Amr ibn ‘Uthman, who said: "‘Ali عليه‌السلام came out to his companions while they were discussing chivalry (المروءة). He said: 'Where do you stand regarding the Book of Allah?' They said: 'O Commander of the Faithful, in which place?' He said: 'In His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: إِنَّ اللهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَالْإِحْسَانِ "Indeed Allah enjoins justice and kindness." Justice (العدل) is fairness (الإنصاف), and kindness (الإحسان) is graciousness (التفضل).'"

I say: It was narrated by al-‘Ayyashi from ‘Amr ibn ‘Uthman al-‘Asi from him عليه‌السلام. It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn al-Najjar in his history via the route of al-‘Ukli from his father from him عليه‌السلام, and its wording is: "‘Ali ibn Abi Talib passed by a group of people talking. He said: 'What are you engaged in?' They said: 'We are discussing chivalry.' He said: 'Has Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, not sufficed you of that in His Book when He says: إِنَّ اللهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَالْإِحْسَانِ "Indeed Allah enjoins justice and kindness"? Justice is fairness and kindness is graciousness.'"

I say: In several narrations, justice (العدل) has been interpreted as monotheism (التوحيد) or the two testimonies of faith (الشهادتين), and kindness (الإحسان) as the mastership (الولاية). In others, the prohibition of breaking covenants is linked to the obligation of remaining steadfast upon the mastership.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ عَمِلَ صَالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثَى وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ حَيَاةً طَيِّبَةً "Whoever acts righteously, [whether] male or female, should he be faithful, We shall surely revive him with a good life" (16:97), he said: "He عليه‌السلام said: 'Contentment (القنوع).'"

In al-Ma‘ani, with its chain of narration from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr from some of his companions from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "It was said to him: 'Abu al-Khattab mentions from you that you said: "Once you know the truth, do as you wish."' He said: 'May Allah curse Abu al-Khattab! By Allah, I did not say it like that. Rather, I said to him: "Once you know the truth, do whatever good you wish, and it will be accepted from you." For Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: مَنْ عَمِلَ صَالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثَى وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُولَئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ يُرْزَقُونَ فِيهَا بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ "Whoever acts righteously, whether male or female, should he be faithful—such shall enter paradise, and they will be provided therein without any reckoning" (40:40), and He says: مَنْ عَمِلَ صَالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثَى وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ حَيَاةً طَيِّبَةً "Whoever acts righteously, [whether] male or female, should he be faithful, We shall surely revive him with a good life" (16:97).'"

I say: This is what we previously presented regarding the meaning of the verse.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Abu Basir from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "I said to him: فَإِذَا قَرَأْتَ الْقُرْآنَ فَاسْتَعِذْ بِاللهِ "When you recite the Quran, seek the protection of Allah..." until His saying "...trust (يَتَوَكَّلُونَ)." He said: 'O Muhammad, by Allah, he [Satan] may be given authority over the body of a believer, but he is not given authority over his religion. He was given authority over Job (Ayub) and his physical form was disfigured, but he was not given authority over his religion. He may be given authority over the bodies of believers, but he is not given authority over their religion.'

I said to him: 'His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: إِنَّمَا سُلْطَانُهُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَوَلَّوْنَهُ وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ بِهِ مُشْرِكُونَ "His authority is only over those who befriend him and those who make him a partner [of Allah]" (16:100)?' He said: 'Those who associate partners with Allah—he is given authority over their bodies and over their religions.'"

I say: It was narrated by al-‘Ayyashi from Abu Basir from him (peace be upon him). Returning the pronoun in "with Him" (بِهِ) to Allah is one of the two meanings in the verse.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Hakim narrated and authenticated it, as did al-Bayhaqi in Shu‘ab al-Iman, from Ibn ‘Abbas: "Regarding His saying: إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ 'It is only a human that teaches him,' he said: 'They said: "‘Abdah ibn al-Hadrami, who is a man of books, is only teaching Muhammad." So Allah said: لِسَانُ الَّذِي يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ أَعْجَمِيٌّ وَهَذَا لِسَانٌ عَرَبِيٌّ مُبِينٌ "The tongue of him they hint at is non-Arab, and this is a clear Arabic tongue."'"

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn ‘Azamah al-Sayrafi from someone who informed him from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, created the Holy Spirit (روح القدس), and He did not create a creation closer to Allah than it, though it is not the most noble of His creation to Him. When He intends a matter, He casts it to it, and it casts it to the stars, and so it proceeds. His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ لِسَانُ الَّذِي يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ 'We certainly know that they say, "It is only a human that teaches him." The tongue of him they hint at...'—it was the tongue of Abu Fakihah, the client (مولى) of the Banu al-Hadrami. He was non-Arab (أعجمي) of tongue, and he had followed the Prophet of Allah and believed in him, and he was from the People of the Book. So Quraysh said: 'This man, by Allah, teaches Muhammad his knowledge in his tongue.' Allah says: وَهَذَا لِسَانٌ عَرَبِيٌّ مُبِينٌ 'and this is a clear Arabic tongue.'"

I say: The narrations regarding this man differ. In this narration, he is Abu Fakihah, the client of the Banu al-Hadrami. In the previous narration, he is ‘Abdah ibn al-Hadrami. From Qatadah, it is said he was ‘Abdah ibn al-Hadrami and was called Maqis. From al-Suddi, it is said he was a Christian slave of the Banu al-Hadrami who had read the Torah and the Gospel, called Abu Bishr. From Mujahid, it is said he was the son of al-Hadrami, a non-Arab who spoke Roman (Byzantine Greek). From Ibn ‘Abbas, in another narration, it is said he was a blacksmith in Mecca named Bal‘am, who was non-Arab of tongue; the polytheists would see the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله entering and leaving his presence, so they said: "Only Bal‘am teaches him."

What is certain from their contents is that he was a Roman man, a client of the Banu al-Hadrami living in Mecca, a Christian with expertise in the books of the People of the Book, whom they accused of teaching the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from al-Dahhak regarding the verse: "They used to say: 'Only Salman the Persian teaches him.' So Allah revealed: لِسَانُ الَّذِي يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ أَعْجَمِيٌّ 'The tongue of him they hint at is non-Arab.'"

I say: This does not align with the verse being Meccan.

In the same source, Ibn al-Khara’iti in Masawi al-Akhlaq and Ibn ‘Asakir in his history narrated from ‘Abdullah ibn Jarad: "He asked the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: 'Does a believer commit adultery?' He said: 'That may happen.' He asked: 'Does a believer steal?' He said: 'That may happen.' He asked: 'Does a believer lie?' He said: 'No.' Then the Prophet of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله followed it with: إِنَّمَا يَفْتَرِي الْكَذِبَ الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ 'Only they fabricate lies who do not believe...'"

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from al-‘Abbas ibn al-Hilal from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida عليه‌السلام: "He mentioned a man who was a liar, then said: 'Allah said: إِنَّمَا يَفْتَرِي الْكَذِبَ الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ "Only they fabricate lies who do not believe..."'"

Ayahs

مَن كَفَرَ بِٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ إِيمَـٰنِهِۦٓ إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ وَقَلْبُهُۥ مُطْمَئِنٌّۢ بِٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ وَلَـٰكِن مَّن شَرَحَ بِٱلْكُفْرِ صَدْرًا فَعَلَيْهِمْ غَضَبٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٠٦)
Excepting someone who is compelled [to recant his faith] while his heart is at rest in it, those who disbelieve in Allah after [affirming] their faith and open up their breasts to unfaith, Allah’s wrath shall be upon them and there is a great punishment for them. (106)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمُ ٱسْتَحَبُّوا۟ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا عَلَى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (١٠٧)
That, because they preferred the life of the world to the Hereafter and that Allah does not guide the faithless lot. (107)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ طَبَعَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَسَمْعِهِمْ وَأَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْغَـٰفِلُونَ (١٠٨)
They are the ones Allah has set a seal on their hearts, their hearing and their sight, and it is they who are the heedless. (108)
لَا جَرَمَ أَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (١٠٩)
Undoubtedly, they are the ones who will be the losers in the Hereafter. (109)
ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا فُتِنُوا۟ ثُمَّ جَـٰهَدُوا۟ وَصَبَرُوٓا۟ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهَا لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١١٠)
Thereafter your Lord will indeed be forgiving and merciful to those who migrated after they were persecuted, waged jihad and remained steadfast. (110)
۞ يَوْمَ تَأْتِى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ تُجَـٰدِلُ عَن نَّفْسِهَا وَتُوَفَّىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا عَمِلَتْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (١١١)
The day [will come] when every soul will come pleading for itself and every soul will be recompensed fully for what it has done, and they will not be wronged. (111)

(Commentary)

The verses contain a threat against disbelieving after having believed—which is apostasy (الارتداد)—and a beautiful promise for the emigrants (المهاجرين) after they were persecuted, as well as for those who strive (المجاهدين) and are patient (الصابرين) for the sake of Allah. They also address the ruling of precautionary dissimulation (التقية).

His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ كَفَرَ بِاللهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ إِيمانِهِ إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ — "Whoever disbelieves in Allah after his belief—excepting him who is forced..." [QARAI TRANSLATION: 16:106]. Tranquility (الاطمئنان) is stillness and stability (السكون والاستقرار), and expansion (الشرح) is opening up (البسط). It is stated in al-Mufradat: "The root of sharh (الشرح) is the slicing and opening of meat and the like. It is said: 'I sliced the meat' (شرحت اللحم). From this comes the 'expansion of the chest' (شرح الصدر), meaning its expansion with divine light (نور إلهي), tranquility (سكينة) from Allah, and a spirit (روح) from Him. The Exalted said: رَبِّ اشْرَحْ لِي صَدْرِي — 'My Lord! Open my breast for me' [QARAI TRANSLATION: 20:25], and أَلَمْ نَشْرَحْ لَكَ صَدْرَكَ — 'Did We not open your breast for you?' [QARAI TRANSLATION: 94:1], and أَفَمَنْ شَرَحَ اللهُ صَدْرَهُ — 'Is he whose breast Allah has opened...' [QARAI TRANSLATION: 39:22]. To 'expand' (شرح) difficult speech means to elaborate upon it and reveal its hidden meanings." End quote.

His saying: مَنْ كَفَرَ بِاللهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ إِيمانِهِ — "Whoever disbelieves in Allah after his belief" is a condition (شرط) whose apodosis (جواب) is His saying: فَعَلَيْهِمْ غَضَبٌ مِنَ اللهِ — "on such shall be Allah’s wrath" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 16:106]. Coupled to this is His saying: وَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ — "and there is a great punishment for them" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 16:106]. The plural pronoun in the retribution (الجزاء) refers back to the conditional noun (اسم الشرط) "whoever" (مَنْ) because, in terms of meaning, it is a universal (كلي) possessing multiple individuals.

His saying: إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ وَقَلْبُهُ مُطْمَئِنٌّ بِالْإِيمانِ — "excepting him who is forced while his heart is at rest in faith" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 16:106] is an exception from the generality of the condition. What is meant by compulsion (الإكراه) is being forced to utter the word of disbelief and to manifest it outwardly, for the heart does not accept compulsion. The meaning is: "Excepted is he who is forced into disbelief after belief, so he disbelieves outwardly while his heart is at rest in faith (الإيمان) — the inner conviction and submission to the truth."

His saying: وَلكِنْ مَنْ شَرَحَ بِالْكُفْرِ صَدْراً — "but those who open their breasts to unfaith" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 16:106], meaning he expanded his chest for disbelief (الكفر), accepting it with contentment and harboring it. This sentence is a rectification (استدراك) of the exception, thus returning to the meaning of the excepted-from (المستثنى منه). The sense is: "What I intended by My saying: 'Whoever disbelieves in Allah after his belief' was not the one who is forced while his heart is at rest in faith, but rather I intended the one who opens his breast to disbelief." In the combination of the exception and the rectification, there is a complete clarification of the condition. This is the subtle point (النكتة) for interposing the exception between the condition and the apodosis, rather than delaying it until the conditional sentence was complete.

It has been said that His saying: مَنْ كَفَرَ — "Whoever disbelieves" is an appositive (بدل) for الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِآياتِ اللهِ — "those who do not believe in Allah’s signs" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 16:105] in the previous verse, and that His saying: وَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْكاذِبُونَ — "and it is they who are the liars" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 16:105] is a parenthetical sentence (جملة معترضة). Furthermore, His saying: إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ — "excepting him who is forced" is an exception from that, and His saying: وَلكِنْ مَنْ شَرَحَ — "but those who open" is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (خبر)—or what stands in its place—is His saying: فَعَلَيْهِمْ غَضَبٌ مِنَ اللهِ — "on such shall be Allah’s wrath."

The meaning—according to this view—would be: "Only those who disbelieve after their belief fabricate lies, except him who is forced while his heart is at rest in faith." At that point, the speech would be complete, and then He would begin by saying: "But those who open their breasts to unfaith, on such shall be Allah’s wrath."

Sound taste (الذوق السليم) is sufficient to dismiss this view, given its feebleness (السخافة).

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمُ اسْتَحَبُّوا الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا عَلَى الْآخِرَةِ وَأَنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْكافِرِينَ — "That is because they preferred the life of this world to the Hereafter, and because Allah does not guide the faithless lot" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 16:107]. This is an explanation of the reason why Allah's wrath befell them and why the great punishment was established against them. It is because they chose the life of this world (الحياة الدنيا)—which is the material life (الحياة المادية) that has no goal other than animalistic enjoyment and preoccupation with the desires of the self—over the Hereafter (الآخرة), which is the permanent, eternal life in the proximity of the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين), and is the ultimate goal of human life.

In other words, these people desired nothing but the world, severed themselves from the Hereafter, and disbelieved in it. And Allah does not guide the faithless (الكافرين). Since Allah did not guide them, they strayed from the path of felicity, Paradise, and Divine Pleasure (الرضوان), thus falling into Allah's wrath and a great punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ طَبَعَ اللهُ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ وَسَمْعِهِمْ وَأَبْصارِهِمْ وَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْغافِلُونَ — "They are the ones on whose hearts, hearing, and sight Allah has set a seal, and it is they who are the heedless" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 16:108]. This is an indication that choosing the life of this world over the Hereafter and being deprived of the guidance of Allah, the Glorified, is the description of those whose hearts, hearing, and sight Allah has sealed (طبع), and who are called the heedless (الغافلون).

By choosing the life of this world as their ultimate goal and being deprived of guidance to the next, they severed themselves from the Hereafter and became attached to the world, making it their sole end. Consequently, their senses and intellect stopped at the world, failing to transcend it to what lies beyond—the Hereafter. Thus, they do not see that which they should take as a lesson, nor do they hear an admonition by which they might be exhorted, nor do they intellectually grasp an argument by which they might be guided to the Hereafter.

Thus, their hearts, hearing, and sight are sealed; neither their hearts, nor their hearing, nor their sight can reach what would point them toward the Hereafter. They are heedless (غافلون) of it, not awakening to any of its affairs.

It becomes clear that the description in the previous verse serves as a definition for the sealing (الطبع) and heedlessness (الغفلة) mentioned in this verse. Allah's withdrawal of guidance from them following their attachment to the world is the meaning of the seal and heedlessness. The seal (الطبع) is a divine act attributed to Him, the Exalted, which He performs upon them as a recompense (مجازاة), while heedlessness (الغفلة) is an attribute attributed to themselves.

His saying, the Exalted: لا جَرَمَ أَنَّهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ هُمُ الْخاسرُونَ — "Undoubtedly, they are the losers in the Hereafter" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 16:109]. This is because they squandered their capital in the world, so they remained without provisions to live by in their Hereafter. A similar expression occurs in Surah Hud: لا جَرَمَ أَنَّهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ هُمُ الْأَخْسَرُونَ — "Undoubtedly, they are the ones who will be the greatest losers in the Hereafter" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 11:22]. Perhaps the reason for the emphasis there is that He, the Exalted, added to their attributes there that they barred others from the way of Allah; so refer back to it.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما فُتِنُوا ثُمَّ جاهَدُوا وَصَبَرُوا إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنْ بَعْدِها لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — "Then indeed your Lord, to those who emigrated after they were persecuted, then waged jihad and were patient—indeed your Lord, after that, is all-forgiving, all-merciful" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 16:110]. Persecution (الفتنة) originally meant putting gold into fire to manifest its quality, then it came to be used for any trial or torture. The Quraysh and the polytheists of Mecca used to persecute the believers to turn them back from their religion, torturing them with various types of punishment until some would die under torture—as they persecuted Ammar and his father and mother. His parents were killed, and Ammar apostatized outwardly, escaping them through precautionary dissimulation (التقية). Regarding this, the previous verses were revealed, as will be mentioned, Allah willing, in the discussion of narrations.

From this, it becomes clear that the verse is connected to what preceded it in His saying: إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ وَقَلْبُهُ مُطْمَئِنٌّ بِالْإِيمانِ — "excepting him who is forced while his heart is at rest in faith." It is in the sense of our saying: "And after all that, indeed Allah is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful to those who emigrated after they were persecuted, then strove and were patient."

So His saying: ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما فُتِنُوا — "Then indeed your Lord, to those who emigrated after they were persecuted" is a beautiful promise of forgiveness and mercy on the Day of Resurrection for the emigrants after they were persecuted, in contrast to the total loss He promised to others on that day. This was conditioned upon striving (الجهاد) and patience (الصبر) after emigration.

His saying: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنْ بَعْدِها لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — "indeed your Lord, after that, is all-forgiving, all-merciful" is like a summary of the beginning of the speech—due to its length—so that the end may be joined to it. It serves the purpose of emphasis (التأكيد), like our saying: "Zayd is in the house, Zayd is in the house doing such and such." It also indicates that the mentioned conditions of the speech have an impact on the ruling; thus, Allah, the Glorified, is not pleased with them unless they emigrate, nor with their emigration unless they strive after it and remain patient.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تَأْتِي كُلُّ نَفْسٍ تُجادِلُ عَنْ نَفْسِها وَتُوَفَّى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ — "The day when every soul will come pleading for itself, and every soul will be paid fully for what it has done, and they will not be wronged" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 16:111]. The "coming" of the soul on the Day of Resurrection is a metaphor for its presence before the King and Judge (الديان), as He said: فَإِنَّهُمْ لَمُحْضَرُونَ — "they will surely be hauled [into it]" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 37:127]. The pronoun in His saying: عَنْ نَفْسِها — "for itself" refers to the soul (النفس), and there is no harm in adding "soul" to the pronoun of "soul." For "soul" (النفس) is sometimes intended to mean the human person (الشخص الإنساني), as in His saying: مَنْ قَتَلَ نَفْساً بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ — "whoever slays a soul, without [its being guilty of] slaying a soul" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 5:32]. Sometimes it is intended for emphasis (التأكيد), where its meaning is identical to the emphasized word preceding it, whether it be a human or otherwise, as when it is said: "the man himself" (الإنسان نفسه), "the horse itself" (الفرس نفسه), "the stone itself" (الحجر نفسه), or "the blackness itself" (السواد نفسه). It is also said: "the soul of the man," "the soul of the horse," "the soul of the stone," and "the soul of the blackness." In His saying: عَنْ نَفْسِها, the first "soul" (the mudaf) is intended in the second sense, and the second "soul" (the mudaf ilayh) is intended in the first sense. The use of the pronoun has removed the ugliness of repeating the word through genitive construction. This amount is sufficient to bypass the lengthy discussions provided by the commentators.

His saying: يَوْمَ تَأْتِي كُلُّ نَفْسٍ تُجادِلُ عَنْ نَفْسِها — "The day when every soul will come pleading for itself" is an adverbial phrase (الظرف) related to His saying in the previous verse: لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — "is all-forgiving, all-merciful." The soul's "pleading for itself" is its defense of itself, having forgotten everything besides itself—contrary to its state in the world where it was attached to everything except itself by forgetting itself. This is only because the reality of the matter has become manifest to it: that man has no path to what is beyond his own soul, and in reality, he has nothing but to be occupied with his soul.

So on that day, the soul comes and is present for the reckoning (الحساب), pleading and insisting on defending itself with whatever excuses it can muster.

His saying: وَتُوَفَّى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ — "and every soul will be paid fully for what it has done, and they will not be wronged." Full payment (التوفية) is the giving of a right in its entirety without any reduction. He linked the full payment to the action itself when He said: ما عَمِلَتْ — "what it has done," thereby indicating that what is given is the action itself, without it being subjected to change or substitution. In this is the perfection of justice (العدل), as nothing is added to what it deserved, nor is anything subtracted from it; therefore, He followed it with His saying: وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ — "and they will not be wronged."

In the verse, there is an indication:

First: That no soul will defend or plead for another on the Day of Resurrection; rather, it will only be occupied with itself, having no leisure for anyone else, as He said: يَوْمَ لا يُغْنِي مَوْلًى عَنْ مَوْلًى شَيْئاً — "the day when a friend will not avail a friend in any way" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 44:41], and He said: يَوْمَ لا يَنْفَعُ مالٌ وَلا بَنُونَ — "the day when neither wealth nor children will avail" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 26:88], and He said: يَوْمٌ لا بَيْعٌ فِيهِ وَلا خُلَّةٌ وَلا شَفاعَةٌ — "a day in which there will be no bargaining, neither friendship, nor intercession" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 2:254].

Second: That the pleading will not benefit it at all in averting the retribution it has deserved, for what it is recompensed with is the very essence of what it did. There is no way to change this relationship, and it is not an act of injustice in any way.

(Discussion of Narrations)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "When the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله intended to emigrate to Medina, he said to his companions: 'Disperse from me. Whoever among you has strength, let him delay until the end of the night, and whoever does not have strength, let him go at the beginning of the night. When you hear that I have settled in the land, then join me.'

So Bilal the Mu'adhin, Khabbab, and Ammar—and a slave girl from Quraysh who had embraced Islam—remained in Mecca until morning. The polytheists and Abu Jahl seized them. They presented Bilal with the demand to disbelieve, but he refused. They began placing an iron armor on him in the sun, then they would make him wear it. When they made him wear it, he would say: 'One, One' (أحد أحد). As for Khabbab, they began dragging him over thorns.

As for Ammar, he said a word to them that pleased them out of precautionary dissimulation (تقية). As for the slave girl, Abu Jahl drove four stakes into the ground for her, then stretched her out and thrust a spear into her heart until he killed her. Then they let Bilal, Khabbab, and Ammar go, and they joined the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. They informed him of what had happened. What Ammar had spoken weighed heavily upon him, so the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to him: 'How was your heart when you said what you said? Was it expanded by what you said or not?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'And Allah revealed: إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ وَقَلْبُهُ مُطْمَئِنٌّ بِالْإِيمانِ — "excepting him who is forced while his heart is at rest in faith."'"

I say: The slave girl mentioned in the narration is Sumayyah, the mother of Ammar. Yasir, the father of Ammar, was also with them. It is said that the father of Ammar and his mother were the first two martyrs in Islam. The narrations are widespread (مستفيضة) regarding their being killed through persecution (الفتنة), Ammar's outward manifestation of disbelief out of precautionary dissimulation (تقية), and the revelation of the verse concerning him.

In the same source, Abd al-Razzaq, Ibn Sa'd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il narrated via Abu Ubaydah ibn Muhammad ibn Ammar from his father, who said: "The polytheists seized Ammar ibn Yasir and did not let him go until he insulted the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and mentioned their gods with goodness. Then they let him go.

When he came to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, he said: 'What is behind you?' He said: 'Evil. I was not left until I spoke ill of you and mentioned their gods with goodness.' He said: 'How do you find your heart?' He said: 'At rest in faith.'

He said: 'If they return, then return [to what you said].' Then was revealed: إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ وَقَلْبُهُ مُطْمَئِنٌّ بِالْإِيمانِ."

In al-Majma', from Ibn Abbas and Qatadah: "The verse was revealed concerning a group who were forced—they were Ammar, Yasir his father, his mother Sumayyah, Suhayb, Bilal, and Khabbab. They were tortured. The father of Ammar and his mother were killed, and Ammar gave them with his tongue what they wanted from him. Then the Glorified informed the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. Some people said: 'Ammar has disbelieved.' But he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Nay, indeed Ammar is filled with faith from his crown to his feet, and faith has mixed with his flesh and blood.'

Ammar came to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله while he was weeping. He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'What is behind you?' He said: 'Evil, O Messenger of Allah. I was not left until I spoke ill of you and mentioned their gods with goodness.' The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله began wiping his eyes and saying: 'If they return to you, then return to them with what you said.' Then the verse was revealed."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Sa'd narrated from Umar ibn al-Hakam, who said: "Ammar ibn Yasir was tortured until he did not know what he was saying. Suhayb was tortured until he did not know what he was saying. Abu Fukayhah was tortured until he did not know what he was saying. Bilal, Amir, Ibn Fuhayrah, and a group of Muslims—concerning them this verse was revealed: ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما فُتِنُوا — 'Then indeed your Lord, to those who emigrated after they were persecuted...'"

I say: Among them named in some narrations are Abbas ibn Abi Rabi'ah, and in others, he and al-Walid ibn Abi Rabi'ah, al-Walid ibn al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah, and Abu Jandal ibn Suhayl ibn Amr. The most comprehensive narration in this regard is from Ibn Abbas: "This verse was revealed concerning those of the companions of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله who were being persecuted: ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما فُتِنُوا."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Abu Amr al-Zubayri from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام in a tradition, he said: "As for what is obligated upon the heart regarding faith (الإيمان)—acknowledgment (الإقرار), gnosis (المعرفة), the knot of conviction (العقد), contentment (الرضا), and submission (التسليم)—it is that there is no god but Allah alone, without partner, a single God who has taken neither spouse nor son; and that Muhammad is His servant and Messenger; and the acknowledgment of what he brought from Allah of prophets or books. That is what Allah has obligated upon the heart of acknowledgment and gnosis—and it is its action. It is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ وَقَلْبُهُ مُطْمَئِنٌّ بِالْإِيمانِ ـ وَلكِنْ مَنْ شَرَحَ بِالْكُفْرِ صَدْراً."

In the same source, with its chain of narration from Mas'adah ibn Sadaqah, who said: "It was said to Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام: 'The people narrate that Ali عليه‌السلام said on the pulpit of Kufa: "O people, you will be called to insult me, so insult me; then you will be called to disassociate from me, so do not disassociate from me."' He said: 'How much the people lie against Ali عليه‌السلام!' Then he said: 'He only said: "You will be called to insult me, so insult me; then you will be called to disassociate, and indeed I am upon the religion of Muhammad," and he did not say: "and do not disassociate from me."'

The questioner said to him: 'What if he chooses death over disassociation?' He said: 'By Allah, that is not required of him, and he has nothing [better] than what Ammar ibn Yasir followed when the people of Mecca forced him while his heart was at rest in faith. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to him then: "O Ammar, if they return, then return, for Allah has revealed your excuse: إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ وَقَلْبُهُ مُطْمَئِنٌّ بِالْإِيمانِ—and He has commanded you to return if they return."'"

I say: al-Ayyashi narrated this meaning in his Tafsir from Mu'ammar ibn Yahya ibn Salim from Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام. His saying عليه‌السلام: "and He has commanded you to return if they return" is derived from the verse, as the exception in it did not refer to a specific person but was based on a description (العنوان), which is the compulsion of one whose heart is at rest in faith. As for it being a command from Him, the Exalted, just as the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله commanded it, perhaps the reason is that the explicit meaning of the exception is permissibility (الجواز). With such permissibility, there is no room for refusal, which would be exposing oneself to death and casting oneself into destruction. Thus, this permissibility is joined with obligation (الوجوب) rather than mere license (الإباحة).

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Amr ibn Marwan, who said: "I heard Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام say: 'The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "Four traits have been lifted from my nation: what they do by mistake, what they forget, what they are forced into, and what they cannot bear." And that is in the Book of Allah: إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ وَقَلْبُهُ مُطْمَئِنٌّ بِالْإِيمانِ.'"

[Surah al-Nahl (16): Verses 112 to 128]

Ayahs

وَضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا قَرْيَةً كَانَتْ ءَامِنَةً مُّطْمَئِنَّةً يَأْتِيهَا رِزْقُهَا رَغَدًا مِّن كُلِّ مَكَانٍ فَكَفَرَتْ بِأَنْعُمِ ٱللَّهِ فَأَذَٰقَهَا ٱللَّهُ لِبَاسَ ٱلْجُوعِ وَٱلْخَوْفِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَصْنَعُونَ (١١٢)
Allah draws a parable: There was a town secure and peaceful, its provision coming abundantly from every place. But it was ungrateful toward Allah’s blessings. So Allah made it taste hunger and fear because of what they used to do. (112)
وَلَقَدْ جَآءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مِّنْهُمْ فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ وَهُمْ ظَـٰلِمُونَ (١١٣)
Certainly there had come to them an apostle from among themselves, but they impugned him. So the punishment seized them while they were doing wrong. (113)
فَكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ حَلَـٰلًا طَيِّبًا وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ (١١٤)
So eat out of what Allah has provided you as lawful and good, and give thanks for Allah’s blessing, if it is Him that you worship. (114)
إِنَّمَا حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْمَيْتَةَ وَٱلدَّمَ وَلَحْمَ ٱلْخِنزِيرِ وَمَآ أُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ بِهِۦ ۖ فَمَنِ ٱضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ بَاغٍ وَلَا عَادٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١١٥)
He has forbidden you only carrion, blood, the flesh of swine, and that which has been offered to other than Allah. But if someone is compelled [to eat any of that], without being rebellious or aggressive, indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (115)
وَلَا تَقُولُوا۟ لِمَا تَصِفُ أَلْسِنَتُكُمُ ٱلْكَذِبَ هَـٰذَا حَلَـٰلٌ وَهَـٰذَا حَرَامٌ لِّتَفْتَرُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ لَا يُفْلِحُونَ (١١٦)
Do not say, asserting falsely with your tongues, ‘This is lawful, and that is unlawful,’ attributing lies to Allah. Indeed those who attribute lies to Allah will not be felicitous. (116)
مَتَـٰعٌ قَلِيلٌ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١١٧)
[Their share of the present life is] a trifling enjoyment, and there will be a painful punishment for them. (117)
وَعَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ حَرَّمْنَا مَا قَصَصْنَا عَلَيْكَ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَمَا ظَلَمْنَـٰهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (١١٨)
We forbade to the Jews what We have recounted to you earlier, and We did not wrong them, but they used to wrong themselves. (118)
ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ عَمِلُوا۟ ٱلسُّوٓءَ بِجَهَـٰلَةٍ ثُمَّ تَابُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ وَأَصْلَحُوٓا۟ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهَا لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١١٩)
Moreover, your Lord will indeed be forgiving and merciful to those who repent after they having committed evil out of ignorance and reform themselves. (119)
إِنَّ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ كَانَ أُمَّةً قَانِتًا لِّلَّهِ حَنِيفًا وَلَمْ يَكُ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (١٢٠)
Indeed Abraham was a nation [all by himself], obedient to Allah, a Hanif, and he was not a polytheist. (120)
شَاكِرًا لِّأَنْعُمِهِ ۚ ٱجْتَبَىٰهُ وَهَدَىٰهُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (١٢١)
Grateful [as he was] for His blessings, He chose him and guided him to a straight path. (121)
وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً ۖ وَإِنَّهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١٢٢)
We gave him good in this world, and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the Righteous. (122)
ثُمَّ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ أَنِ ٱتَّبِعْ مِلَّةَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ حَنِيفًا ۖ وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (١٢٣)
Thereafter We revealed to you [saying], ‘Follow the creed of Abraham, a Hanif, who was not a polytheist.’ (123)
إِنَّمَا جُعِلَ ٱلسَّبْتُ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ فِيهِ ۚ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَيَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ (١٢٤)
The Sabbath was only prescribed for those who differed about it. Your Lord will indeed judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they differ. (124)
ٱدْعُ إِلَىٰ سَبِيلِ رَبِّكَ بِٱلْحِكْمَةِ وَٱلْمَوْعِظَةِ ٱلْحَسَنَةِ ۖ وَجَـٰدِلْهُم بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن ضَلَّ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ ۖ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ (١٢٥)
Invite to the way of your Lord with wisdom and good advice and dispute with them in a manner that is best. Indeed your Lord knows best those who stray from His way, and He knows best those who are guided. (125)
وَإِنْ عَاقَبْتُمْ فَعَاقِبُوا۟ بِمِثْلِ مَا عُوقِبْتُم بِهِۦ ۖ وَلَئِن صَبَرْتُمْ لَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لِّلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (١٢٦)
Should you retaliate, retaliate with the like of what you have been made to suffer, but if you are patient that is surely better for the steadfast. (126)
وَٱصْبِرْ وَمَا صَبْرُكَ إِلَّا بِٱللَّهِ ۚ وَلَا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا تَكُ فِى ضَيْقٍ مِّمَّا يَمْكُرُونَ (١٢٧)
So be steadfast, and you cannot be steadfast except with Allah [’s help]. And do not grieve for them, nor be upset by their guile. (127)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوا۟ وَّٱلَّذِينَ هُم مُّحْسِنُونَ (١٢٨)
Indeed Allah is with those who are Godwary and those who are virtuous. (128)

(Commentary)

This section completes the verses concerning legal rulings (آيات الأحكام) mentioned previously, detailing the prohibited and permitted foods. It forbids declaring things lawful or unlawful by way of innovation (ابتداعاً) without Allah’s permission. It also mentions some of the rulings prescribed for the Jews that were subsequently abrogated (نسخت). In this, there is a connection to the earlier discussion on abrogation (النسخ) in His saying: وَإِذا بَدَّلْنا آيَةً مَكانَ آيَةٍ (And when We change a verse in place of another verse [16:101]). It further indicates that what was revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is indeed the religion of Abraham (عليه‌السلام), which is built upon moderation (الاعتدال) and monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — while lifting the severities imposed upon the Jews as a consequence of their wrongdoing.

At the end of the section, there is a command for justice in retribution, an exhortation toward patience (الصبر) and seeking reward from Allah (الاحتساب), and a beautiful promise of divine support and sufficiency if they remain God-conscious (اتقوا) and do good.

His saying, the Exalted: ضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً قَرْيَةً كانَتْ آمِنَةً مُطْمَئِنَّةً يَأْتِيها رِزْقُها رَغَداً (Allah draws a parable: A town that was secure and at rest, its provision coming to it abundantly from every quarter [16:112]) until the end of the verse. Abundance (الرغد) in living refers to that which is spacious and wholesome.

This is a parable (المثل) struck by Allah, the Exalted, in which He describes a town to which He granted all the necessities of life. He perfected all of this by sending a prophet to them, calling them to what would rectify their worldly and otherworldly affairs. However, they were ungrateful for His favors and denied His messenger. Consequently, Allah transformed His blessing into vengeance and punished them for the wrongdoing (الظلم) they committed by rejecting His messenger. Within this parable is a warning against ingratitude for Allah’s blessing (نعمة) after it has been bestowed, and against disbelieving in His signs (آيات) after they have been revealed.

Furthermore, it serves as a preamble and preparation for what He will mention regarding permitted and prohibited foods, and the prohibition of legislating the lawful and unlawful without Allah’s permission. All of this is derived from the context (سياق) of the verses, as each preceding verse directs the gaze toward the succeeding one.

It has been said that this "town" is Mecca; Allah punished them with hunger for seven years when they disbelieved in Allah’s favors—though He had expanded them—and denied His messenger, whom He had sent to them. They were afflicted with drought, and their caravans were raided by the wrath of Allah, the Glorified, after the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prayed against them. This was mentioned in al-Majma‘ and attributed to Ibn Abbas, Mujahid, and Qatadah.

However, while the verse is inherently applicable to what was mentioned, the context of the verses is more suited to it being a general parable mentioned as a preamble and preparation, as we have explained.

His saying: ضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً قَرْيَةً كانَتْ آمِنَةً مُطْمَئِنَّةً يَأْتِيها رِزْقُها رَغَداً (Allah draws a parable: A town that was secure and at rest, its provision coming to it abundantly [16:112]) describes the town with three consecutive attributes. The middle one, "at rest" (مطمئنة), acts as a link between the two ends. For when a town is secure (أمنت) from dangers—such as attacks by evildoers, raids, killing, the taking of captives, and the looting of wealth—and is also secure from natural disasters like earthquakes and the like, it becomes "at rest" and tranquil (سكنت). Thus, its inhabitants are not forced into exodus or dispersal.

A completion of its being "at rest" is that its provision comes to it abundantly from every place, so its people do not need to emigrate, traverse wildernesses, sail the seas, or endure extreme hardships in seeking and bringing provision to it.

Thus, the town’s characterization by these three attributes—security (الأمن), being at rest (الاطمئنان), and the arrival of its provision from every place—perfects and completes for it all material and external blessings. The Glorified will then add spiritual blessings in the following verse: وَلَقَدْ جاءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مِنْهُمْ (Certainly there has come to them an apostle from among themselves [16:113]). Thus, it is a town upon which Allah perfected and completed His favors.

His saying: فَكَفَرَتْ بِأَنْعُمِ اللهِ فَأَذاقَهَا اللهُ لِباسَ الْجُوعِ وَالْخَوْفِ (but it was ungrateful for the blessings of Allah. So Allah made it taste the hunger and fear [16:112]). The use of the term "favors" (أنعم) — which is a plural of paucity (جمع قلة) — is to point to the aforementioned categories, which are three: security, being at rest, and the arrival of provision. "Tasting" (الإذاقة) is a metaphor (استعارة) for a slight touch or reaching. Making them "taste" hunger and fear suggests that He who delivers these is capable of doubling and increasing them beyond measure; and how could it be otherwise? For He is Allah, to Whom belongs all power.

Then, the annexation (إضافة) of "garment" (لباس) to hunger and fear signifies encompassment and total envelopment, just as a garment covers the body and surrounds it. It suggests that even this slight amount of hunger and fear that He made them taste enveloped them like a garment covers a human body, and He, the Glorified, is capable of increasing it further. He is the ultimate in His subduing power (قهر) and dominance (غلبة), while they are the ultimate in their wretchedness and insignificance.

He then concluded the verse with His saying: بِما كانُوا يَصْنَعُونَ (because of what they used to do [16:112]) to indicate that the law of recompense (سنة المجازاة) for gratitude and ingratitude is firmly established.

The meaning is: Allah struck a parable of a town whose people were secure from every evil and harm threatening their lives, honor, and wealth; they were settled and not compelled to move; their provision came to them wholesome and vast from every place without them being forced to travel or emigrate. But the people of this town were ungrateful for these divine favors and did not thank Him, the Glorified. So Allah made them experience a small portion of His vengeance—by stripping away these favors—which was the hunger and fear that generalized and enveloped them in exchange for their persistent ingratitude, as a recompense for their disbelief.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ جاءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مِنْهُمْ فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَخَذَهُمُ الْعَذابُ وَهُمْ ظالِمُونَ (Certainly there has come to them an apostle from among themselves, but they impugned him. So the punishment seized them while they were wrongdoers [16:113]). This is the spiritual blessing (النعمة المعنوية) that He added to the aforementioned material blessings. In it was the rectification of their worldly life and their return to the Hereafter, a warning against ingratitude for Allah’s favors, and an explanation of the ill-omen and misery therein. However, they denied their messenger, who was from among them—they knew him and realized that he was only calling them to a divine matter and guiding them to the path of rectitude (الرشاد) and true happiness. Thus, they wronged themselves, and the punishment seized them because of their wrongdoing (ظلم).

Through this exposition, the subtle points (النكات) within the constraints mentioned in the verse become apparent.

His saying, the Exalted: فَكُلُوا مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللهُ حَلالاً طَيِّباً (So eat of the lawful and good things that Allah has provided you [16:114]) until the end of the verse. This is a derivation (تفريع) based on the result obtained from the parable. The implicit meaning is: if this is the case—that ingratitude for this abundant provision leads to punishment, and denying the call leads to punishment—then eat of what Allah has provided you while it is lawful (حلالاً) and good (طَيِّباً). That is, you are not prohibited from it and you find it wholesome; so eat of it and be grateful for Allah’s favor if it is Him that you worship.

From this, several points become clear:

First: The verse is directed toward the general permissibility of the good things of provision. There is no basis for what some have mentioned—that the meaning is "eat of the spoils of war (الغنائم) that Allah has provided you as lawful and good provision"—based on the claim that the verse was revealed after the Battle of Badr, that the previous parable was struck for the people of Mecca, that the messenger they denied was the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and that the punishment that seized them was the devastating slaughter of their leaders on the day of Badr. All of this lacks evidence from the wording of the verses. Furthermore, it was previously supported that the surah is Meccan.

Second: The meaning of "lawful" (الحل) and "good" (الطيب) is that the provision is such that man is not naturally prohibited from it and his nature finds it wholesome. That is, lawfulness and goodness are according to nature (الطبع), and that is the criterion for legal permissibility (الحلية الشرعية) which follows permissibility according to the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being. For religion is primordial (فطري), as Allah, the Glorified, created man equipped with a digestive system and made certain earthly things from animals and plants suitable for his constitution, toward which his nature inclines without aversion. Thus, he has the right to eat from them, and that is "lawfulness" (الحل).

Third: His saying: فَكُلُوا (So eat) is a preliminary command (أمر مقدمي) in relation to His saying: وَاشْكُرُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ (and give thanks for Allah’s blessing [16:114]). The mention of "blessing" (النعمة) alludes to the reason for the ruling, as the fact that something is a blessing is the reason for the obligation of gratitude (الشكر) for it.

Fourth: His saying: إِنْ كُنْتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ (if it is Him that you worship [16:114]) is an address to the believers (المؤمنين), for they are the ones who worship Allah and do not worship any other. The restriction (القصر) in the sentence—indicated by placing the object before the verb—is a "restriction of reversal" (قصر القلب). Others, namely the polytheists, only worship idols and deities besides Allah.

To make the address directed at the polytheists and claim that "worship" (العبادة) in His saying إِنْ كُنْتُمْ إِيَّاهُ تَعْبُدُونَ means "obedience" (الإطاعة), or that the meaning is "if your claim is true that you intend by your worship of your deities to worship Him, the Exalted," does not lead to any useful conclusion. For defining worship as obedience requires a contextual clue (قرينة), and there is none. Moreover, the polytheists do not worship Allah, the Glorified, even by associating Him in worship, nor do they intend by the worship of their deities to worship Him; rather, they deem Him, the Exalted, far above their worship, as He is too majestic to be reached by perception or for any direction of intent to end at Him.

The fact that the address in the verse is to the believers necessitates that the parable was struck for their sake, and that the other legislative addresses before and after the verse are directed to them. It has been said that the address is to people in general—both believers and disbelievers—but applying this to the verses is not without affectation (تكلف), though it is less problematic than restricting the address to the polytheists.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْمَيْتَةَ وَالدَّمَ وَلَحْمَ الْخِنْزِيرِ وَما أُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ اللهِ بِهِ فَمَنِ اضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ باغٍ وَلا عادٍ فَإِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ (He has only forbidden you carrion, blood, the flesh of swine, and that which has been offered to other than Allah. But should someone be compelled, without being rebellious or transgressive, then Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful [16:115]). The discussion regarding the meaning of this verse has already passed in the commentary (تفسير) of Surah al-Baqarah (verse 173), Surah al-Ma'idah (verse 3), and Surah al-An'am (verse 145).

This verse, with its meaning despite variations in wording, occurs in four places in the Quran: in Surahs al-An'am and al-Nahl, which are Meccan—among the earliest and latest revealed in Mecca—and in Surahs al-Baqarah and al-Ma'idah, which are among the earliest and latest revealed in Medina. It indicates the restriction of prohibited foods to these four: carrion (الميتة), blood (الدم), the flesh of swine (لحم الخنزير), and that which has been offered to other than Allah (ما أهل لغير الله به), as some have noted.

However, by referring to the Sunnah (السنة), it becomes clear that these are the primary prohibitions intended in the Book. Prohibitions other than these four are those which the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) forbade by the command of his Lord, and He, the Exalted, has said: ما آتاكُمُ الرَّسُولُ فَخُذُوهُ وَما نَهاكُمْ عَنْهُ فَانْتَهُوا (Take whatever the Apostle gives you, and relinquish whatever he forbids you [59:7]). Some narrations (روايات) indicating this meaning have already been mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَقُولُوا لِما تَصِفُ أَلْسِنَتُكُمُ الْكَذِبَ هذا حَلالٌ وَهذا حَرامٌ لِتَفْتَرُوا عَلَى اللهِ الْكَذِبَ (Do not say, asserting the lie with your tongues, ‘This is lawful and this is forbidden,’ so as to fabricate a lie against Allah [16:116]) etc. The particle "for" (لما) in His saying لِما تَصِفُ is infinitive (مصدرية), and "the lie" (الكذب) is the object of "asserting" (تصف). That is: do not say "this is lawful and this is forbidden" because of your tongues' assertion of a lie, for the purpose of fabricating a lie against Allah.

The fact that the address in the verses is to the believers—as supported by the context mentioned earlier—or to people in general, supports that the meaning of His saying وَلا تَقُولُوا لِما تَصِفُ أَلْسِنَتُكُمُ الْكَذِبَ هذا حَلالٌ وَهذا حَرامٌ is a prohibition against innovation (الابتداع) by introducing something lawful or unlawful into the rulings current in society and practiced among them without revelation being sent down regarding it. For that is the introduction of what is not of the religion into the religion, and a fabrication against Allah, even if the one who established it does not attribute it to Him, the Exalted.

This is because religion (الدين) in the parlance of the Quran is the "way of life" (سنة الحياة). He, the Glorified, has repeatedly said: يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَيَبْغُونَها عِوَجاً (who bar [others] from the way of Allah and seek to make it crooked [7:45]) or similar phrases. Thus, religion belongs to Allah, and whoever adds anything to it has attributed it to Him, the Exalted, as a fabrication against Him, even if he remains silent about the attribution or denies it with his tongue.

The majority of commentators mentioned that the verse means a prohibition of what the polytheists used to permit, such as carrion, blood, and what was offered to other than Allah, or what they used to prohibit, such as the bahirah and sa'ibah and others. However, the context—as mentioned—does not support this.

Then He, the Glorified, said in the position of explaining the reason for the prohibition: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى اللهِ الْكَذِبَ لا يُفْلِحُونَ (Indeed those who fabricate lies against Allah will not be felicitous [16:116]). He then explained their deprivation of success by His saying: مَتاعٌ قَلِيلٌ وَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ (A brief enjoyment, and there is a painful punishment for them [16:117]).

His saying, the Exalted: وَعَلَى الَّذِينَ هادُوا حَرَّمْنا ما قَصَصْنا عَلَيْكَ مِنْ قَبْلُ (To the Jews We forbade what We have already related to you [16:118]) etc. The meaning of His saying ما قَصَصْنا عَلَيْكَ مِنْ قَبْلُ (what We have already related to you) is—as has been said—what He, the Exalted, related to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in Surah al-An'am—which was revealed before Surah al-Nahl without doubt—in His saying: وَعَلَى الَّذِينَ هادُوا حَرَّمْنا كُلَّ ذِي ظُفُرٍ (To the Jews We forbade every animal with undivided hooves [6:146]) until the end of the verse.

The verse is in the position of repelling an objection (دفع الدخل) and contains a connection to the issue of abrogation mentioned earlier. It is as if someone were to say: "If the prohibited foods are restricted to the four mentioned—carrion, blood, the flesh of swine, and what is offered to other than Allah—and everything else is lawful, then what are these things that were prohibited for the Children of Israel before? Is this not an injustice toward them?"

So He answered it by saying: "We prohibited that for them, and We did not wrong them in its prohibition, but they used to wrong themselves, so We prohibited certain things for them." That is, it was lawful for them and permitted, but they wronged themselves and disobeyed their Lord, so We recompensed them by prohibiting it as a punishment. As He, the Glorified, said in another place: فَبِظُلْمٍ مِنَ الَّذِينَ هادُوا حَرَّمْنا عَلَيْهِمْ طَيِّباتٍ أُحِلَّتْ لَهُمْ (Due to the wrongdoing of the Jews, We forbade them certain good things that were permitted to them [4:160]). And if, after all that, they were to return to their Lord and repent for their sins, Allah would turn to them in mercy and lift the prohibition, permitting them what He had forbidden them; indeed, He is All-Forgiving, Most Merciful.

Thus, it has become clear that the verse is connected to what preceded it regarding the discussion of the lawful and the unlawful, and that it is like an answer to a hypothetical question. Furthermore, what follows it in His saying: ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ عَمِلُوا السُّوءَ (Then indeed your Lord, to those who commit evil [16:119]) is connected to it and completes its content.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ عَمِلُوا السُّوءَ بِجَهالَةٍ ثُمَّ تابُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ وَأَصْلَحُوا إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنْ بَعْدِها لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ (Then indeed your Lord, to those who commit evil out of ignorance and then repent after that and reform—indeed after that your Lord is all-forgiving, all-merciful [16:119]). Ignorance (الجهالة) and lack of knowledge (الجهل) are one; it originally refers to the opposite of knowledge. However, "ignorance" (الجهالة) is often used to mean the lack of complete disclosure of reality, even if the person is not devoid of some knowledge that makes them accountable (التكليف). This is like the state of one who commits prohibitions while knowing they are prohibited, but whose carnal desires overcome him and drive him to disobedience, not allowing him to reflect on the reality of this opposition and sin. Thus, he has knowledge of what he committed—and for that reason, he is held accountable and punished for what he did—yet he is simultaneously ignorant of the reality of the matter; had he possessed full insight, he would not have committed it.

The meaning of "ignorance" (الجهالة) in the verse is this sense. For if the first meaning were intended—that the evil deed was unknown in terms of its ruling or its subject matter—the act would not be a sin requiring repentance (التوبة), and thus forgiveness and mercy.

The verse—as previously indicated—is connected to what preceded it, completing its content. The meaning of the two verses is: We did not wrong the Children of Israel in prohibiting the good things We forbade them; rather, they were the ones who wronged themselves by committing sins and persisting in them, which led to the prohibition of good things for them. After all that, the door of forgiveness and mercy is open. Indeed, your Lord, for those who committed an evil deed (السوء)—which is the sin (السيئة)—out of ignorance, then repented after that and reformed (أصلحوا) so that the repentance becomes clear and established, indeed your Lord after that—meaning after the repentance—is All-Forgiving, Most Merciful.
