In conditioning repentance first with reform (الإصلاح), then returning the pronoun at the end to repentance alone in His saying إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنْ بَعْدِها لَغَفُورٌ (indeed after that your Lord is all-forgiving), there is an indication that the encompassment of forgiveness and mercy is a consequence of repentance. As for reform, it is only to clarify the repentance and demonstrate that it is a true repentance and a serious return, not merely a form devoid of meaning.

His saying at the end of the verse: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنْ بَعْدِها (indeed after that your Lord) is a summary of the detail in His saying at its beginning: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ (Then indeed your Lord, to those who). Its benefit is to protect the listener's understanding from confusion and deviation, and to highlight the concern for the "after-ness" of forgiveness and mercy in relation to repentance. This is similar to what passed in His saying: ثُمَّ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لِلَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما فُتِنُوا ثُمَّ جاهَدُوا وَصَبَرُوا إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنْ بَعْدِها لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ (Then indeed your Lord, to those who migrated after they were persecuted, then waged jihad and were patient—indeed after that your Lord is all-forgiving, all-merciful [16:110]).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ إِبْراهِيمَ كانَ أُمَّةً قانِتاً لِلَّهِ حَنِيفاً وَلَمْ يَكُ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ (Indeed Abraham was a nation, obedient to Allah, upright, and he was not one of the polytheists [16:120]). This verse and those following it are connected to the previous restriction of prohibited foods to four and the permissibility of what lies beyond them. This verse, along with the three following it, serves as a detailed exposition (التفصيل) of what preceded it. It is as if it were said: "This is the state of the creed (ملة) of Moses, in which We prohibited for the Children of Israel some of the good things that were permitted to them. As for this creed that We have revealed to you, it is the creed that Abraham realized; so Allah chose him and guided him to a straight path, and rectified through it his worldly life and his Hereafter. It is a moderate creed following the primordial nature (الفطرة), permitting the good things and prohibiting the foul things (الخبائث). Acting upon it brings about the same good that it brought for Abraham (عليه‌السلام)."

Regarding His saying: إِنَّ إِبْراهِيمَ كانَ أُمَّةً (Indeed Abraham was a nation), it is said in al-Mufradat: "His saying: إِنَّ إِبْراهِيمَ كانَ أُمَّةً قانِتاً لِلَّهِ means he stood in the place of a whole community in the worship of Allah, similar to their saying: 'So-and-so is a tribe in himself.'" [End quote]. This is close to what was narrated from Ibn Abbas. It has also been said that it means an "Imam" (الإمام) who is followed. It has also been said that he was a "nation" restricted to one person for a period of time, as there was no monotheist on earth who declared the oneness of Allah other than him.

His saying: قانِتاً لِلَّهِ حَنِيفاً وَلَمْ يَكُ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ (obedient to Allah, upright, and he was not one of the polytheists). Obedience (القنوت) is submission and worship, or the persistence therein. Uprightness (الحنف) is the inclination from the two extremes toward the very center, which is moderation (الاعتدال).

His saying, the Exalted: شاكِراً لِأَنْعُمِهِ اجْتَباهُ وَهَداهُ إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ (grateful for His blessings. He chose him and guided him to a straight path [16:121]). Choosing (الاجتباء) is from jibayah, which is "collecting." Allah’s choosing of a human being is His purifying him for Himself and gathering him from dispersal among various paths. Following His saying شاكِراً لِأَنْعُمِهِ (grateful for His blessings) with His saying اجْتَباهُ (He chose him) etc., without a conjunction, suggests causality (العلية). This supports what was mentioned in Surah al-A'raf in the commentary on His saying: وَلا تَجِدُ أَكْثَرَهُمْ شاكِرِينَ (and You will not find most of them to be grateful [7:17])—that the reality of gratitude (الشكر) is sincerity in servitude (الإخلاص في العبودية).

His saying, the Exalted: وَآتَيْناهُ فِي الدُّنْيا حَسَنَةً وَإِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ (We gave him good in this world, and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the righteous [16:122]). The "good" (الحسنة) is a good life; for he (عليه‌السلام) possessed great wealth and immense virtue.

We have expanded the discussion on the meaning of "choosing" (الاجتباء) in the commentary of Surah Yusuf at verse 6; on the meaning of "guidance" (الهدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — and the "straight path" (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — in the commentary of al-Fatihah at verse 6; and on the meaning of His saying وَإِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ (and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the righteous) in Surah al-Baqarah, verse 130. So refer to those.

In Allah’s description of Abraham (عليه‌السلام) with these attributes, there is an indication that they are among the gifts of this upright religion (الدين الحنيف). If a person adheres to it, it will lead him to what it led Abraham (عليه‌السلام).

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ أَنِ اتَّبِعْ مِلَّةَ إِبْراهِيمَ حَنِيفاً وَما كانَ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ (Then We revealed to you: ‘Follow the creed of Abraham, upright, and he was not one of the polytheists’ [16:123]). The repetition of his being characterized by uprightness and the negation of polytheism is for increased emphasis and concern.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما جُعِلَ السَّبْتُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ (The Sabbath was only prescribed for those who differed about it [16:124]) until the end of the verse. It is said in al-Mufradat: "The root of sabt (السبت) is 'cutting' (القطع). From it is sabt al-sayr (cutting the journey), sabt al-sha'r (shaving the hair), and sabt al-anf (cutting off the nose). It is said that the Sabbath day was so named because Allah began the creation of the heavens and the earth on Sunday, creating them in six days as He mentioned, then He 'cut' (ceased) His work on Saturday, so it was named thus."

"So-and-so sabata" means he entered into the Sabbath. His saying: يَوْمَ سَبْتِهِمْ شُرَّعاً (on the day of their Sabbath, appearing [7:163]) is said to mean the day of their ceasing work. وَيَوْمَ لا يَسْبِتُونَ (and on the day they did not keep the Sabbath [7:163]) is said to mean they did not cease work, or the day they were not in the Sabbath; both refer to the same state. His saying: إِنَّما جُعِلَ السَّبْتُ (The Sabbath was only prescribed) means the cessation of work therein. وَجَعَلْنا نَوْمَكُمْ سُباتاً (and We made your sleep a rest [78:9]) means a cessation of work, which refers to what He said in describing the night: لِتَسْكُنُوا فِيهِ (that you may rest in it [10:67]). [End quote].

Thus, the meaning of "the Sabbath" (السبت) according to what he mentioned is the day itself, but the meaning of its "prescribing" (جعل) is the prescribing of the cessation of work therein and its legislation. It is also possible that it refers to the infinitive meaning (المعنى المصدري) rather than the day in which that was prescribed, as is apparent from His saying: تأْتِيهِمْ حِيتانُهُمْ يَوْمَ سَبْتِهِمْ شُرَّعاً وَيَوْمَ لا يَسْبِتُونَ لا تَأْتِيهِمْ (their fish would come to them on the day of their Sabbath, appearing on the surface, but on the day they did not keep the Sabbath they would not come to them [7:163]).

In any case, the natural flow of speech would have been to say: "The Sabbath was only prescribed for (لـ) those..." to indicate a type of specialization or possession, and that Allah legislated for them in every week a day to cease work and devote themselves to the worship of their Lord, which is the Sabbath, just as He made for the Muslims in every week a day to gather for worship and prayer, which is Friday.

However, His saying: إِنَّما جُعِلَ السَّبْتُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ (The Sabbath was only prescribed upon (على) those who differed about it) by making the verb "prescribed" (جعل) transitive with "upon" (على) rather than "for" (اللام) is like their saying: "I have a debt upon you" (لي عليك دين) and "this is against you, not for you." Thus, it conveys the meaning of obligation (التكليف), severity (التشديد), and trial (الابتلاء). That is, it was only prescribed for the purpose of severity upon them and to try and test them. This prescription was against them, not for them, as their affair regarding it led to the cursing of a group of them and the transformation (المسخ) of others, as alluded to in Surah al-Baqarah (verse 65) and Surah al-Nisa (verse 47).

Based on this, it is more appropriate that the meaning of His saying اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ (differed about it) refers to their differing regarding the Sabbath after the legislation. For they split regarding it into factions: those who accepted it, those who rejected it, and those who used ruses to work during it, as alluded to in their stories in Surahs al-Baqarah, al-Nisa, and al-A'raf. It does not refer to their differing about it before the legislation—such as it being offered to them to keep the Sabbath one day a week for worship, then that day being Friday, and they differing about it such that the Sabbath was then imposed upon them—as occurs in some narrations.

The meaning is: the Sabbath day—or the cessation of work for worship one day every week—was only prescribed as a severity, a trial, a temptation, and a burden upon the Jews who differed about it after its legislation, between those who accepted it, those who rejected it, and those who used ruses to work while pretending to accept it. And indeed, your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that wherein they used to differ.

Based on this construction, the weight of this verse is the same as His previous saying: وَعَلَى الَّذِينَ هادُوا حَرَّمْنا (To the Jews We forbade...) etc., in that it is in the sense of an answer to a hypothetical question, connected to the earlier discussion on abrogation. The implicit meaning is: "As for the prescribing of the Sabbath for the Jews, it was only prescribed not for them but against them, so that Allah might try them, test them, and be severe with them," as similar instances were repeated regarding them because they were rebellious, transgressive, and arrogant. In short, the verse addresses the objection regarding the legislation of certain non-primordial rulings for the Jews and their abrogation in this Sharia.

The reason it was not joined to His earlier saying وَعَلَى الَّذِينَ هادُوا حَرَّمْنا etc., is that the issue of the Sabbath is of a different nature than the issue of permitting good things and the exception of prohibited foods. You have seen that the discourse in its continuity from His saying وَعَلَى الَّذِينَ هادُوا to His saying وَما كانَ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ consists of seven complete verses, and then this verse was joined to them as the eighth, appended to them.

From here, the answer becomes clear to the objection that the placement of the Sabbath's prescription between the account of the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) command to follow the creed of Abraham (عليه‌السلام) and the command to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to call toward it—in other words, the occurrence of His saying إِنَّما جُعِلَ السَّبْتُ etc., between His saying ثُمَّ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ etc., and His saying ادْعُ إِلى سَبِيلِ رَبِّكَ etc.—is like separating a tree from its bark.

The summary of the answer is that His saying ثُمَّ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ أَنِ اتَّبِعْ مِلَّةَ إِبْراهِيمَ is part of the previous context, and His saying إِنَّما جُعِلَ السَّبْتُ is connected to what preceded it as you have seen. As for His saying ادْعُ إِلى سَبِيلِ رَبِّكَ, it is a new beginning (استئناف) and a command to call to the way of Allah using various modes of discourse, not specifically to the creed of Abraham, such that it would be connected to the previous verse in a specific way—even if the way of Allah is the creed of Abraham itself. For the wording has one ruling, and the meaning, in terms of the ultimate outcome, has another ruling. So understand this.

The scholars have deep differences regarding the interpretation of "differing" (الاختلاف). Some said the meaning is: "The Sabbath was only prescribed against those who differed with their prophet regarding it, when he commanded them to venerate Friday, but they turned away from it and took the Sabbath, so Allah prescribed it against them as a severity." Thus, the differing is prior to the prescription, not subsequent to it. Perhaps the particle "in" (في) is for causality, as the differing in this case did not occur in the Sabbath but because of the Sabbath.

It has also been said that "differing" (الاختلاف) means "opposition" (المخالفة), for they opposed their prophet regarding the Sabbath and did not differ within it.

It has also been said that they were commanded to take Friday without it being specified, and that was left to their independent reasoning (اجتهادهم). Their rabbis then differed in specifying it and Allah did not guide them to it, so they fell into the Sabbath.

It has also been said that the meaning is that they differed among themselves regarding the status of the Sabbath: one group preferred it over Friday, and another group did the opposite and preferred Friday over it. These are among the various things said, and the basis for them is what was mentioned in some narrations of the story.

You are well aware that none of these views aligns perfectly with the wording of the verse, so the conclusion is what we have presented.

His saying, the Exalted: ادْعُ إِلى سَبِيلِ رَبِّكَ بِالْحِكْمَةِ وَالْمَوْعِظَةِ الْحَسَنَةِ وَجادِلْهُمْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ (Invite to the way of your Lord with wisdom and good advice and dispute with them in a manner that is best [16:125]) until the end of the verse. There is no doubt that it is understood from the verse that these three—wisdom (الحكمة), good advice (الموعظة), and disputation (المجادلة)—are among the methods of speaking and negotiation. He was commanded to call using one of these matters, so they are among the modes and methods of the call (الدعوة), even if disputation is not considered a "call" in its most specific sense.

Wisdom (الحكمة) has been interpreted—as in al-Mufradat—as reaching the truth through knowledge and reason. Advice (الموعظة)—as narrated from al-Khalil—is reminding one of good in a way that softens the heart. Disputation (الجدال)—as in al-Mufradat—is negotiation by way of contention and seeking dominance.

Reflection on these meanings suggests that the intended meaning of wisdom (الحكمة)—and Allah knows best—is the proof (الحجة) that produces the truth in which there is no doubt, weakness, or ambiguity. Advice (الموعظة) is the exposition by which the soul becomes gentle and the heart softens, due to what it contains of rectifying the listener's state through warnings, lessons, beautiful praise, commendable effects, and the like.

Disputation (الجدال) is the proof used to turn the opponent away from what he persists in and contends about, without necessarily intending the manifestation of the truth, but rather by holding him accountable through what he and the people accept, or what he alone accepts in his speech or proof.

Thus, what He, the Exalted, mentioned of wisdom, advice, and disputation corresponds in order to what is termed in the art of logic (فن الميزان) as demonstration (البرهان), rhetoric (الخطابة), and dialectic (الجدل).

However, He, the Glorified, qualified advice as "good" (الحسنة) and disputation as "that which is best" (بالتي هي أحسن). This indicates that some advice is not good, and some disputation is "best," some is "good," and some is neither "best" nor "good." Allah, the Exalted, commands the "good" kind of advice and the "best" kind of disputation.

Perhaps the reasoning at the end of the verse in His saying: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ ضَلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِالْمُهْتَدِينَ (Indeed your Lord knows best those who stray from His way, and He knows best those who are guided [16:125]) clarifies the reason for the qualification. Its meaning is that He, the Glorified, knows best the state of the people of straying from His true religion, and He knows best the state of the guided within it. He knows that what benefits in this path is wisdom, good advice, and the best disputation, and nothing else.

Sound reasoning supports this, for His way, the Exalted, is true belief and true action. It is well known that calling to it through advice, for example, by one who does not follow the advice he gives, is a call through action to the opposite of what the speech calls to. Similarly, calling to it through disputation using false premises that the opponent accepts to manifest the truth is an attempt to revive a right by reviving a falsehood—or if you will, reviving a right by killing a right—unless the disputation is by way of contradiction (المناقضة).

From here, it becomes clear that the "goodness" of advice is in terms of the goodness of its effect on the truth intended by it: that the advisor himself follows what he advises, and employs such good character as increases its acceptance in the listener's heart, so the heart softens, the skin shivers, the ear retains it, and the eye humbles itself.

The disputant should avoid what increases the opponent's provocation toward rejection and stubbornness, or drives him to obstinacy and persistence. He should avoid using false premises even if the opponent accepts them—except in contradiction—and avoid poor expression, belittling the opponent and what he holds sacred of beliefs, and avoid cursing, insulting, and any other form of ignorance. For in that is the revival of truth by the revival of falsehood—that is, the killing of truth, as you have seen.

Disputation is in greater need of perfect goodness than advice; for this reason, He, the Glorified, permitted the "good" kind of advice but did not permit any disputation except "that which is best."

Furthermore, in His saying: بِالحِكْمَةِ وَالْمَوْعِظَةِ الْحَسَنَةِ وَجادِلْهُمْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ, there is an ordering in terms of individuals: wisdom is permitted in all its forms; advice is divided into good and not good, and the permitted kind is the good advice; and disputation is divided into good and not good, then the good is divided into that which is best and otherwise, and the permitted kind is that which is best. The verse is silent about the distribution of these methods according to those being called; thus, the criterion for their use in terms of the occasion is the goodness of the effect and the attainment of the objective, which is the manifestation of the truth.

It is permissible to use all three methods in one instance, and in another, two methods or one, according to what the situation demands and what suits the occasion.

From this, it becomes clear that the view of some—that the apparent meaning of the verse is that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) should combine the three methods in his call—is misplaced. For there is no evidence for the necessity of combining them for every individual called; as for all those called collectively, it is achieved.

Similarly, what some have mentioned—that the three methods mentioned in the verse are ordered according to the levels of people's understanding in their readiness to accept the truth—is also noted. They say: some people are the "elite" (الخواص), who are the possessors of luminous souls with strong readiness to perceive intellectual realities, intense attraction to high principles, and great familiarity with knowledge and certainty; these are called with wisdom, which is demonstration (البرهان).

Others are the "commonality" (العوام), who are the possessors of murky souls and weak readiness, along with intense familiarity with sensory things and strong attachment to forms and customs, being incapable of receiving demonstrations without being antagonistic to the truth; these are called with good advice.

Others are the people of antagonism and stubbornness, who dispute with falsehood to refute the truth and act obstinately to extinguish the light of Allah with their mouths. False views have become entrenched in their souls, and the imitation of their ancestors in their superstitious creeds has overcome them; advice and lessons do not benefit them, nor does the driver of demonstrations guide them. These are the ones he was commanded to dispute with in the best manner.

This view is not devoid of precision, but it does not necessitate the restriction of each method to its corresponding level of understanding. For the elite may benefit from advice and disputation, and the commonality—who are the people of customs and forms—may benefit from the best disputation. There is no indication in the wording of the verse for the mentioned restriction.

Likewise, what some have mentioned—that the best disputation is not part of the "call" (الدعوة) at all, but rather its purpose is something else, namely silencing and defeating the opponent (الإلزام والإفحام)—is also noted. He said: "For this reason, disputation was not joined in the verse to what preceded it; rather, the context was changed and it was said: وَجادِلْهُمْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ." This view overlooks the reality of the dialectical syllogism (القياس الجدلي). For while silencing the opponent may be an end for the dialectical syllogism, it is not a permanent end. Often, a syllogism is composed of accepted or conceded premises—especially in practical matters and non-certain sciences like jurisprudence, principles, ethics, and literary arts—without intending silencing or defeat.

Furthermore, in silencing and defeating there is a "call," just as in advice there is a "call," even if their forms differ according to the methods. Yes, the change in context is due to the meaning of contention and seeking dominance inherent in disputation.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ عاقَبْتُمْ فَعاقِبُوا بِمِثْلِ ما عُوقِبْتُمْ بِهِ وَلَئِنْ صَبَرْتُمْ لَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لِلصَّابِرِينَ (If you decide to punish, then punish with the like of what you have been punished with. But if you are patient, that is surely better for the patient [16:126]). It is said in al-Mufradat: "Punishment (العقوبة), penalty (العقاب), and punishing (المعاقبة) are specific to torment." [End quote].

The root of its meaning is the "heel" (العقب), which is the back of the foot. The "sequel" of a thing (عقيب الشيء) and the "consequence" of an affair (عاقبة الأمر) is what follows it from behind or its end. "Following up" (التعقيب) is bringing something after another. Your "punishing" another (معاقبتك غيرك) is to bring what displeases him after his bringing what displeased you; thus, it applies to recompense (المجازاة) and requital (المكافأة) with torment.

His saying: وَإِنْ عاقَبْتُمْ فَعاقِبُوا بِمِثْلِ ما عُوقِبْتُمْ بِهِ (If you decide to punish, then punish with the like of what you have been punished with). The address here is to the Muslims—as indicated by the context—and its necessity is that "punishing" (المعاقبة) refers to the recompense of the polytheists and disbelievers, and His saying عُوقِبْتُمْ بِهِ (what you have been punished with) refers to the disbelievers' punishment of them and their requital of them for believing in Allah and rejecting their deities.

The meaning is: "If you intend to recompense the disbelievers and torment them, then recompense them for what they did to you with the like of how they tormented you as a requital for your faith and your jihad in the way of Allah."

His saying: وَلَئِنْ صَبَرْتُمْ لَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لِلصَّابِرِينَ (But if you are patient, that is surely better for the patient). That is: if you are patient with the bitterness of what you were punished with, and you do not punish or requite, it is better for you insofar as you are patient. This is because it involves preferring Allah’s pleasure and His reward for the hardship and calamity that befell you over your own self-satisfaction through the healing of revenge. Thus, the act becomes sincere for His noble countenance. Furthermore, in pardoning and forgiveness, there is the exercise of chivalry (الفتوة), which has its beautiful effects.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاصْبِرْ وَما صَبْرُكَ إِلَّا بِاللهِ (Be patient, and your patience is only by [the help of] Allah [16:127]) until the end of the verse. This is a command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to be patient, and a glad tiding to him that Allah has strengthened him to be patient with the bitterness of what he encounters in His way. For He, the Exalted, mentions that his patience is only by a power and strength from his Lord, then He commands him to be patient. The command necessitates the ability of the one commanded to perform the commanded act. Thus, in His saying وَما صَبْرُكَ إِلَّا بِاللهِ, there is an indication that Allah has strengthened you for what He commanded you.

His saying: وَلا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ (and do not grieve for them [16:127]), meaning for the disbelievers because of their disbelief. The interpretation of this meaning has already passed previously in this surah and others.

His saying: وَلا تَكُ فِي ضَيْقٍ مِمَّا يَمْكُرُونَ (and do not be upset by their schemes [16:127]). It appears that the intended meaning is a prohibition against being distressed by their plotting in the present or continuously, rather than merely in the future.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ مُحْسِنُونَ (Indeed Allah is with those who are God-conscious and those who are virtuous [16:128]). That is, both godwariness (التقوى) and virtue (الإحسان) are each an independent cause for the gift of divine support, the nullification of the enemies' plotting, and the repelling of their schemes. Thus, the verse is a reasoning for His saying وَلا تَكُ فِي ضَيْقٍ مِمَّا يَمْكُرُونَ and a promise of victory.

These three verses are more similar in content to the Medinan verses than the Meccan ones. Narrations from both schools have been reported stating that they were revealed upon the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) return from Uhud, as will come in the discussion of narrations, even if it is possible to justify their connection to what preceded them in some way, as some have attempted.

What must be noted is that the verse preceding these three is more comprehensive of the surah's purpose than these three. Furthermore, the verses of the surah—disregarding His saying وَالَّذِينَ هاجَرُوا (verse 41), His saying مَنْ كَفَرَ بِاللهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ إِيمانِهِ (verse 106) until the end of several verses, and His saying وَإِنْ عاقَبْتُمْ until the end of the surah—possess a single, continuous context.

( A Discussion of Narrations )

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً (And Allah draws a parable [16:112]), he said: "He (peace be upon him) said: 'It was revealed concerning a people who had a river called al-Tharthar. Their lands were fertile and full of goodness. They used to clean themselves [after defecation] with dough, saying: "It is softer for us." So they were ungrateful for Allah’s favors and made light of them. Consequently, Allah withheld al-Tharthar from them, and they suffered drought until Allah made them need to eat that which they used to clean themselves with, to the point that they would divide it among themselves.'"

I say: It was also narrated in al-Kafi from him, with his chain of transmission from Amr ibn Shimr from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) in detail; and by al-Ayyashi from Hafs and Zayd al-Shahham from him (peace be upon him).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Anas ibn Malik, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'There is no servant for whom a nation (أمة) bears witness except that Allah accepts their testimony; and a "nation" is a man and above. Indeed, Allah says: إِنَّ إِبْراهِيمَ كانَ أُمَّةً قانِتاً لِلَّهِ حَنِيفاً وَلَمْ يَكُ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ (Indeed Abraham was a nation, obedient to Allah, upright, and he was not one of the polytheists).'"

I say: What pertains to this hadith has already passed in the commentary on the verses of testimony (آيات الشهادة).

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Sama‘ah ibn Mihran, who said: "I heard Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) saying: 'The world existed and there was none in it except one who worshipped Allah. Had there been another with him, He would have joined him to him, as He says: إِنَّ إِبْراهِيمَ كانَ أُمَّةً قانِتاً لِلَّهِ حَنِيفاً ـ وَلَمْ يَكُ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ. So he remained patient with that for as long as Allah willed. Then Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, comforted him with Ishmael and Isaac, so they became three.'"

I say: It was also narrated in al-Kafi with his chain of transmission from Sama‘ah from the Righteous Servant (peace be upon him).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Shafi‘i in al-Umm, al-Bukhari, and Muslim narrated from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'We are the last [to come] but the foremost on the Day of Resurrection, despite the fact that they were given the Book before us and we were given it after them. Then, this is their day which was made obligatory upon them—Friday—but they differed about it. So Allah guided us to it, and the people follow us in it: the Jews tomorrow and the Christians the day after tomorrow.'"

I say: Something similar was narrated by Ahmad and Muslim from Abu Hurayrah and Hudhayfah from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), but the narration was not mentioned in the commentary of the verse.

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Layla al-Ash‘ari that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Hold fast to the obedience of your leaders and do not oppose them; for their obedience is the obedience of Allah and their disobedience is the disobedience of Allah. Indeed, Allah only sent me to invite to His way with wisdom and good advice. So whoever opposes me in that is among the perished, and the protection (ذمة) of Allah and the protection of His Messenger is withdrawn from him. And whoever is in charge of any of your affairs and acts otherwise, upon him is the curse of Allah, the angels, and all of mankind."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَجادِلْهُمْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ (and dispute with them in a manner that is best [16:125]), he said: "He (peace be upon him) said: 'With the Quran.'"

In al-Kafi, from him with his chain of transmission from Abu Amr al-Zubayri from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: ادْعُ إِلى سَبِيلِ رَبِّكَ بِالْحِكْمَةِ وَالْمَوْعِظَةِ الْحَسَنَةِ ـ وَجادِلْهُمْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ, he said: "With the Quran."

I say: Its apparent meaning is that it is an interpretation of بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ (in a manner that is best), and its summary is disputation according to the way of the Quran, which contains the discipline (أدب) of Allah.

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from al-Hasan ibn Hamzah, who said: "I heard Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) saying: 'When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saw what was done to Hamzah ibn Abd al-Muttalib, he said: "O Allah, to You belongs all praise, and to You is the complaint, and You are the one whose help is sought against what I see." Then he said: "If I am victorious, I shall surely mutilate, and I shall surely mutilate, and I shall surely mutilate." He said: So Allah revealed: وَإِنْ عاقَبْتُمْ فَعاقِبُوا بِمِثْلِ ما عُوقِبْتُمْ بِهِ ـ وَلَئِنْ صَبَرْتُمْ لَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لِلصَّابِرِينَ. Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "I shall be patient, I shall be patient."'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Tabarani, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala'il narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said on the day Hamzah was killed and mutilated: 'If I am victorious over the Quraysh, I shall surely mutilate seventy men from among them.' So Allah revealed: وَإِنْ عاقَبْتُمْ (verse 126). Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Rather, we shall be patient, O Lord.' So he was patient and forbade mutilation (المثلة)."

I say: Something similar in meaning was also narrated from Ubayy ibn Ka‘b, Abu Hurayrah, and others from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

— Completed, and praise be to Allah —

( Addendum )

A comment on page 320, line 20, regarding His saying: "So the beginning of the verse..." This is in view of the text of the verse, but the requirement of the context of the verses preceding it is that "religion" (الدين) is in the sense of "recompense" (الجزاء), and this is supported by His saying: "He will pay them in full" (يوفتيهم).
